confirmed.title: Same-window event extends/reopens the span with no idle check — the chatty-while-away inflation the consume docstring claims to prevent
confirmed.detail: Verified. `on_focus` (269-296) never consults idle: same-window (282-288) calls `set_span_end(ev.timestamp)` + `open.end = ev.timestamp` unconditionally; the switch/reopen path (291-294) calls `open_span` for any Track event. The idle gate is ONLY in `on_tick` (316), which `consume` runs only when `now >= next_tick` (370), ~every TICK_SECS=20s. Between ticks on_focus extends freely. D32 (decisions.md:151) and the consume docstring (343-345) both claim idle is checked 'on every loop wake' to stop a chatty live-title tab ballooning the span — but the check is per-tick, not per-wake, and the away branch (316-320) sets current=None WITHOUT rolling back the open.end on_focus already wrote. So a changing title (build counter/clock/badge — the AXTitleChanged churn D39 Q1 names) while away: (1) inflates the persisted end up to ~TICK_SECS past true idle, never rolled back; (2) after the tick closes the span, each later title event re-enters on_focus with current==None, skips 282, takes nothing at 291, and opens a fresh phantom span at 294 while still idle — the next tick closes but doesn't roll it back, so a title stream spawns a chain of phantom spans across the away window. The macOS last_title dedupe (macos/mod.rs:191) drops only IDENTICAL titles, so changing titles still fire. The invariant does not hold on the on_focus paths.
confirmed.fix: Gate on_focus on idle like on_tick: read current_idle_secs() on event arrival in consume and pass into on_focus; if idle >= gate_secs_for(focus.app), close any open span at its existing end, set current=None, keep last_focus, do NOT extend or open. Keep on_tick the single judge but have on_focus honor the same deadline. Tighten the docstring. Add a test: a stream of same-app distinct-title events while idle>=gate must not extend or reopen.
confirmed.evidence: on_focus (269-296) has no idle ref; on_tick (316) is the only idle check, run only when now>=next_tick (consume:370). Away branch (316-320) drops to current=None without rolling back open.end written at 284. D32 (decisions.md:151) + docstring (343-345) claim 'checked on every loop wake'. macos last_title dedupe (macos/mod.rs:191) drops only identical titles.

confirmed.title: on_tick resume gate uses last_focus app, widening the patient-gate window for phantom reopens
confirmed.detail: Verified the mechanism: when current is None the gate is chosen from last_focus (310-315 or_else), so a patient app keeps the 600s tolerance on the resume path. This is consistent with D39 decision A (reopen the same window after idle-close since macOS fires no event on return) and the bounded-patient-gate intent (a real walk-away still closes at the cap). It is only a real over-count when combined with r0's reopen-while-idle gap, and even then bounded by the gate. On its own it is intended behaviour, not a bug — author confidence already low. Downgrading to info; the substantive fix belongs to r0.
confirmed.fix: No standalone change — this is the D39 bounded-patient-gate + decision-A resume tolerance working as designed. If r0's idle-on-on_focus fix lands, the phantom-reopen window it widens disappears with it.
confirmed.evidence: on_tick gate selection (310-315) falls back to last_focus app. D39 (decisions.md:156) documents per-app gate + decision A as intended; cap bounds worst-case to ~10 min. Author confidence 'low'.

confirmed.title: FocusEvent.bundle_id is a dead carrier field — set by macOS source, never read
confirmed.detail: Confirmed write-only. FocusEvent.bundle_id (35) is set at macos/mod.rs:217 but a grep for reads of the FocusEvent field returns nothing — db.rs, enrich.rs, and the consumer state machine never read ev.bundle_id. The macOS impl uses its own self.bundle_id (206, 214) for browser/terminal enrichment before building the event, so carrying it across the channel earns nothing — leftover plumbing. D39 confirms FocusEvent is capture-internal (no IPC/bindings impact), so removal is IPC-safe. Not autofixable: removal touches macos/mod.rs:217 and the test helper focus() at mod.rs:399-407 — a multi-file logic edit, not a mechanical fmt/import fix.
confirmed.fix: Delete the bundle_id field from FocusEvent (35) and the `bundle_id: Some(self.bundle_id.clone())` line in macos/mod.rs emit_with_title (217). Verify cargo build/clippy/test stay green.
confirmed.evidence: grep for FocusEvent-field reads of bundle_id returns only writer macos/mod.rs:217; macOS-internal self.bundle_id uses at 206,214,251. No read in mod.rs/db.rs/enrich.rs. D39 (decisions.md:156): FocusEvent is capture-internal.

confirmed.title: FocusEvent.pid is a dead carrier field — set by macOS source, never read by the consumer
confirmed.detail: Confirmed write-only, same shape as bundle_id. FocusEvent.pid (36) is set by polling.rs:58 and macos/mod.rs:218, and the test helper at mod.rs:403 sets pid:1. Nothing downstream reads ev.pid: resolve_focus, on_focus, on_tick, open_span, db.rs, enrich.rs never reference it. The macOS source uses its own self.pid (214,249,251) before building the event — the field is never consumed. Not autofixable: removal touches polling.rs:58, macos/mod.rs:218, and the test helper mod.rs:403.
confirmed.fix: Delete the pid field from FocusEvent (36) and its three assignments: polling.rs:58 (`pid: window.process_id as i32`), macos/mod.rs:218 (`pid: self.pid`), test helper mod.rs:403 (`pid: 1`). Verify gates stay green.
confirmed.evidence: grep for ev.pid/focus.pid across src-tauri/src returns no FocusEvent-field reads; writers are polling.rs:58, macos/mod.rs:218, test helper mod.rs:403. macOS uses self.pid (214,249,251), not the field.

confirmed.title: GATE_SECS doc comment over-narrates the reading-allowance rationale
confirmed.detail: Confirmed at lines 104-106. The doc spans three lines; the second/third re-explain product rationale ('Also the everyday reading allowance — scroll/trackpad count as input …') that appears in D32/D39 in decisions.md and is not an invariant a code reader needs. The first sentence fully defines the constant. Minor pure-comment trim.
confirmed.fix: Trim to the first sentence: `/// Default input-idle gate: at/over this many seconds with no input the open span is closed and time stops accruing.`
confirmed.evidence: Lines 104-106; reading-allowance rationale duplicates D32 (decisions.md:151) and D39 (decisions.md:156).

confirmed.title: PATIENT_GATE_SECS comment is decision-archaeology
confirmed.detail: Confirmed at lines 108-112. The comment narrates the debate outcome ('Bounded, never disabled … honest-mirror trade-off … set by the Codex+Opus debate'), all of which lives in D39. The bounded-cap behaviour is already visible from `idle_secs >= gate` in on_tick (316). One line with the D39 cross-ref suffices. Minor trim.
confirmed.fix: Reduce to one line: `/// A longer idle gate for surfaces where attentively watching with little input is the norm (agent/build runs). Still bounded — a real walk-away closes the span. Dogfood-tunable (D39).`
confirmed.evidence: Lines 108-112 restate D39 (decisions.md:156) verbatim ('never disabled', 'honest-mirror trade-off', 'Codex+Opus debate').

confirmed.title: PATIENT_APPS comment re-narrates the categorization-decoupling decision
confirmed.detail: Confirmed at lines 114-117. The comment explains the decoupling-from-categorization rationale ('… deliberately decoupled from categorization — a browser doing research is not a patient surface'), which is D39 material, not needed to read the list. The PATIENT_APPS list values themselves are dogfood-tunable by design (D39) and must NOT be flagged — only the prose comment is the bloat. Minor trim.
confirmed.fix: Reduce to one line naming what the list is: `/// Process-name substrings (case-insensitive) that earn the patient gate: editor / terminal / agent surfaces where you watch work happen.`
confirmed.evidence: Lines 114-117 duplicate D39 (decisions.md:156, Q3 categorization). List values are deliberate per D39, left untouched.

confirmed.title: AWAY_APPS comment duplicates the D41 rationale already in resolve_focus
confirmed.detail: Confirmed. The 5-line comment on AWAY_APPS (179-183) explains the D41 'input-idle reads ~0 while locked' reasoning, and the identical reasoning is repeated in resolve_focus at lines 190-191 ('The idle gate is blind while locked … the away-app is the signal we trust (D41)'). Same decision narrated twice within ~12 lines. Keep one. Minor trim.
confirmed.fix: Keep a one-line constant comment (`/// Frontmost apps macOS swaps in when the screen locks / screensaver runs — treat focus on them as 'away' (D41).`) and delete the duplicate block in resolve_focus (190-191), or vice versa. Keep exactly one.
confirmed.evidence: Lines 179-183 and 190-191 both narrate the same D41 (decisions.md:165) locked-idle-reads-~0 rationale.

confirmed.title: Redundant inline comments restating obvious branch behaviour in on_focus
confirmed.detail: Confirmed at lines 281 and 290. The two inline comments restate exactly what the next lines do, and the function doc at 267-268 already states the coalesce-else-switch policy. Low-value trim. Caveat: if r0's fix adds an idle check to on_focus, the same-window comment will be rewritten anyway — fold this into r0's edit rather than a separate pass.
confirmed.fix: Drop both inline comments; the function-level doc at 267-268 already states the policy. Defer if r0's fix touches the same lines.
confirmed.evidence: Lines 281 and 290 restate the immediately-following code; function doc at 267-268 already states the policy.

confirmed.title: Same-window re-fire writes set_span_end even when the timestamp didn't advance
confirmed.detail: Confirmed real inconsistency. on_focus same-window (283-285) calls set_span_end + sets open.end unconditionally, while on_tick (322-326) guards its extend with `if now > open.end`. A non-advancing/duplicate same-window timestamp triggers a redundant DB write of an unchanged end under the shared Arc<Mutex> lock. Adding the guard mirrors on_tick. Overlaps r0 — fold into r0's fix. Low severity (redundant write, not a correctness bug alone).
confirmed.fix: Guard the write to mirror on_tick: `if ev.timestamp > open.end { db::set_span_end(conn, open.id, ev.timestamp)?; open.end = ev.timestamp; }`. Best folded into r0's fix on the same path.
confirmed.evidence: on_focus same-window (283-285) writes unconditionally; on_tick (323) guards with `if now > open.end`. Same extend semantics, inconsistent guarding.

confirmed.title: consume() startup log prints GATE_SECS but the gate is per-app, so the number is misleading
confirmed.detail: Confirmed at lines 347-350. The banner prints `(tick {}s, idle gate {}s)` with TICK_SECS, GATE_SECS — advertising a single idle gate, but since D39 the gate is per-app via gate_secs_for (120 vs 600). Printing only GATE_SECS is a half-truth from before the patient gate existed. Cosmetic log-only.
confirmed.fix: Print just the tick interval (`"[Capture] consumer up (tick {TICK_SECS}s)"`) or print both gates. Simplest is dropping the idle-gate from the banner.
confirmed.evidence: Lines 347-350 print only GATE_SECS; gate_secs_for (123-130) selects per-app between GATE_SECS and PATIENT_GATE_SECS (D39, decisions.md:156).

confirmed.title: on_tick doc + inline 'away' comment over-explain a simple deadline check
confirmed.detail: Confirmed. The on_tick doc (299-301) plus inline lines 317-318 narrate the resume mechanism that the code states directly (current=None leaving last_focus, plus the resume branch 327-330). Low-value trim. Caveat: the 'leave its end at the last tick' note is exactly the behaviour r0 shows is wrong for the chatty-while-away case — do not delete it in a way that erases the (now-questionable) claim before r0 is resolved; address it as part of r0.
confirmed.fix: Trim the doc to one line; the resume-branch comment at 327 plus current=None suffice. Reconcile with r0 first — the 'leave its end at the last tick' claim is what r0 disputes.
confirmed.evidence: Lines 299-301 (doc) and 317-318 (inline) describe the same resume mechanism the code at 319/327-330 shows. The 'leave its end' phrasing intersects r0.

confirmed.title: open_span swallows category-lookup errors with a print, inconsistent with the Result-propagating neighbours
confirmed.detail: Confirmed inconsistency at 238-244: db::find_category failure is caught, eprintln'd, turned into None, while the next db::insert_event (245) propagates with `?`. Two adjacent DB errors handled two ways in one function. The caller (consume) already log-and-continues (359-363, 374-377), so propagating here loses nothing. But this is a judgment call, not a defect — swallowing the lookup error so the span still records (uncategorized) is a defensible choice. Note the cited line 245 is the insert `?`; the swallow is at 238-243. Downgrading to info: real consistency nit, not a bug; not autofixable (changes error semantics — propagating would skip the span on lookup failure).
confirmed.fix: Optionally propagate: `let category_id = db::find_category(conn, &focus.app, &focus.title)?;` and drop the match+eprintln (238-244). Confirm tests pass — propagating skips the span on a lookup error where today it records uncategorized.
confirmed.evidence: open_span 238-244 (catch+eprintln→None) vs 245 (`?` propagate). Caller error logging at consume:359-363 and 374-377.

confirmed.title: Polling source repeats the 'not window.title / Screen-Recording' rationale already in the module doc
confirmed.detail: Confirmed. The inline comment at polling.rs:59-60 restates the module-level doc at lines 4-8, which already says active-win-pos-rs reads kCGWindowName, gated by Screen Recording (C1), titles come from AX. Same point twice in a 70-line file. Minor trim.
confirmed.fix: Keep the module-doc explanation; reduce the inline comment to `// app name only — no title (see module doc).` or delete it.
confirmed.evidence: polling.rs:4-8 (module doc) and 59-60 (inline) both state the kCGWindowName/Screen-Recording/C1 rationale.

confirmed.title: emit_with_title spawns osascript/pgrep synchronously on the main run loop — an unresponsive scripted app freezes the UI
confirmed.detail: Verified. emit_with_title (mod.rs:202-225) calls browser::inspect (line 206) and terminal::front_cwd (line 214) synchronously. browser::inspect → run_osa (browser.rs:70-81) does Command::new(OSASCRIPT)...output() with NO timeout; terminal::front_cwd → iterm2_cwd (terminal.rs:28-32) and child_pids (terminal.rs:56-60) likewise spawn /usr/bin/osascript and /usr/bin/pgrep with .output() and no timeout. emit_with_title is reached from (a) observer_callback (line 322, ctx.emit → emit_with_title), documented to run on the main run loop (module header lines 6-9, callback doc line 297), and (b) switch_to (line 144) and on_activation (line 122), both driven by the NSWorkspace activation block (line 86-88) on the main thread. capture/mod.rs confirms only consume() (line 346) runs on a separate thread doing DB+git; the osascript enrichment is NOT on that thread. So a hung/beachballing Chrome/iTerm AppleEvent round-trip blocks the main CFRunLoop Tauri pumps, stalling the UI. With AXFocusedUIElementChanged registered (line 42/47) the callback can fire rapidly. This is a genuine latent responsiveness bug, not a documented deliberate choice — D33 records the enrichment-is-inline decision but not the unbounded-subprocess-on-main-thread blocking risk.
confirmed.fix: Move the osascript/pgrep enrichment off the main thread: emit a FocusEvent carrying only the AX title (cheap, pure AX) and resolve url/cwd on the consume() worker thread, OR bound each subprocess with a timeout (e.g. spawn + wait_timeout) so a stuck browser/terminal cannot pin the run loop. Do not just add a timeout silently — keep the title path synchronous so titles are never lost.
confirmed.evidence: emit_with_title at mod.rs:202-225 calls browser::inspect (206) + terminal::front_cwd (214); both spawn Command::output() with no timeout (browser.rs:71-75, terminal.rs:28-32, 56-60). Callback (mod.rs:297-323) + activation block (86-88) run on the main run loop per module header (1-13). capture/mod.rs:346 consume() is the only off-main thread and does not run enrichment. Not covered by any D-number as deliberate.

confirmed.title: Title-only dedupe drops URL/cwd-only changes (same-title navigation goes unrecorded)
confirmed.detail: Verified. emit() (mod.rs:187-197) reads focused_window_title, compares against last_title (lines 190-192) and returns early if unchanged, before emit_with_title would enrich with browser url (line 211) and terminal cwd (line 214). So if the AX title is constant while the url/cwd changes (SPA navigation preserving document.title, or a `cd` whose title template omits the path) and AX delivers no AXTitleChanged/focus event, emit() never fires and the new url/cwd is not recorded. Impact is genuinely bounded: it only bites when AX itself delivers no title/focus event for the change, and span coalescing in consume() already handles redundant url/cwd. This is a real, narrow fidelity gap on the project-inference signal, correctly rated info.
confirmed.fix: If url/cwd fidelity matters for project inference, key the dedupe on the full emitted identity (title + url + cwd) rather than title alone — but note that would move the (expensive, main-thread) enrichment ahead of the dedupe, compounding r0, so prefer to document the limitation on last_title (line 181) as an accepted trade-off.
confirmed.evidence: emit() at mod.rs:187-196 dedupes on title only; emit_with_title (202-225) adds url (211) and cwd (214) that the dedupe never sees. last_title field doc at line 181.

confirmed.title: Redundant manual new() alongside #[derive(Default)] on a unit marker
confirmed.detail: Verified. MacosCapture is a zero-field unit struct with both #[derive(Default)] (line 52) and a hand-written impl with pub fn new() -> Self { Self } (lines 55-59). The only call site is macos::MacosCapture::new() in capture/mod.rs:91. Two constructors for a unit marker is pure ceremony.
confirmed.fix: Drop the manual impl MacosCapture { pub fn new() ... } block (lines 55-59) and change the sole call site (capture/mod.rs:91) to MacosCapture::default(); or drop #[derive(Default)] and keep new(). Keep exactly one. Note clippy under -D warnings may flag new_without_default if you keep new() without Default, so removing new() and using default() is the cleaner direction.
confirmed.evidence: #[derive(Default)] at mod.rs:52; manual new() at 55-59; single call site MacosCapture::new() at capture/mod.rs:91.

confirmed.title: Module doc re-narrates design decisions that belong in decisions.md
confirmed.detail: Verified the 13-line header (lines 1-13) does narrate the RcBlock mechanism, !Send leaks, Hard rule 3, and C1. However this is borderline: the header is dense but mostly describes what the module does and the key safety invariant (main-run-loop, leaked !Send keep-alives), which a reader of this unsafe FFI file genuinely needs. The project culture (CLAUDE.md, decisions.md) deliberately values documenting the why near tricky code. Real but low-value, hence info not warning.
confirmed.fix: Optionally trim the rationale prose to D-number references (D29/D33) while keeping the operational summary (what it captures + that all native !Send state is leaked for the process lifetime). Not required.
confirmed.evidence: Module header mod.rs:1-13 cross-references D29 and Hard rule 3; the same rationale lives in decisions.md D29/D33 (lines 99, 119).

confirmed.title: on_activation is a one-line passthrough wrapper that ignores its argument
confirmed.detail: Verified. on_activation (mod.rs:122-126) ignores _notif and just does `if let Some(app) = frontmost() { self.switch_to(app); }`. Its only caller is the activation RcBlock at lines 86-88. The method and unused parameter exist only to mirror the notification signature. Minor; harmless indirection.
confirmed.fix: Optionally inline the body into the RcBlock (lines 86-88): `move |_| { if let Some(app) = frontmost() { state.borrow_mut().switch_to(app); } }` and delete on_activation. Low priority.
confirmed.evidence: on_activation at mod.rs:122-126 takes _notif (unused) and calls switch_to; sole caller is the RcBlock at 86-88.

confirmed.title: Multiple UAF/lifecycle comments restate the same Drop-ordering invariant
confirmed.detail: Verified the invariant is explained in switch_to (lines 135-137), AppObserver::Drop (lines 164-166), and the callback Safety block (297-301). Line 169 `let _ = &self.observer; // released on field drop` is a genuine no-op that states the obvious. For unsafe FFI with real UAF risk, some repetition is defensible, but the no-op statement plus its comment is true cruft. Info.
confirmed.fix: Optionally cut the duplicate at lines 135-136 to a one-liner referencing AppObserver::Drop, and delete the no-op `let _ = &self.observer;` plus its comment (line 169) — fields drop on their own.
confirmed.evidence: Drop-ordering comments at mod.rs:135-137, 164-166; no-op `let _ = &self.observer;` at line 169.

confirmed.title: focused_window_title and copy_focused_window both re-copy AXFocusedWindow
confirmed.detail: Verified. focused_window_title (mod.rs:353-368) and copy_focused_window (371-376) both call copy_attr(app_el, ATTR_FOCUSED_WINDOW) then do an unsafe CFRetained::as_ptr(...).cast::<AXUIElement>() pointer dance — focused_window_title borrows the ref (line 356-358), copy_focused_window retains it (line 373-375). Two near-identical unsafe casts of the AXFocusedWindow value; getting one subtly wrong while the other stays correct is exactly the FFI footgun worth consolidating.
confirmed.fix: Have focused_window_title call copy_focused_window(app_el) to obtain the owned window element, then read ATTR_TITLE from it — one copy-and-cast path. Verify the existing on-device behavior is preserved (retain is sound; the borrow variant is the riskier one being eliminated). Run cargo test + clippy after.
confirmed.evidence: focused_window_title at mod.rs:353-368 (cast at 356-358); copy_focused_window at 371-376 (cast at 373-375); both start with copy_attr(app_el, ATTR_FOCUSED_WINDOW).

confirmed.title: Comment explaining why kAX constants are re-declared is archaeology
confirmed.detail: Verified. Line 36 `// AX attribute + notification names (the kAX* constants are NOT re-exported).` The parenthetical explains an objc2 binding-crate quirk. It is a minor note-to-self, but it actually answers an obvious 'why are these string literals instead of the SDK constants?' question a future maintainer would ask — so trimming is optional, not a smell worth flagging above info.
confirmed.fix: Optionally shorten to `// AX attribute + notification names.` Low value.
confirmed.evidence: Comment at mod.rs:36; constants declared as string literals lines 37-42 because the binding crate does not re-export kAX*.

confirmed.title: Obvious field/struct comments restate the code
confirmed.detail: Verified. Line 173 `// Data the AX C callback reaches through refcon. Mutated only on the main thread.` and line 181 `/// Last emitted title, for dedupe debounce (drops chatty duplicates).` The 'Mutated only on the main thread' note is a real concurrency invariant for code using RefCell across an FFI callback boundary — that is load-bearing for the !Send/aliasing reasoning, not pure restatement. 'drops chatty duplicates' is mild editorializing. Net: low-value style nit, info.
confirmed.fix: Optionally trim line 181 to `/// Last emitted title, for dedupe.` Keep the main-thread invariant note (it documents why the RefCell interior mutability is sound).
confirmed.evidence: Field doc at mod.rs:173 and 181; the main-thread-only invariant underpins the unsafe shared-ref-plus-interior-mutability reasoning in observer_callback (311-313).

confirmed.title: browser.rs header is a 9-line essay of decision-archaeology (D8/C5/C6/R18)
confirmed.detail: Verified. browser.rs header (lines 1-9) narrates D8, the Chromium mode-first ladder, Safari's lack of a scriptable private property (R18), and the -1743 fallback (C6); the WHY recurs inline at lines 28-29 (Brave/Edge naming), 42-43, and 62-64. D8 and the Safari/incognito stance are explicitly deliberate (decisions.md D8 line 28, D33 line 119). The privacy reasoning sits next to security-critical code where over-documentation is defensible. Real verbosity but low value to change; info.
confirmed.fix: Optional: condense the header to ~2 lines and lean on D8/R18 references; keep the per-variant doc-comments (especially the Private variant). Do not remove the substance of the D8 reasoning — it guards a privacy hard rule.
confirmed.evidence: browser.rs header 1-9; inline rationale at 28-29 and 62-64; D8/R18 stance documented in decisions.md D8 (28) and D33 (119).

confirmed.title: URL parse branch can use match-on-strip directly
confirmed.detail: Verified. Lines 56-65: match run_osa(...).as_deref() with Some("PRIVATE"), Some(s) => inner match on s.strip_prefix("URL\t"), None => Normal(None). The control flow is correct and idiomatic; the -1743 comment at lines 62-64 is the only verbose part. This is a comment-volume nit, not a real simplification of logic — the suggested change is purely cosmetic.
confirmed.fix: Optional: trim the -1743 comment at lines 62-64 to a one-liner. The match structure itself is fine; do not restructure.
confirmed.evidence: browser.rs:56-65 match; -1743 explanatory comment at 62-64.

confirmed.title: osascript subprocess spawn duplicated between terminal.rs and browser.rs
confirmed.detail: Verified. iterm2_cwd (terminal.rs:25-42) does Command::new("/usr/bin/osascript").arg("-e").arg(script).output().ok()? then status check + trim stdout — the same shape as browser.rs run_osa (browser.rs:70-81), which uses the OSASCRIPT constant. terminal.rs hardcodes the literal path instead of a shared constant. Two copies of the same fragile subprocess plumbing. Real duplication; rated info since each is small and they differ in sentinel handling ("missing value").
confirmed.fix: Extract a shared run_osascript(script: &str) -> Option<String> helper (e.g. in a small macos submodule, or have terminal call browser's) used by both inspect/run_osa and iterm2_cwd; terminal layers the "missing value" sentinel on top. Note: if r0 is addressed by moving enrichment off-thread, this helper is the natural shared seam to add a timeout in one place.
confirmed.evidence: iterm2_cwd at terminal.rs:25-42 (spawn 28-32, hardcoded path); run_osa at browser.rs:70-81 using const OSASCRIPT (browser.rs:13).

confirmed.title: terminal.rs header narrates spike provenance and R22 rationale
confirmed.detail: Verified. Header (lines 1-9) references the spike port, R22 (non-root/unsandboxed), D30, and 'The hard part the spike left open'. The '[on-device verify]' markers appear at line 9 and line 47. These are real dev-note markers — D33 explicitly notes terminal fallback accuracy is on-device-verify (decisions.md line 119: 'The fallback accuracy is on-device-verify'), so they reflect a tracked, deliberate status rather than stray TODOs. Mild verbosity; info.
confirmed.fix: Optional: trim header to ~2 lines and reference D30/R22. The '[on-device verify]' markers correspond to a real D33 open item; if kept, leave them or convert to a single tracked note rather than deleting the status.
confirmed.evidence: terminal.rs header 1-9; '[on-device verify]' at lines 9 and 47; decisions.md D33 (line 119) records terminal fallback accuracy as on-device-verify.

confirmed.title: vip_path comment over-explains the buffer split
confirmed.detail: Verified. vip_path_to_string doc (lines 93-94) explains the [[c_char;32];32] layout is 'a 1024-byte MAXPATHLEN buffer split to satisfy old rustc'. The SAFETY comment at line 97 already states the 32*32 contiguity that matters. The 'old rustc' aside is minor archaeology but is genuinely useful context for why libc declares the nested-array shape. Cosmetic; info.
confirmed.fix: Optional: reduce to `/// vip_path is a contiguous 1024-byte MAXPATHLEN buffer; read flat to the NUL.` Low priority.
confirmed.evidence: Doc comment at terminal.rs:93-94; SAFETY contiguity note at line 97; function reads 32*32 bytes flat (98-100).

confirmed.title: NOTIF_MAIN_WINDOW_CHANGED handled identically to FOCUSED_WINDOW_CHANGED — verify it earns a separate constant
confirmed.detail: Verified the callback (mod.rs:317) treats NOTIF_FOCUSED_WINDOW_CHANGED and NOTIF_MAIN_WINDOW_CHANGED identically (both trigger reregister_title_observer), and APP_NOTIFICATIONS (lines 44-48) registers both plus FOCUSED_UI. However the suggestion is explicitly speculative ('if MAIN_WINDOW_CHANGED never fires distinctly in practice') and would require on-device evidence the reviewer does not have. Registering both window-changed notifications is cheap, defensive AX practice — removing one risks missing real focus changes on apps that emit only one. Not a defect; downgrade to info.
confirmed.fix: No code change recommended without on-device evidence that one notification is redundant. At most, add a one-line comment noting both are registered defensively because apps differ in which window-changed notification they emit.
confirmed.evidence: APP_NOTIFICATIONS at mod.rs:44-48 includes FOCUSED_WINDOW_CHANGED, MAIN_WINDOW_CHANGED, FOCUSED_UI_CHANGED; callback OR-branch at line 317 treats the first two the same. No D-number, and the reviewer's own fix is conditioned on unavailable on-device data.

confirmed.title: reprocess_logs (SQL LIKE) and find_category (Rust substring) categorize the same rows differently
confirmed.detail: Confirmed against the code and the documented contract. find_category (db.rs:408-411) matches with `match_target.to_lowercase().contains(&rule.pattern.to_lowercase())` — a literal substring test. reprocess_logs (db.rs:429-439) instead builds `format!("%{}%", rule.pattern)` and runs `lower(process_name|window_title) LIKE lower(?2)` with NO `ESCAPE` clause. In SQLite LIKE, `_` matches any single character and `%` matches any run, so a pattern containing `_` or `%` (e.g. the literal project/window name `usage_os`) matches MORE rows under reprocess than under live capture. Because reprocess_logs is the retroactive path whose whole job is to apply the same rules consistently (its doc and D45 frame it as mirroring live categorization), this is a silent correctness divergence: hitting reprocess can move spans into/out of categories that live capture would not. D45 (decisions.md:179) explicitly locks the engine semantics as case-insensitive SUBSTRING — it even calls out that `title: *.fig` is wrong because the engine is substring not wildcard ('`.fig` works, `*.fig` doesn't'). So the substring path is the intended contract and the LIKE path violates it; this is a real bug, not a deliberate choice.
confirmed.fix: Make both paths use identical substring semantics. Preferred: drop LIKE and reprocess in Rust — read candidate rows and reuse find_category's `.contains()` so there is ONE matcher. If keeping SQL LIKE, escape the meta-chars and add an ESCAPE clause: `let p = rule.pattern.replace('\\',"\\\\").replace('%',"\\%").replace('_',"\\_"); let pattern = format!("%{p}%");` and append `ESCAPE '\\'` to both LIKE clauses. Add a regression test with an underscore pattern (e.g. `usage_os`) asserting reprocess and find_category agree.
confirmed.evidence: db.rs:408-411 uses .contains(); db.rs:429 builds %..% and db.rs:432/437 run `LIKE lower(?2)` with no ESCAPE. decisions.md:179 (D45) locks the engine as case-insensitive substring and rejects wildcard semantics for `title:` rules. The LIKE path therefore contradicts the documented contract.

confirmed.title: Empty exclusion/rule pattern matches everything (contains("") is always true)
confirmed.detail: Confirmed. match_exclusion (db.rs:801) does `target.contains(&ex.pattern.to_lowercase())` and find_category (db.rs:408-411) does `match_target.to_lowercase().contains(&rule.pattern.to_lowercase())`. In Rust `"anything".contains("")` is always true, so an empty pattern is a catch-all: an empty exclusion would mark every event Private/Exclude (privacy over-reach that silently stops recording titles, or drops all events), and an empty rule pattern would categorize everything. The command handlers create_rule (lib.rs:208-225) and create_exclusion (lib.rs:~293) pass the pattern straight to the repo with no `trim().is_empty()` guard, and create_rule/create_exclusion in db.rs (374-386, 753-771) also do not reject it. So a blank field persists and silently catches everything. Severity kept at info: this requires a deliberate blank entry by the user in the Settings editor, not a routine path, but the guard is cheap and the failure mode (silent global private/exclude) is severe enough to warrant it.
confirmed.fix: Reject empty/whitespace-only patterns at the write boundary. In create_rule and create_exclusion (db.rs) — or their lib.rs command handlers — return an error when `pattern.trim().is_empty()`. Add a unit test asserting the error.
confirmed.evidence: db.rs:801 and db.rs:408-411 both use substring .contains on a user-supplied pattern; lib.rs create_rule (208-225) and create_exclusion forward pattern with no emptiness check; db.rs create_rule/create_exclusion likewise.

confirmed.title: reprocess_logs rule precedence comment contradicts the code (first-match-wins, not priority)
confirmed.detail: Confirmed comment drift. Lines 422-424 read: 'Get all rules (ordered by ID, so first rule created = higher priority if we assume ID order) // To support re-ordering, we'd need a priority column, but for now ID order is fine. // Logic: First rule that matches wins.' get_rules (db.rs:358-360) is `ORDER BY id ASC`, and reprocess's `category_id IS NULL AND ... LIKE` UPDATE loop means the first rule to claim a row wins — deterministic first-by-id-wins, matching find_category which iterates the same ordering and returns on first match. D45 confirms 'first rule wins / first-by-id wins' as the intended behaviour. The 'if we assume ID order' hedge mis-frames a deterministic, documented invariant as uncertain. This is purely a comment fix; the code is correct.
confirmed.fix: Tighten the comment to state the invariant: e.g. `// Rules apply in id-ascending order; first match claims the row (matches find_category).` Drop the 'if we assume ID order' hedge and the speculative priority-column aside (overlaps db-s6).
confirmed.evidence: db.rs:358-360 get_rules ORDER BY id ASC; db.rs:428-441 first-match-wins UPDATE loop; D45 (decisions.md:179) documents first-by-id-wins as intended.

confirmed.title: get_setting / find_project_by_alias / get_project repeat the same query-one-row dance
confirmed.detail: Confirmed. get_setting (db.rs:546-554), find_project_by_alias (db.rs:576-590), and get_project (db.rs:646-662) each hand-roll `prepare` + `query` + `if let Some(row) = rows.next()? { Ok(Some(..)) } else { Ok(None) }`. The exact `if let Some(row) = rows.next()?` shape appears at db.rs:549, 585, 651 (grep-confirmed, 3 instances). rusqlite's `query_row(...).optional()` does this in one expression and is already used in this same file two functions away in resolve_or_create_project (db.rs:619-625) — so the verbose form is inconsistent with the established idiom. OptionalExtension is already imported (db.rs:1). Kept at warning: real, fixable duplication with a clear in-file precedent.
confirmed.fix: Rewrite each with query_row(...).optional(): get_setting → `conn.query_row(SQL, [key], |row| row.get(0)).optional()`; find_project_by_alias → `conn.query_row(SQL, (alias_kind, alias_value), |row| row.get(0)).optional()`; get_project → `conn.query_row(SQL, [id], |row| Ok(Project{ ... })).optional()`. Run cargo test (export/settings/project tests cover these) + clippy/fmt before merge.
confirmed.evidence: db.rs:549,585,651 all use the same `if let Some(row) = rows.next()?` dance; resolve_or_create_project (db.rs:619-625) already uses query_row(...).optional(); OptionalExtension imported at db.rs:1.

NOTE: moving it under `#[cfg(test)]` is safe here only because all callers are in the same crate's test module — verify no integration test in tests/ uses it before doing so (grep covered src/).

The doc on set_span_end (db.rs:237-239) says the mutation is 'the only mutation the capture write path needs' and 'The consumer state machine owns the open span, so there's no "find the last row" guesswork here (see capture::consume)' — contrasting against a superseded design, i.e. capture-write-path archaeology (D37-D41). confirmed.title: CSV header string duplicates ACTIVITY_LOG_COLUMNS instead of deriving from it
confirmed.detail: Observation confirmed but the proposed fix is WRONG and would break the merge gate. ACTIVITY_LOG_COLUMNS (db.rs:175-176) lists the 12 columns separated by `', '` (comma-SPACE, with a `\` line continuation): `"id, process_name, window_title, ..."`. The CSV header literal (db.rs:492-495) uses BARE commas: `"id,process_name,window_title,..."`. So `writeln!(w, "{ACTIVITY_LOG_COLUMNS}")` would emit spaces into the header — corrupting the CSV column names AND failing the existing test test_export_events_csv_header_and_rows (db.rs:1482 asserts `lines[0].starts_with("id,process_name,window_title,")`, no spaces). The reviewer's premise ('the constant's names match the CSV header verbatim') is false. The two-sources-of-truth risk is real but minor (a test guards column count at db.rs:1483, and any name reorder would also break the SELECT-vs-row_to_activity_log mapping loudly). Downgraded to info; if anyone wants one source of truth they must first normalize the constant to bare commas (which would then require updating the SELECT at db.rs:263/498 to tolerate that), so it is NOT a trivial swap.
confirmed.fix: Do NOT use `writeln!(w, "{ACTIVITY_LOG_COLUMNS}")` as-is — it injects spaces and breaks the header + its test. If unifying: change ACTIVITY_LOG_COLUMNS to bare-comma form `"id,process_name,..."` (no spaces), verify rusqlite still accepts it in the SELECTs at db.rs:263 and 498, then derive the header from it. Otherwise leave the literal and rely on the existing count test. Low priority.
confirmed.evidence: ACTIVITY_LOG_COLUMNS at db.rs:175-176 uses ', ' separators; CSV header literal at db.rs:492-495 uses bare commas; test at db.rs:1482 asserts bare-comma prefix. The proposed fix would change header output and fail the test.

confirmed.title: README "Current chain" table is stale — lists only migrations 1–2 of 6

The README's stated purpose (Rule 3, line 17-19: "register the file ... with its version + name") makes it a contributor-facing authoritative map, so 0003-0006 missing misleads anyone choosing the next version number. CLAUDE.md's Definition of Done requires docs to read true against code. This is a doc-staleness info nit, not a code bug.

It is two lines and is mild over-explanation. This is a subjective style call, not a defect; the 'dependency-free' note ties to the deps-conscious ethos (D35). Info-level at most.

confirmed.title: drift_error() helper wraps a single call site in ceremony
confirmed.detail: Confirmed: drift_error() (lines 137-143) is a 6-line helper with a doc comment, used exactly once at line 99. Inlining the SqliteFailure construction would be marginally shorter. But the named helper arguably reads more clearly at the call site (`return Err(drift_error(...))`) than an inline SqliteFailure/ffi::Error construction, and naming the intent ('drift') has value. This is a subjective micro-refactor, not a defect — downgraded to info.

confirmed.title: Drift check silently ignores a stored migration absent from MIGRATIONS (binary downgrade)
confirmed.detail: Confirmed. verify_applied_checksums (line 97) does `if let Some(migration) = MIGRATIONS.iter().find(|m| m.version == version)` — a schema_migrations row whose version is not in the binary's MIGRATIONS array is silently skipped. For a forward-only design (D35), the dangerous direction is the on-disk DB being AHEAD of the running binary (a downgrade/rollback or a user opening an old build), where the schema may contain columns/tables the binary doesn't expect. The checksum guard catches edits to known migrations but not 'DB is from the future'. This is a genuine gap, though low-likelihood for a single-user local desktop app, so warning (not critical). Forward-only being by-design (D35) does NOT cover this — D35 documents the absence of down-migrations, not tolerance of a newer DB.
confirmed.fix: Add a guard before running: if current_version(conn) > MIGRATIONS.last().version, return a clear 'database was created by a newer version of UsageOS' error. Or, if intentionally tolerated, add a one-line comment at line 97 stating rows beyond the binary's chain are accepted.
confirmed.evidence: migrations.rs line 97 find()/if-let confirmed; current_version() (line 77) and MIGRATIONS (line 22) available for the check. D35 in decisions.md covers forward-only/no-down-migrations but not newer-DB tolerance.

The comment is longer than the two UPDATEs. However, migration files are immutable once shipped (D35), and the project's documented ethos values self-explaining, auditable SQL; a header explaining a non-obvious recategorization (and the deliberate Research-only scoping of the history UPDATE) has real value to a reader. confirmed.title: `(SELECT COUNT(*) FROM activity_logs) = 0` fresh-install guard repeated on every statement
confirmed.detail: Confirmed. The fresh-install gate `(SELECT COUNT(*) FROM activity_logs) = 0` is repeated on every mutating statement: lines 12, 13, 14, 15 (the four UPDATE name changes), 21 (the Personal INSERT), 27 (the DELETE), and 42 (the rules INSERT-SELECT). The migration's own header (lines 6-7) promises 'every statement is gated ... so this migration ONLY ever changes a fresh database' — and that invariant rests entirely on every statement carrying the guard identically. A future statement added without the guard would silently rewrite an existing user's categories/rules — the exact harm the header rules out. Correctness depends on perfect copy-discipline, and there is no test locking the invariant. This is a real fragility, not pure style; warning.
confirmed.fix: Add a test asserting a DB seeded with an activity_logs row is untouched by 0006 (names, Personal absence, rules all unchanged) to lock the invariant. Optionally restructure so the empty-DB gate is expressed once (e.g. a temp guard table populated only when empty, joined into each statement).
confirmed.evidence: 0006 lines 12-15, 21-22, 27-30, 37-42 each carry `(SELECT COUNT(*) FROM activity_logs) = 0`. Header lines 6-7 state the per-statement-gate invariant. migrations.rs tests cover fresh-install behaviour only (seeds_..., fresh_install_routes_...) — no existing-data no-op test. D47 confirms fresh-installs-only is the intended contract.

The 'every statement gated on empty activity_logs' invariant (lines 6-7) is genuinely useful — see s5, the whole migration's safety rests on it — so the header is not pure noise. The dev-DB aside is archaeology, but trimming is a style preference on an immutable shipped file. Info.

confirmed.title: Schema comment says the →contexts rename is 'deferred (D31)' — stale after D46 reversed direction
confirmed.detail: Partially confirmed. The comment at lines 9-12 says 'The internal table name stays categories (the →contexts rename is deferred, D31)' and line 50 'the table keeps its legacy name activity_logs, D31'. Per D46 the Context→Category rename was fully reversed — code/IPC now say Category, matching the SQL — so a 'rename to contexts is deferred' note points at an abandoned direction and is genuinely stale wording. BUT: 0001 is a SHIPPED, IMMUTABLE migration (D35) — editing it after release is a deliberate startup error via the checksum drift guard, and 0001 is recorded in any existing DB. So this comment CANNOT be edited in place without tripping the drift guard (the same reason D46 protected the seed_canonical_contexts name). The staleness is real but unfixable in this file by design; the right place for the correction is decisions.md/README. Downgraded to info, and the suggested fix in the original finding (strip the prose from 0001) would TRIP the drift guard and break startup for existing DBs — must not be applied.
confirmed.fix: Do NOT edit 0001 (checksum drift guard, D35/D46). Leave the SQL file untouched; if the stale 'rename deferred' wording matters, note the D46 reversal in decisions.md/README rather than the immutable migration.
confirmed.evidence: 0001 lines 9-12 and 50-54 confirmed. D46 reversed Context→Category everywhere and explicitly protected the immutable migration name seed_canonical_contexts from the rename to avoid the drift guard; same immutability applies to 0001's body. D35 documents the checksum drift guard.

Reporter rated this 'low' confidence; downgraded to info and noting the fix is unsafe.
confirmed.fix: Do NOT edit 0002 (checksum drift guard, D35). Leave as-is.

confirmed.title: 0006 (c) deletes then re-inserts Spotify/Music rules — net move could be an UPDATE
confirmed.detail: Confirmed the mechanics: lines 26-30 DELETE the breaks-category Spotify/Music process rules, then lines 32-42 re-INSERT Spotify/Music (within the personal_apps list) under the personal category. For those two patterns it is effectively a category move expressed as delete-then-insert. The reporter rated this 'low' and explicitly says 'it's correct'. The delete+insert is also arguably cleaner than a special-cased UPDATE given the bulk personal_apps insert handles 17 apps uniformly — making Spotify/Music a separate UPDATE would fragment that list. This is a subjective micro-preference on an immutable shipped file (editing it trips the drift guard, D35). Info, do not change.

confirmed.title: Slug-less user category collides with truly-uncategorized time under the "other" slug, merging two different names
confirmed.detail: Verified real. The schema (migrations/0001_initial_schema.sql:15 `slug TEXT UNIQUE`, NULL allowed) and db.rs:123-129 explicitly document `None` slug = a user-created category. `category_of` (line 162-168) maps BOTH a meta whose `slug == None` AND a no-category event to the same `OTHER_SLUG`/"other". `build_day_view` keys `totals` by slug (line 183) and `or_insert((name, 0))` (line 196) keeps whichever name landed first, so two distinct slug-less user categories — or a slug-less user category plus genuinely-uncategorized time — merge into one `CategorySlice` mislabeled with one of the names. `raw_runs`/coalescing (line 413-418) keys on slug too, so dial arcs merge them as well, and `compute_recap_facts` leading/second can mis-name. This is an edge: it only bites when the user creates two or more own categories AND has events in each (or in one + uncategorized). The canonical 4 seeds all carry slugs (db test 1454-1465), so today's seeded setup is unaffected; it becomes reachable once the M4 rules editor lets users create slug-less categories and assign apps to them.
confirmed.fix: For a meta with `slug == None`, derive a per-category slug from the category id (e.g. `format!("other-{id}")`) so distinct user categories don't merge with each other or with truly-uncategorized time; reserve `OTHER_SLUG`/`OTHER_NAME` for the genuine no-category (`None` branch) case. Note the frontend `--c-<slug>` colour mapping (db.rs:122) expects user categories to carry their own `color` separately, so verify the dial still resolves a token for `other-<id>` (likely the neutral fallback) before shipping.
confirmed.evidence: migrations/0001_initial_schema.sql:11 ("A NULL slug is a user-created category"); db.rs:123-129 + test test_get_categories_exposes_canonical_slug (1454-1465) confirm user categories have slug=None; rollup.rs:164 `unwrap_or_else(|| OTHER_SLUG.to_string())` collapses them; totals keyed by slug at 183/196.

confirmed.title: Dead `.filter(|c| c.secs > 0)` on the runner-up category — slices never have zero secs
confirmed.detail: Verified. `second` (lines 622-628) filters `categories.get(1)` with `.filter(|c| c.secs > 0)`. Every `CategorySlice` in `categories` is built in `build_day_view` (lines 200-212) from `totals`, and `totals` only gains an entry inside the loop after `active_secs += secs` ran for an event with `secs != 0` and `!is_idle` (lines 187-197), so every slice necessarily has `secs > 0`. The filter can never reject anything — a harmless no-op that costs a reader a double-take. `leading` at 618 correctly omits the same filter, underscoring the inconsistency.
confirmed.fix: Drop `.filter(|c| c.secs > 0)` at line 624 — `categories.get(1)` alone conveys "a second category exists." If the invariant is worth pinning, replace it with a one-line comment rather than a runtime guard.
confirmed.evidence: build_day_view loop at rollup.rs:185-198 only inserts into totals for non-zero, non-idle events; slices map 1:1 from totals (200-212); leading (618) lacks the filter while second (624) has it.

confirmed.title: `category_of(...).0` clones both slug and name just to compare the slug in the deep-secs hot loop
confirmed.detail: Verified. `build_day_slice` line 246 does `category_of(event, categories).0 == DEEP_SLUG`. `category_of` (161-168) clones the slug AND `meta.name.clone()` (line 165) on every event, then `.0` discards the name. For the Week view this runs over up to 7 days of events, allocating a throwaway `String` name per event. Real but minor — an avoidable allocation in a non-perf-critical read-time rollup, hence info not warning. Note `build_day_view` (line 195) and `raw_runs` (line 413) legitimately use both fields, so the existing `category_of` stays.
confirmed.fix: Add a `slug_of(event, categories) -> &str` (or `Cow<str>`) helper that resolves only the slug without cloning the name, and use it at line 246; keep `category_of` for the name-consuming paths. Confirm it still returns `OTHER_SLUG` for the None/None-slug branches so the deep check is unchanged.
confirmed.evidence: rollup.rs:165 `meta.name.clone()` always runs; line 246 only reads `.0`; build_day_slice loops all events of the day.

confirmed.title: RawRunBuilder and SegRun are the same struct with a pure-copy conversion between them
confirmed.detail: Verified. `RawRunBuilder` (280-286) and `SegRun` (318-324) have identical fields (slug, name, start, end, segments); `SegRun::from_raw` (327-335) is a field-for-field move doing no work, invoked twice in `raw_runs` (424, 431). The only real distinction is method ownership (builder: new/add; SegRun: host_active/absorbed/total_active/finish), which can coexist on one type. This is leftover from the D40 builder-unification (D40 deleted the old parallel `RunBuilder`/`TimelineRunBuilder`, but left this two-step within the new path). Downgraded from warning to info: it's a real readability cleanup, not a correctness or merge-gate risk, and the two-phase split is a defensible (if redundant) modeling choice — the build/coalesce phases are conceptually distinct.
confirmed.fix: Collapse into one struct (keep the name `SegRun`): move `new`/`add` onto it and delete `from_raw`; `raw_runs` pushes the builder directly. Keep all methods on the one type. Run `cargo test` — the absorb/segmentation tests should be unaffected since fields/behaviour are identical.
confirmed.evidence: Identical field lists at 281-285 vs 319-323; from_raw (327-335) is a pure move; D40 entry (decisions.md:160) documents the unification that left this remnant.

confirmed.title: Constant doc-comments re-narrate D34/D41 dogfooding history that belongs in decisions.md
confirmed.detail: Verified the comments exist: `IDLE_GAP_ENDS_RUN_SECS` (20-23), `ABSORB_SECS` (25-30, including the accurate "Raised 90→180s after dogfooding (D41)" — D41 in decisions.md:165 confirms this tuning), `MAX_ABSORB_FRACTION_PCT` (31-34). They do re-tell the *why* that lives in decisions.md (D40/D41). However, CLAUDE.md also says these constants are dogfood-tunable by design (D38-D41) and the prompt-context flags them as intentional tunable knobs, not magic-number smells; a short pointer to the rationale at the knob you'll actually edit has real value for the next dogfooding pass. This is a low-stakes style preference, not a defect — downgrade to info and treat as optional.
confirmed.fix: Optional: trim each to the invariant plus a bare D-ref, e.g. `/// An idle/untracked gap at least this long ends a category-run (D34a).` and `/// Max wall-clock span of an absorbed excursion; 180s after D41 dogfood tune.` Keep the D-number so the tuner can find the full rationale; drop the multi-sentence clustering/placeholder narration.
confirmed.evidence: Comments at 20-34 verified; D41 (decisions.md:165) confirms the 90→180 history is accurate, i.e. it duplicates the ADR.

confirmed.title: SegRun::finish carries a debate-rationale paragraph that should be a one-liner
confirmed.detail: Verified: lines 361-363 inside `finish` re-argue the D34a host-only invariant ("Headline numbers are host-category only ... the off-category detour never injects a phantom project slice (D34a, debate)"). The struct-level doc at 315-317 already states the same host-only invariant. So it is duplicated decision-archaeology. Downgraded to info — it's a defensible inline reminder at the exact code that enforces the invariant (the `host_active()` + host-filtered projects loop), and removing it is a pure style call with no functional benefit.
confirmed.fix: Optional: reduce 361-363 to a single line, e.g. `// Host-category only (D34a): the detour's secs live on its segment, not here.` The struct-level invariant carries the rest.
confirmed.evidence: finish() body 360-393 verified; struct doc 315-317 states the same host-only rule; D40 (decisions.md:160) is the source ADR.

confirmed.title: match on Option to break the loop is more directly a let-else / if-let
confirmed.detail: Verified. `absorb_excursions` collects `target: Option<(usize, usize)>` (445) and at 476-496 does `match target { Some((i, j)) => {…merge…}, None => break }`. The `None => break` arm is a roundabout way to say "stop when no absorbable window was found." A `let Some((i, j)) = target else { break };` flattens one nesting level and reads more directly. Clean, mechanical-ish, low-risk simplification — but it touches control flow (logic), so not auto-applied.
confirmed.fix: Replace the trailing `match target { … }` with `let Some((i, j)) = target else { break };` followed by the merge body (478-493) at the same indent. Run `cargo test` (the absorb fixpoint tests) and `cargo clippy -D warnings` — clippy actually prefers let-else here, so this also silences a potential `manual_let_else` lint.
confirmed.evidence: rollup.rs:476-496 verified; the merge body 478-493 is the only Some arm, None is a bare break.

confirmed.title: RecapFacts doc re-explains the rule-6 contract already stated in the module header

Foundation Models sidecar reuses the same struct ... template as fallback" that the module header (lines 4-5) already establishes. The "kept honest — we don't compute what we don't say" is meta-commentary. Real minor duplication, but it's a useful at-a-glance contract right on the struct the AI seam will reuse (the seam is intentionally dormant until chunk D per D49), and trimming it is a pure style preference — info, optional.

confirmed.title: Three adjacent functions each repeat the "one source of truth, dial and Timeline can never disagree" claim

confirmed.title: build_runs hand-copies seven fields that are a strict subset of TimelineRun
confirmed.detail: Verified: `build_runs` (529-546) maps each `TimelineRun` to `CategoryRun` by re-listing every field except `segments` (536-544). `CategoryRun` (70-80) is exactly `TimelineRun` (138-147) minus `segments`. A `From<TimelineRun> for CategoryRun` impl would localise the projection so it can't drift if a field is added to both. Real, but it's a 7-field copy in one place and the explicit map is arguably clearer at a glance; info-level ergonomic nicety, not a defect. (The line cited by the reviewer is 529, the fn start; the field copy is 536-544.)

Both are plain structs deriving the same traits, so no specta/IPC impact (CategoryRun's shape is unchanged); run `cargo test` + bindings-freshness check to confirm.

confirmed.title: human_secs_long carries the '47m → 47 million' spike anecdote inline
confirmed.detail: Verified: `human_secs_long` doc (688-690) explains the long form exists because "the spike found the model misreads [47m] as 47 million," and the same note appears as a test comment in ai/mod.rs:114 (confirmed via grep). So it is duplicated. The fix's own phrasing keeps the rationale; this is style-only.

confirmed.evidence: rollup.rs:688-690 verified; ai/mod.rs:114 grep hit confirms the duplicate note in the test.

confirmed.title: duration() clamps negative spans with .max(0) but nothing guarantees or documents why end<start can occur
confirmed.detail: Verified the code: `duration` (156-158) is `(event.end_time - event.start_time).max(0)`. The clamp IS justified by a documented reality, contrary to the finding's "impossible case" framing: D38 (decisions.md:151) makes the capture writer extend spans "forward-only (handles sleep/wake + backward clock skew)" and D41 (decisions.md:165) documents lock/sleep cycles where the HID clock misbehaves. A `.max(0)` here is a cheap, correct guard against a backward clock step producing a negative span at read time — removing it would let one skewed row poison `active_secs`/`pct`. So the finding's suggested 'drop the clamp' is wrong; only the 'add a one-line comment' half has merit, and even that is optional. Downgrade to info.
confirmed.fix: Optional, documentation-only: add `// clock can step back on sleep/wake (D38); clamp so a skewed row can't go negative.` Do NOT drop the `.max(0)` — it's load-bearing given the forward-only write path.
confirmed.evidence: D38 (decisions.md:151) 'extend-forward-only ... handles sleep/wake + backward clock skew'; D41 (decisions.md:165) documents lock/sleep clock anomalies — the clamp guards a real, documented case.

confirmed.title: 127.0.0.1 with a port is not treated as a local dev server (asymmetry with localhost)
confirmed.detail: Confirmed. Line 176 reads `host == "localhost" || host == "127.0.0.1" || host.starts_with("localhost:")`. parse_url (line 318-319) keeps the port on host: for `http://127.0.0.1:3002/`, rest.split_once('/') yields host=`127.0.0.1:3002`, which equals neither `127.0.0.1` (exact) nor matches `localhost:` prefix, so it falls through line 184/185 to Classified::NoSignal. By contrast `http://localhost:3002/` is caught by the starts_with arm → Ambiguous. So the loopback-IP-with-port dev server (the `vite --host`/Docker case) is mislabeled no-signal, losing the D30 ambiguous-correlation signal that the loopback hostname form preserves. The test `localhost_and_dashboards_are_ambiguous` (line 453-473) only exercises `http://localhost:3002/`, leaving the 127.0.0.1:port path uncovered. This is a real asymmetry, not a documented choice — D30 explicitly wants localhost dev servers classed Ambiguous for correlation.
confirmed.fix: Strip the port before comparing, mirroring parse_site: `let bare = host.split(':').next().unwrap_or(host); if bare == "localhost" || bare == "127.0.0.1" {` and drop the now-redundant starts_with arm. Add `http://127.0.0.1:3002/` to the ambiguous test array. Not autofixable (logic change touching classification behavior + needs a test).
confirmed.evidence: parse_url line 318-319 keeps port on host; line 176 lacks a 127.0.0.1:port arm; test at 453-473 only covers localhost:3002. D30 (decisions.md:105) wants localhost dev servers → ambiguous for Phase-2 correlation, so missing the loopback-IP form is a genuine gap.

confirmed.title: git is shelled out (up to 2 subprocesses) on every terminal focus event with no caching
confirmed.detail: Confirmed as written. classify_cwd (line 114) calls git() at line 115 (rev-parse --show-toplevel) and line 120 (remote get-url origin) — two fork/exec per cwd-bearing focus event, no memoization by path or toplevel. The finding itself correctly notes this runs on the dedicated capture thread (capture/mod.rs consume path), is injection-safe (fixed argv with -C), and is purely a per-event cost note, not a safety bug. Severity info is right; the finding does not claim otherwise and explicitly marks the fix optional.
confirmed.fix: Optional memoization keyed by input dir (or resolved toplevel) for process lifetime, since a repo's remote rarely changes in a session. Only worth it if focus-switch CPU shows up in dogfooding. Not autofixable (adds shared state on a perf hunch).
confirmed.evidence: git() called twice in classify_cwd (lines 115, 120); git() impl at 279-295 does Command::new("git"). Capture is event-driven on a dedicated thread (decisions.md D33/capture port), so this is cost not blocking.

confirmed.title: classify_title can mint phantom projects from non-editor titles containing ' - '
confirmed.detail: Confirmed behavior: classify_title (line 190-212) splits on ` — `/` - `, takes the last segment (line 201), and only suppresses an exact (case-insensitive) APP_NAMES match (line 202). A dashed non-editor title like `Re: invoice (5) - billing@acme.com - Fastmail` yields candidate `Fastmail`, and since it is weak (line 210) it first reconciles via the `name` alias (resolve, line 89-95) but creates a brand-new weak project when no alias matches. So noise can pollute the project list. However: title is the lowest-precedence, intentionally-lossy weak signal (D30 ranks window-title weakest), and D30 explicitly accepts that a wrong label is a cost while the no-fragmentation reconciliation is the primary guard. This is a heuristic-quality observation, not a rule break — the finding's own text concedes this. Downgrading warning→info accordingly; the finding was filed under correctness but is tuning, deferred to D34a-style dogfood.
confirmed.fix: Tighten toward editor-shaped titles: only create-new from a title when find_project_by_alias("name", candidate) already exists (else NoSignal), or widen suppression to a common-app/site-suffix denylist. Defer to dogfooding (D30 recall is explicitly re-measured later per R23/R26/R27). Not autofixable (heuristic/behavior change).
confirmed.evidence: classify_title line 201 takes parts[last]; line 202 suppresses only exact APP_NAMES; resolve line 89-95 creates new when no name alias matches. D30 (decisions.md:105) ranks title weakest and accepts wrong-label cost; recall tuning is explicitly deferred (R23/R26/R27).

confirmed.title: github.com matched with ends_with — subdomain/lookalike host slips through
confirmed.detail: Confirmed. Line 159 is `if host.ends_with("github.com")`. A lookalike host such as `notgithub.com` or `evilgithub.com` satisfies ends_with and enters the owner/repo parser (lines 160-171), minting a bogus canonical `owner/repo` project from a non-GitHub host. owner/repo extraction is only meaningful for the real github.com (and www.github.com). The other ends_with uses (DEV_DASHBOARDS line 179, GENERAL_HOSTS line 182) are intentional subdomain matching where any subdomain is fine, but here the literal path-shape assumption makes ends_with wrong. Re-labeling as correctness (a wrong project gets created) rather than pure simplify, but keeping warning severity.
confirmed.fix: Match exact host: `if host == "github.com" || host == "www.github.com" {`. Note host may carry a port from parse_url, but GitHub URLs in practice have none; if defensiveness is wanted, strip port first. Not autofixable (logic change).
confirmed.evidence: Line 159 host.ends_with("github.com") feeds owner/repo parse at 160-171; parse_url returns raw host so `notgithub.com` passes. No decision sanctions lookalike matching.

confirmed.title: Redundant/inconsistent localhost check — port handling differs across the two loopback forms
confirmed.detail: Confirmed, and this is the simplify-lane framing of the same root cause as enrich-r0 (line 176). The three-way OR is inconsistent: `host == "127.0.0.1"` never matches `127.0.0.1:3000` while `host.starts_with("localhost:")` does match the ported localhost form. Stripping the port once (as parse_site/mod.rs:15 already does) makes both loopback forms behave identically and removes the awkward starts_with arm. Kept at info as a cleanup; the behavioral correctness impact is tracked under enrich-r0 (do not double-fix — the single port-strip edit resolves both).
confirmed.fix: `let bare = host.split(':').next().unwrap_or(host); if bare == "localhost" || bare == "127.0.0.1" {` — drops the starts_with arm and fixes 127.0.0.1:PORT. Same edit as enrich-r0's fix. Not autofixable (behavior-affecting).
confirmed.evidence: Line 176; parse_site (mod.rs:15) already does host.split(':').next() — the same idiom duplicated. Same line as enrich-r0.

confirmed.title: Dead final branch: GENERAL_HOSTS check is redundant with the catch-all NoSignal
confirmed.detail: Confirmed. Lines 182-184 run `GENERAL_HOSTS.iter().any(...)` and return Classified::NoSignal; line 185 is the unconditional `Classified::NoSignal` fallthrough. Any host not matched by github/localhost/dashboards already returns NoSignal at line 185, so the GENERAL_HOSTS branch is observationally a no-op and the GENERAL_HOSTS const (lines 248-261) has no behavioral effect. Confirmed grep shows GENERAL_HOSTS is referenced only at line 182 and its definition at 248. Note: deleting both will trip clippy/-D warnings only if it leaves the const defined-but-unused — so the const must be deleted together with the branch (the fix already says this), otherwise dead_code fails the gate. With both removed, gates stay green.
confirmed.fix: Delete lines 182-184 and the GENERAL_HOSTS const (lines 248-261); rely on the trailing Classified::NoSignal. If kept as intent documentation, move it into a test. Not marking autofixable: it is more than dead-import removal (deletes a const + a runtime branch, must be done as a pair to satisfy -D warnings) — apply deliberately.
confirmed.evidence: Lines 182-185: branch returns NoSignal, next line returns NoSignal. grep confirms GENERAL_HOSTS used only at 182, defined at 248. Removing the branch alone would orphan the const and fail dead_code under -D warnings.

confirmed.title: Module doc re-narrates the D30 decision rationale (belongs in decisions.md)
confirmed.detail: Confirmed the text exists (module header lines 1-10 includes 'A wrong label costs more trust than a gap, so we assign only on a confident, unambiguous signal', 'the spike's headline finding', 'so one project never fragments'), which does duplicate D30's rationale (decisions.md:105). However this is a low-stakes style preference, not a defect — a module header summarizing why the abstain model exists is defensible and arguably helpful for a heuristic-heavy file. Keeping as info but flagging it is genuinely optional; the project's own docs-in-lockstep ethos tolerates a pointer-style header. No functional impact.

See decisions.md D30 for the abstain/no-fragmentation rationale.' Autofixable as a pure comment edit — but it is subjective wording, so leave to author discretion.

confirmed.title: Candidate struct field comments restate D30 reconciliation rationale

Sibling to enrich-s3/s8 (same over-narration pattern across the file).
confirmed.fix: Optional: keep one terse line on the weak field ('weak signal: reconcile by name_alias before creating — D30') and trim the duplicates on name_alias and in resolve's doc. Autofixable as a pure comment edit; leave to author discretion on wording.
confirmed.evidence: Lines 42-45 (field comments), 86-87 (resolve doc), 6-8 (module header) all carry the same reconciliation prose.

confirmed.title: parse_site duplicates port-stripping that parse_url could own
confirmed.detail: Confirmed. parse_site (mod.rs:13-21) calls project::parse_url, discards scheme and path, then re-strips the port at line 15 (`host.split(':').next().unwrap_or("")`). The localhost classifier at project.rs:176 needs the same port-strip (per enrich-r0/s1). Port-stripping the host is done in two places with the inconsistency that parse_url itself returns the port-bearing host. Low-priority cleanup; correct as-is. Confidence is appropriately low.
