Removes the duplicated split(':').next() idiom. Not autofixable (touches a pub(crate) signature/shared helper extraction).
confirmed.evidence: mod.rs:15 split(':').next() duplicates the same dance needed at project.rs:176; parse_url (project.rs:311) returns raw host.

confirmed.title: no-remote branch clones folder three times
confirmed.detail: Partially confirmed. The double-git observation duplicates enrich-r1 and is explicitly conceded as fine in the finding itself. The substantive part is the no-remote branch (lines 133-139): it does `canonical_key: folder.clone()`, `display_name: folder.clone()`, and `name_alias: Some(folder)` — that is two clones plus a move of the original (not three clones; the third use moves folder). So 'clones folder three times' is slightly overstated: it is two clones + one move. Real but trivial micro-verbosity; downgrading to info. No correctness impact.
confirmed.fix: Minor: e.g. `let key = folder; Candidate { canonical_key: key.clone(), display_name: key.clone(), name_alias: Some(key), .. }` — one fewer clone, or accept as-is. Not worth a behavior risk. Not autofixable (logic-adjacent restructuring of a struct literal).
confirmed.evidence: Lines 133-139: folder.clone() (134), folder.clone() (135), Some(folder) (137) — two clones + a move, not three clones as stated. Double-git is the same as enrich-r1.

confirmed.title: parse_url returns owned Strings where borrows suffice
confirmed.detail: Confirmed factually: parse_url (line 311) returns (String, String, String) and allocates via to_string()/format! (lines 313-321) although callers (classify_url line 144, parse_site mod.rs:14) only read the parts. Borrowing would avoid per-event allocations. But this is a leaf helper called once per focus event (not a hot loop), the alloc cost is negligible at that cadence, and the suggested signature change (returning &str/Cow with the format!("/{path}") owned piece) adds lifetime/Cow complexity for no measurable gain. Over-engineering relative to the call frequency; downgraded to info, do-not-act unless profiling says otherwise. The finding's own confidence is low.
confirmed.fix: Optional micro-opt: return borrowed slices with a Cow for the leading-slash path case. Given once-per-event cadence the current owned-String form is clearer; recommend leaving as-is. Not autofixable (public-ish signature + lifetimes).
confirmed.evidence: parse_url line 311-322 allocates 3 Strings; callers at project.rs:144 and mod.rs:14 only read. Called per focus event, not a hot loop.

confirmed.title: git() comment over-explains the threading rationale
confirmed.detail: Confirmed the comment exists (lines 277-278: 'Shells out (the consumer runs on a dedicated thread, so this never blocks the async executor).'). It does put a caller-invariant on a leaf helper. But the note is accurate and short, and the threading guarantee is load-bearing context for why shelling out here is acceptable — arguably useful at the call boundary. Pure style; downgraded to discretionary info, sibling to enrich-s3/s4 over-narration pattern. No functional impact.
confirmed.fix: Optional: trim to 'Run `git -C <dir> ...`, returning trimmed stdout on success, None on failure/empty.' Autofixable as a comment-only edit; leave to author discretion since the threading note has some documentary value.
confirmed.evidence: Lines 277-278 carry the executor sentence on the git() leaf helper; same over-narration family as the module header and field comments.

confirmed.title: classify_title separator split is over-built for a single observed format
confirmed.detail: Confirmed: lines 191-196 split on ` — ` then flat_map split on ` - `, map trim, filter empty, collect into a Vec, then only use parts[parts.len()-1] (line 201) and parts.len() (line 198). The intermediate Vec + double-delimiter flat_map is heavier than 'take the trailing segment after the rightmost separator'. Real minor over-build. Downgraded to info: it is correct and the allocation is once-per-event (negligible). The suggested rsplit fix is reasonable but a behavior-equivalent rewrite that needs the existing tests (cwd_and_title_do_not_fragment line 391, bare_app_title_is_no_signal line 516) to stay green — worth care, not urgency. Confidence appropriately low.
confirmed.fix: Optional: replace the Vec pipeline with a rightmost-separator split, e.g. find the last ` — `/` - ` and take the trailing trimmed segment; NoSignal if none. Must preserve the parts.len()<2 → NoSignal semantics (a title with no separator yields no project). Verify against existing title tests. Not autofixable (logic rewrite).
confirmed.evidence: Lines 191-196 build a Vec via flat_map over two delimiters; only parts[last] (201) and len (198) are used. Tests at 391 and 516 pin the behavior.

confirmed.title: Abstain reason as &'static str + duplicated literals instead of an enum
confirmed.detail: Confirmed: ProjectAssignment::Abstain(&'static str) (line 33) carries free-form `"ambiguous"`/`"no-signal"` literals minted in infer_project (lines 79-83), the legal values are enumerated in a doc comment (lines 31-32), and the caller stringifies via reason.to_string() (capture/mod.rs:223). An AbstainReason enum would make the two kinds exhaustive and typo-proof. This is a valid design-taste suggestion, not a defect — current code is correct and the two literals are produced in exactly one place (lines 80/82), limiting typo risk. Note a refactor would ripple into capture/mod.rs:223 and must keep persisting the exact strings `no-signal`/`ambiguous` that D31 pins as the stored project_abstain_reason values (decisions.md:110) — so any enum must serialize to those exact strings or it breaks the documented storage contract. Downgraded to info (over-engineering lane, low priority, low confidence by author's own mark).
confirmed.fix: Optional: `enum AbstainReason { Ambiguous, NoSignal }` with `fn as_str(&self) -> &'static str` returning exactly "ambiguous"/"no-signal" (must match D31's stored values), Abstain(AbstainReason); update capture/mod.rs:223 to use as_str(). Removes the doc enumeration and stringly-typed literals. Not autofixable (type change crossing the persistence contract).
confirmed.evidence: Line 33 Abstain(&'static str); literals at 79-83; doc at 31-32; capture/mod.rs:223 reason.to_string(). D31 (decisions.md:110) pins project_abstain_reason ∈ {NULL, no-signal, ambiguous} as stored strings — the constraint a refactor must honor.

confirmed.title: Module doc re-narrates decision IDs (D48/D9/C5/C19) instead of describing behavior
confirmed.detail: Confirmed the lines exist: the 8-line header (lines 1-7) does carry D48/D9/C5/C19 tags. However this is a defensible style choice, not a defect. CLAUDE.md's whole workflow is built on dense decision cross-referencing (D1-D50, ADR-style, 'cross-reference from plans/handoffs'), and D49 documents this seam as deliberately dormant with the tags being the live trace back to rationale. The header still describes behavior (one entry point, formats facts, falls back on failure/empty). Trimming is a matter of taste and the cited tags genuinely point to real decisions a reader can resolve. Downgraded to a take-it-or-leave-it info suggestion; not actionable as a quality bug.
confirmed.fix: Optional: trim D/C tags from prose if the team prefers leaner headers, keeping the behavioral summary. Not required — the tags are consistent with the project's cross-referencing convention.
confirmed.evidence: Read mod.rs lines 1-7 — tags present as described. context/decisions.md D49 confirms the seam is deliberately dormant and the decision IDs are the documented rationale trail; CLAUDE.md mandates decision cross-referencing.

confirmed.title: AiError doc re-explains that build_recap ignores the variant distinction
confirmed.detail: Confirmed: lines 14-15 carry the 'treats every variant identically ... keeps the failure legible for logging' doc. But the premise that this defends an unused split is wrong: D49 explicitly plans chunks B-C to spawn the Swift sidecar with 'status tags (C4-C7)', and Unavailable vs Model map exactly to those planned status tags (Apple Intelligence off / device ineligible vs guardrail refusal / overflow). The comment documents real near-term intent, not dead archaeology. It is slightly verbose but accurate and forward-pointing. Downgraded to an optional trim.
confirmed.fix: Optional: shorten to '/// Why a narration attempt failed.' if leaner is preferred; the per-variant docs (lines 18, 20) carry the detail. Keeping the logging note is also reasonable given the planned chunk-B/C status tags.
confirmed.evidence: Read mod.rs lines 14-22. decisions.md D49: 'productionize the Swift sidecar ... status tags (C4-C7)' — the two variants are the documented future consumers.

confirmed.title: generated_by sentinel strings split across two files (const here, bare literal in rollup.rs)
confirmed.detail: Confirmed the factual claim: GENERATED_BY_MODEL = "foundation-models" is a named const at ai/mod.rs:12, while its counterpart "template" is a bare string literal at rollup.rs:684, and tests assert the raw strings in both files (mod.rs:123 'foundation-models', mod.rs:134/143 'template', rollup.rs:905 'template'). So the two sentinels that classify the same Recap.generated_by field do live in different modules with inconsistent treatment (one const, one inline). This is a mild, real consistency/drift smell. Downgraded from the implied actionable to info: the strings are stable, asserted by tests in both files (drift would be caught by cargo test, a merge gate), and co-locating them is a nicety not a correctness fix. Worth doing opportunistically.
confirmed.fix: Optional: define both sentinels as consts near the Recap struct in rollup.rs (e.g. RECAP_BY_TEMPLATE/RECAP_BY_MODEL) and have render_template_recap (rollup.rs:684) and build_recap (mod.rs:59) reference them instead of literals. Keep test assertions on the literal values so they remain an independent guard.
confirmed.evidence: Read ai/mod.rs:12,59 (const + use) and rollup.rs:684 (bare 'template' literal); grep confirms tests assert raw strings in both files (mod.rs:123/134/143, rollup.rs:905). cargo test is a documented merge gate, limiting drift risk.

confirmed.title: Narrator::narrate doc restates the prompt-guarding decision already covered by build_recap
confirmed.detail: Confirmed: lines 28-29 doc the narrate method with the 'instructed not to alter numbers ... build_recap still defends with the fallback' sentence, which does overlap with build_recap's own doc (lines 48-50) and hard rule 6. Mild redundancy. But it is accurate context for an implementor writing the real narrator (it tells them the contract: don't trust the model to obey, the caller still guards). This is a style preference, not a defect. Downgraded to optional trim.
confirmed.fix: Optional: reduce to '/// Phrase the pre-formatted facts prompt into prose.' The numbers/fallback policy is restated in build_recap (lines 48-62).
confirmed.evidence: Read mod.rs lines 28-30 and 48-62 — the fallback policy is indeed stated in both places. Overlap is real but the trait-doc framing is a legitimate contract note.

confirmed.title: Trait doc explains the dyn/async-crate dispatch rationale (belongs in decisions, not the trait)
confirmed.detail: Confirmed: line 25 carries 'Generic dispatch only (no `dyn`), so the async method needs no extra crate.' next to the #[allow(async_fn_in_trait)] attribute (line 26). The claim that the attribute alone signals the choice is only half-true — the attribute explains why the lint is allowed, but the 'no dyn / no extra crate (avoiding async-trait)' rationale is non-obvious and genuinely useful to a future maintainer tempted to box the trait. This is a defensible explanatory comment, not bloat. Lowest-value of the set; downgraded to optional.
confirmed.fix: Optional: drop the second sentence if leaner is preferred. Retaining it is reasonable — it preempts a maintainer adding async-trait or Box<dyn Narrator>, which would break the generic-dispatch design.
confirmed.evidence: Read mod.rs lines 24-31 — comment and #[allow(async_fn_in_trait)] both present; the rationale explains a real design constraint (generic-only dispatch to avoid async-trait), not merely restating the attribute.

confirmed.title: cache_key collisions can serve one app's icon to a different app
confirmed.detail: Verified real. Dedup in collect_dir (apps.rs:112) keys on stem.to_lowercase(), so "Foo Bar" and "Foo-Bar" are distinct keys and BOTH survive dedup. But the cache filename uses cache_key(stem) (apps.rs:133, 189-193), which maps every non-ASCII-alphanumeric char to '_': cache_key("Foo Bar") == cache_key("Foo-Bar") == "Foo_Bar". Non-ASCII collapses too ("Café"/"Cafe"). When app B is processed after app A wrote the colliding PNG, is_cached_fresh (apps.rs:196) compares B's icns mtime against A's PNG mtime; if A's PNG is newer, sips is skipped and B reads A's PNG (apps.rs:147) — B is shown A's icon. Silent (no error), order-dependent across the cache lifetime. The doc at line 188 says 'stable per app name' but it is only stable per *normalized* name, which is not injective. Real but low-probability (requires two installed apps whose stems differ only in non-alphanumeric/diacritic chars). Note: even the fix's distinct-PNG approach is fine, but the underlying collision can only bite the rare near-homograph pair; impact is one wrong icon, never data loss or a panic.
confirmed.fix: Make the filename injective per stem: append a short stable hash of the original stem, e.g. format!("{}-{:08x}.png", cache_key(stem), hash_of(stem)), or hex-encode the non-alphanumeric bytes instead of collapsing each to '_'. Either stays filesystem-safe while keeping distinct names distinct. Must not panic / add a dep beyond std (a tiny inline FNV-1a fold suffices, matching the project's no-new-dep posture, D43).
confirmed.evidence: apps.rs:112 dedup key = stem.to_lowercase(); apps.rs:133/189-193 cache filename = cache_key(stem) collapsing non-alnum to '_'; apps.rs:196-204 is_cached_fresh compares mtimes; apps.rs:147 reads the cached PNG on a fresh hit. No other call site uses cache_key (grep), so no siblings.

confirmed.title: suggest_slug doc-comment re-narrates the D47 decision rationale
confirmed.detail: Verified. The doc on suggest_slug (apps.rs:49-54) re-narrates the WHY that already lives in D47 ('Deliberately conservative... ambiguous ones span several of our categories... The rules engine remains the only thing that actually assigns'). The match arms (apps.rs:57-63) plus the test suggest_slug_maps_high_confidence_types_and_abstains (apps.rs:253-274) make the conservatism self-evident. Minor style nit only.
confirmed.fix: Trim to one line, e.g. /// Map Apple's LSApplicationCategoryType to a canonical category slug for a *suggested* default (D47); None when absent or ambiguous. The productivity/business/browsers narration and the 'rules engine remains the only thing' sentence belong in decisions.md (where they already are).
confirmed.evidence: apps.rs:49-54 doc text mirrors D47 in context/decisions.md:188 nearly verbatim. The match + test already encode the behavior.

confirmed.title: Module header is a 10-line essay re-narrating privacy/hard-rule rationale
confirmed.detail: Verified. The module doc (apps.rs:1-10) spends ~10 lines justifying choices against hard rules 1/3 ('same subprocess posture as enrich's git', 'no unwrap/expect/panic here (hard rule 3)', 'On non-macOS (CI Linux) the app dirs are absent and sips is missing'). The no-panic / degrade-to-None behavior is visible in the code, and the CI-Linux note recurs at apps.rs:68, 106, 144. This is decision-archaeology that lives in D43. Info-level.
confirmed.fix: Cut to ~3 lines: what the module does (installed-app catalog + cached 64px PNGs via sips) and the one load-bearing invariant (every failure degrades to icon: None). Drop the hard-rule citations and the repeated CI-Linux explanation.
confirmed.evidence: apps.rs:1-10 module doc; D43 (decisions.md:175-176) already records the no-dep/no-panic/degrade-to-None rationale. CI-Linux note repeated at apps.rs:68,106,144.

confirmed.title: unwrap_or(category_type) fallback in suggest_slug is dead — the branch is unreachable
confirmed.detail: Verified high-confidence. apps.rs:56 is `let leaf = category_type.rsplit('.').next().unwrap_or(category_type);`. str::rsplit always yields at least one item (for "" it yields Some("")), so .next() is always Some and the unwrap_or(category_type) fallback is unreachable belt-and-suspenders. Harmless but misleading. Same dead-fallback shape exists at enrich/project.rs:332 (`path.rsplit('/').next().unwrap_or(path)`).
confirmed.fix: Use unwrap_or_default() (still defensive, drops the misleading fallback), or since it cannot fail, .expect("rsplit yields at least one item") with the invariant comment per hard rule 3. unwrap_or_default() is simplest. Note this changes behavior only in the impossible case, so verify the test still passes (it will — "" maps to None either way).
confirmed.evidence: apps.rs:56; std rsplit/next semantics; sibling identical pattern at enrich/project.rs:332 found via grep. Test at apps.rs:273 covers the "" input.

confirmed.title: cache_key is a thin single-caller helper; its doc restates the body
confirmed.detail: Verified but minor. cache_key (apps.rs:189-193) is a 4-line helper called exactly once (apps.rs:133). The extraction itself earns its keep (testability + clarity at the call site), so 'over-engineering' overstates it; the only actionable bit is the doc comment at apps.rs:188 restating the obvious body, which overlaps the comment-bloat findings. Downgraded to info as a pure style nit. (Do not inline if the cache_key collision fix in apps-r0 lands — the helper is the natural home for an injective key.)
confirmed.fix: Keep the function (especially given apps-r0). Optionally drop the one-line doc at 188 since the name + body are self-explanatory. Do not inline.
confirmed.evidence: apps.rs:189-193 body; sole call site apps.rs:133 (grep confirms only one caller).

confirmed.title: best_icns hand-rolls a max-by-key fold instead of Iterator::max_by_key
confirmed.detail: Verified. best_icns (apps.rs:158-185) accumulates Option<(PathBuf, i32, u64)> with an explicit match &best { None => true, Some((_, bscore, bsize)) => score > *bscore || (score == *bscore && size > *bsize) } (apps.rs:177-183). Since (score, size) is a natural Ord tuple key, this can be expressed with max_by_key. Valid simplification, but a style preference, not a defect — the current code is correct (the name-match test at apps.rs:300-310 passes). The codebase already uses max_by_key elsewhere (rollup.rs:268,631,757), so the idiom is established.
confirmed.fix: Build candidates as (score, size, path) and pick with .max_by_key(|(score, size, _)| (*score, *size)).map(|(_, _, p)| p), removing the None => true special case. Verify clippy/fmt stay green and the best_icns_prefers_name_match_over_largest test still passes.
confirmed.evidence: apps.rs:158-185 manual fold; established max_by_key idiom at rollup.rs:268/631/757. Test apps.rs:300-310 pins the tie-break behavior.

confirmed.title: APP_ICON_HINTS includes "icon", which can mis-prefer a document icon
confirmed.detail: Partially verified, downgraded. APP_ICON_HINTS = ["appicon", "app", "icon", "electron"] (apps.rs:32) adds +2 in best_icns (apps.rs:174). 'icon' is indeed a common document-icon stem, so a bundle with a doc icon literally named icon.icns could win +2 over a non-name-matching real app icon. But this is a deliberate dogfood-tuned heuristic (D43: best_icns picks 'a conventional app-icon name, else the largest'); name-match (+3) still beats it, and the report itself marks this low confidence. This is a speculative tuning concern, not a demonstrable bug, so info not warning.
confirmed.fix: Optional: drop "icon" from the hint list (keep appicon/app/electron which are app-specific), or leave as-is — it is a tunable heuristic per D43. If changed, re-run the real_machine_icons ignored test on macOS to confirm icon coverage doesn't regress.
confirmed.evidence: apps.rs:32 hint list, apps.rs:174 +2 scoring; D43 (decisions.md:176) documents best_icns as a deliberate heuristic. No reproducing test case; finding self-rated low confidence.

confirmed.title: Trailing comments restate the obvious control flow
confirmed.detail: Verified. apps.rs:144 `return None; // sips absent (non-macOS) or unreadable icns`, apps.rs:106 `return; // absent / unreadable dir (e.g. on CI Linux) — skip, not fatal.`, and apps.rs:113 `continue; // already have an app by this name` restate what the let-else / seen.insert guards already say. The CI-Linux degradation note appears multiple times across the file (apps.rs:9-10, 68, 106, 144). Info-level.
confirmed.fix: Drop the trailing comments at apps.rs:106, 113, 144 — the `let Ok(..) else { return }` and `seen.insert(...)` guards are self-documenting. State the degrade-to-None invariant once, in the trimmed module header.
confirmed.evidence: apps.rs:106,113,144 trailing comments vs the self-evident guards at apps.rs:105,112; repeated CI-Linux note at apps.rs:9-10,68.

confirmed.title: b64 comment narrates the no-dependency (D43) decision and asserts 'no panics'
confirmed.detail: Verified. apps.rs:206-207 narrates 'hand-rolled to avoid a dependency (the project keeps its supply chain auditable, hard rule 1)... no panics.' — the why-no-crate is a D43 decision and 'no panics' is an assertion, not info. Per the verification brief the hand-rolled base64 is a deliberate D43 choice (do NOT suggest a crate); I confirmed the implementation is correct against RFC 4648 vectors (the b64_matches_known_vectors test at apps.rs:243-250 covers "","f","fo","foo","foob","foobar" — all canonical). Only the comment is bloat.
confirmed.fix: Reduce the doc to /// Standard base64 (RFC 4648). Leave the implementation untouched — it is correct and the no-crate rationale lives in D43. Do NOT replace with a crate.
confirmed.evidence: apps.rs:206-207 comment; D43 (decisions.md:176) records the hand-rolled-base64 / no-new-dep decision; correctness confirmed by RFC-4648 test vectors at apps.rs:243-250.

confirmed.title: extract_icon: the named `ok` bool then immediate branch reads slightly roundabout
confirmed.detail: Verified, minor. apps.rs:135-145 computes `let ok = Command...map(|o| o.status.success()).unwrap_or(false); if !ok { return None; }`. The named ok adds little; a direct guard reads more cleanly. The current form is fully correct and acceptable — this is a low-value readability preference, so info.
confirmed.fix: Optional: collapse to a guard, e.g. `if !Command::new("sips").args([...]).arg(&icns).arg("--out").arg(&png).output().is_ok_and(|o| o.status.success()) { return None; }`. Keep within the `if !is_cached_fresh` block. Ensure clippy (it may suggest is_ok_and) and fmt stay green.
confirmed.evidence: apps.rs:134-146 the let ok = ...; if !ok { return None; } pattern.

confirmed.title: Watcher-status health threshold duplicates capture::ERROR_THRESHOLD as a bare literal
confirmed.detail: Confirmed at lib.rs:248: get_watcher_status returns `healthy: errors < 6` with a bare literal. capture/mod.rs:59 defines `const ERROR_THRESHOLD: u64 = 6` (private), used at the warn site capture/mod.rs:75 `if count == ERROR_THRESHOLD`. The values agree today but are two independent copies of the same tuning boundary; retuning the const would silently leave the UI health flip on the stale `6`. Real single-source-of-truth issue, low severity (the warning is informational, not a correctness path). This is the SAME finding as commands-s0 (the two reviewers flagged identical lines).
confirmed.fix: Make the constant the single source: change capture/mod.rs:59 to `pub const ERROR_THRESHOLD: u64 = 6;` and rewrite lib.rs:248 as `healthy: errors < capture::ERROR_THRESHOLD,`. The const is already module-scoped under `capture`; making it pub is safe and clippy/fmt-clean. Won't break gates.
confirmed.evidence: lib.rs:248 `healthy: errors < 6`; capture/mod.rs:59 `const ERROR_THRESHOLD: u64 = 6` (not pub); capture/mod.rs:75 `if count == ERROR_THRESHOLD`. Not a documented deliberate choice in decisions context.

confirmed.title: get_watcher_status hardcodes `errors < 6`; capture already names ERROR_THRESHOLD = 6
confirmed.detail: Confirmed — this is the identical issue to commands-r0. The literal `6` is at lib.rs:248 (inside get_watcher_status which begins at line 244); ERROR_THRESHOLD = 6 is named at capture/mod.rs:59. Two copies of the same threshold can drift. Reviewer over-stated severity as warning; it is informational — the watcher-status health flag is a UI badge, not a data-path correctness invariant, and the two values are co-located in a small subsystem. Downgrade in spirit but kept confirmed as the same real duplication. Treat as one fix shared with commands-r0.
confirmed.fix: Make capture/mod.rs:59 `pub const ERROR_THRESHOLD: u64 = 6;` and reference it at lib.rs:248: `healthy: errors < capture::ERROR_THRESHOLD`. Delete the literal `6`. Safe; passes fmt/clippy/test.
confirmed.evidence: Same evidence as commands-r0: lib.rs:248 literal `6`; capture/mod.rs:59 named const. Reviewer's 'warning' severity is too high for a non-correctness UI badge; set to info.

confirmed.title: Module-doc block re-narrates `pub` rationale + decision IDs for every mod
confirmed.detail: Confirmed the comments exist: lib.rs:6-29 attach 2-4 line paragraphs to each mod, and the 'why pub (dead_code under -D warnings)' rationale is repeated for db (6-8), capture (10-11), and ai (26-29), with D30/D34/D48 citations. The `pub`-for-dead_code rationale is genuinely useful context for a reader hitting an otherwise-surprising `pub`. Downgrade to info; not actionable as a bug.

confirmed.title: Identical categories+projects HashMap-building block copied across get_day/get_week/get_timeline
confirmed.detail: Confirmed via grep: the exact two-block read-model setup (`db::get_category_metas(&conn)?...map(|(id,slug,name)| (id, CategoryMeta{slug,name})).collect()` then `db::get_projects(&conn)?...map(|p|(p.id,p.display_name)).collect()`) appears verbatim at get_day (95-102), get_week (123-130), and get_timeline (157-164).
