confirmed.title: Startup cleanup uses println!/eprintln! with `[Startup]` prefixes — ad-hoc logging
confirmed.detail: Confirmed: lib.rs uses println!/eprintln! with hand-prefixed tags at 421 ([Startup]), 423 ([Startup]), 429 ([Startup]), and 448 ([Fatal]). Grep shows this `[Prefix]` println convention is pervasive across the whole codebase (capture/mod.rs:76,241,347,360,363,375,378,383; capture/macos/mod.rs:68,102; polling.rs:34,65; db.rs:170,456; migrations.rs:116). So it is a consistent established convention, not an isolated AI-smell, and adopting a logging facade is a project-wide decision out of scope for this subsystem. Downgrade to info/consistency note; no action this PR.
confirmed.fix: Leave as-is. If a logging facade (`log`/`tracing`) is ever wanted it is a project-wide change, not a command-handler fix. The `[...]` prefix convention is already consistent across files.
confirmed.evidence: grep across src-tauri/src/ shows ~20 println!/eprintln! sites all using the same `[Tag]` prefix convention — confirms it's intentional house style, not localized smell.

confirmed.title: Startup retention-cleanup nesting is 5 levels deep (match → Ok → if let → if let → match)
confirmed.detail: Confirmed nesting at lib.rs:415-430: `match db_conn.lock()` → `Ok(conn)` → `if let Ok(Some(days_str))` → `if let Ok(days)` → `match db::cleanup_old_data`. That is deep. But this is the one-time startup setup closure where early-return is awkward (it returns `Ok(())` for tauri setup), and the logic is correct and readable enough. A flatten (extract `fn run_startup_cleanup(conn:&Connection)` using let-else) is a reasonable nicety but not required. Downgrade to info — readability nit, no defect.
confirmed.fix: Optional: extract `fn run_startup_cleanup(conn:&Connection)` that uses `let Ok(Some(s)) = db::get_setting(...) else { return; }` / `let Ok(days) = s.parse() else { return; }` to cut arrow depth. Keep the outer lock-poison match in setup. Pure refactor; clippy/fmt-clean.
confirmed.evidence: lib.rs:415-430 confirmed 5-deep. Self-contained startup closure; correctness fine. Reviewer's own fix admits the let-else-in-setup is 'awkward'.

confirmed.title: Inline @ts-nocheck explanation duplicates what the header string already says
confirmed.detail: Confirmed: lib.rs:466-468 is a 3-line code comment explaining why `@ts-nocheck` is in the emitted header, and the header literal on line 470 already begins `// @ts-nocheck`. There is some redundancy. However the comment explains the *reason* (the generated file trips strict noUnusedLocals), which the header string itself does not state, so it is not pure duplication. Per the verification context, the @ts-nocheck choice is deliberate (D27) — but documenting *why* at the emit site is reasonable. Minor; downgrade to info, optional trim.
confirmed.fix: Optional: shorten to one line at the .header() call, e.g. `// @ts-nocheck: generated boilerplate trips strict noUnusedLocals (D27)`. Do NOT touch src/bindings.ts (generated). No gate impact.
confirmed.evidence: lib.rs:466-468 comment + 470 header literal confirmed. The comment carries the noUnusedLocals reason absent from the header string, so it's not fully redundant.

confirmed.title: Capture-wiring setup comments restate trait docs + decision rationale
confirmed.detail: Confirmed: lib.rs:434-442 carry comments about main-thread registration (D29), consumer-drains-on-its-own-thread (R57), and consumer-is-sole-writer. The CaptureSource trait doc (capture/mod.rs:48-55) and the module doc (capture/mod.rs:1-8) do already state the main-thread/CFRunLoop and single-writer contracts, so there is duplication. But these are the load-bearing concurrency invariants of the app (why capture runs on the main thread and why the writer is on a dedicated thread, tied to !Send keep-alives per D33 and the deferred WAL writer per R57) — repeating them at the wiring site where the threading actually happens is defensible. Subjective; downgrade to info.
confirmed.fix: Optional: collapse to one line, e.g. `// source registers on the main run loop (D29); consumer is the sole DB writer on its own thread (R57).` Keep at least one citation since the threading model is subtle. No gate impact.
confirmed.evidence: lib.rs:434-442 confirmed; capture/mod.rs:1-8 and 48-55 already document the same contracts. Concurrency-critical site, so some restatement is justified — not a defect.

confirmed.title: useDayData has no out-of-order / stale-response guard — fast day navigation can render the wrong day
confirmed.detail: Confirmed. fetchDay (L22-31) awaits getDay then unconditionally setData (L25); the effect (L33-39) re-runs on [fetchDay, live] (fetchDay is keyed on [startUnix,endUnix]) and a 30s setInterval (L37) also fires it. The effect cleanup only clears the interval (L38), nothing cancels/ignores the in-flight promise. So if the range changes (or a poll for day A resolves) after navigating to day B, A's response lands last and overwrites B. The pattern guard already exists elsewhere in the subsystem: useInstalledApps uses an `alive` flag (useInstalledApps.ts L34-40) and DayView's watcher effect uses `cancelled` (DayView.tsx L42-48) — the three data hooks omit it. Real, low-frequency, visible (header/data desync).
confirmed.fix: Capture a per-run flag in the effect: `let alive = true;` and have fetchDay only setData/setError when alive; set alive=false in cleanup (alongside clearInterval). Best folded into a shared useRangeData (see fe-views-s1) so all three hooks are fixed once. Cosmetic stale data only, no panic/SQL/IPC impact.
confirmed.evidence: useDayData.ts L22-39: setData(await getDay(...)) with cleanup that only clearInterval. Contrast useInstalledApps.ts L34-40 (alive flag) and DayView.tsx L42-48 (cancelled flag) — the guard pattern exists but is absent here.

confirmed.title: useTimelineData shares the same stale-response race as useDayData
confirmed.detail: Confirmed, identical shape. fetchTimeline (L25-34) awaits getTimeline and unconditionally setData (L28); effect (L36-42) re-runs on range change + 30s poll (L40); cleanup only clearInterval (L41). No cancelled/alive guard, so a slow timeline response for a prior day can land after navigation and render under the new header.
confirmed.fix: Mirror the useDayData fix: per-run alive flag gating setData/setError, set false in cleanup. Or extract the shared useRangeData hook (fe-views-s1) to fix all three.
confirmed.evidence: useTimelineData.ts L25-42 — same setData(await ...) + clearInterval-only cleanup as useDayData.

confirmed.title: useWeekData shares the same stale-response race
confirmed.detail: Confirmed. fetchWeek (L23-33) does setData(await getWeek(starts, weekEnd)) (L27) with no guard; effect (L35-41) re-runs on [fetchWeek, live] + 30s poll (L39); cleanup only clearInterval (L40). A slow response for the previous week can land after the user moves week and desync the 7 mini-dials/aggregates from the WeekNav title.
confirmed.fix: Add the same per-run alive guard before setData/setError, set false in cleanup. Or consolidate into useRangeData (fe-views-s1).
confirmed.evidence: useWeekData.ts L23-41 — same pattern; cleanup only clears the interval.

confirmed.title: deepestLabel indexes days[] with backend-provided index without a range check
confirmed.detail: Confirmed as a defensive-info finding. WeekView.tsx L42-45: `data?.deepest_day != null ? days[data.deepest_day].toLocaleDateString(...) : '—'`. bindings.ts L412 types deepest_day as `number | null`, so a non-null but out-of-0..6 value would make days[idx] undefined and throw on .toLocaleDateString. The day slices just below (L96 `const slice = data.days[i]` then `slice?.runs`) already defend against frontend/backend count divergence, so the deepest-day lookup is inconsistent. In practice the backend builds exactly 7, so this is a low-probability guard, not a live bug — info is right.
confirmed.fix: Guard the lookup: `data.deepest_day != null && days[data.deepest_day] ? days[data.deepest_day].toLocaleDateString(undefined,{weekday:'short'}) : '—'`.
confirmed.evidence: WeekView.tsx L42-45 unguarded index vs L96/L127 slice?. guards; bindings.ts L412 `deepest_day: number | null`.

confirmed.title: Capture-health banner only re-checks on day change, not on the 30s poll
confirmed.detail: Confirmed. The getWatcherStatus effect is keyed on [start] (DayView.tsx L49; WeekView.tsx L40 on [dayStarts]; TimelineView.tsx L35 on [start]) — it fires once per navigation but is not part of the 30s data poll (which lives in the useXData hooks). So if capture starts erroring while the user stays on today, captureHealthy stays true and DegradedBanner never appears until they navigate. Real gap, but a degraded-banner latency rather than a correctness/data bug — info.
confirmed.fix: Fold watcher status into the same 30s refresh — either call getWatcherStatus inside the data interval (via a useCaptureHealth hook, see fe-views-s3), or add `healthy` to the day/week/timeline payload so it updates every 30s. Low-risk; touches no SQL/IPC shape if done frontend-side.
confirmed.evidence: DayView.tsx L41-49 keyed [start]; WeekView.tsx L32-40 keyed [dayStarts]; TimelineView.tsx L27-35 keyed [start]; the 30s poll is in the hooks, not these effects.

confirmed.title: Pointless join→split round-trip of dayStarts to make a stable dep key
confirmed.detail: Confirmed. useWeekData.ts L21 `const key = dayStarts.join(',')` then L26 inside fetchWeek `const starts = key.split(',').map(Number)`. The stringify/parse exists only to give useCallback a primitive dep (L33 deps [key, weekEnd]). WeekView already memoizes dayStarts (WeekView.tsx L24 useMemo), so the array identity is stable per week. The number→string→number round-trip is brittle and obscure for no benefit.
confirmed.fix: Keep `key` in the dep list for a stable primitive but call getWeek with the array directly: `setData(await getWeek(dayStarts, weekEnd))` and drop the split. Or, since WeekView memoizes the array, depend on `[dayStarts, weekEnd]` and delete `key` entirely. Verify tsc/vitest still pass (pure refactor).
confirmed.evidence: useWeekData.ts L21 + L26 + L33; WeekView.tsx L24 memoizes dayStarts.

confirmed.title: useDayData / useTimelineData are byte-for-byte identical except the fetch fn and error string
confirmed.detail: Confirmed. useDayData.ts and useTimelineData.ts are structurally identical: same DayDataState/TimelineDataState shape (data/loading/error/refresh), same (start,end,live) signature, same fetchX keyed on [startUnix,endUnix], same effect (setLoading(true)+void fetch+30s interval, cleanup clearInterval), same return object. Only the imported getDay/getTimeline and the fallback string ('Couldn't load this day.' vs 'Couldn't load this timeline.') differ. useWeekData is the same modulo its array key. Three near-identical files means each fix (e.g. the race in r0/r1/r2) must be made three times.
confirmed.fix: Extract a generic `useRangeData<T>(fetcher, deps, live, fallbackMsg)` and make the three hooks one-line wrappers; fold the stale-response guard (r0-r2) into it so all three are fixed at once. Pure-logic refactor — keep vitest coverage green.
confirmed.evidence: Direct read of useDayData.ts (L17-42), useTimelineData.ts (L16-45), useWeekData.ts (L17-44) — same structure, differing only in fetcher + fallback string + week's key.

confirmed.title: No stale-response guard: out-of-order fetches can clobber the current range
confirmed.detail: Confirmed as real but DUPLICATE of fe-views-r0/r1/r2, which already capture this exact race across the three hooks at warning severity from the rules lane. Downgraded to info to avoid double-counting the same defect; track and fix it under r0-r2 (ideally folded into the shared useRangeData of fe-views-s1). The note that DayView's getWatcherStatus effect uses a `cancelled` flag while these hooks don't is accurate (DayView.tsx L42-48).
confirmed.fix: Resolved by the r0/r1/r2 fix: per-run alive flag gating setData/setError, set false in cleanup — best inside the extracted useRangeData (fe-views-s1). No separate change needed.
confirmed.evidence: Same lines as fe-views-r0 (useDayData.ts L22-39); this is the simplify-lane restatement of the rules-lane r0-r2 race.

confirmed.title: Identical getWatcherStatus capture-health effect copy-pasted across all three views
confirmed.detail: Confirmed. The same effect — getWatcherStatus().then(s => !cancelled && setCaptureHealthy(s.healthy)).catch(()=>undefined) with a cancelled flag — plus the same useState(true) and the same <DegradedBanner title="Tracking hit a snag" description="UsageOS ran into repeated errors while recording. Your existing data is safe." actionLabel="Retry" onAction={refresh}/> block appears verbatim in DayView (effect L41-49, banner L70-79), WeekView (effect L32-40, banner L57-66), and TimelineView (effect L27-35, banner L41-50). Three copies of identical logic + user-facing copy.
confirmed.fix: Extract a `useCaptureHealth(rangeKey)` hook returning `healthy` and a `<CaptureDegradedBanner onRetry={refresh}/>` carrying the shared copy; each view drops ~10 lines of effect + ~10 of JSX. Doing this is also the natural place to fix fe-views-r4 (poll health on the 30s interval). Pure-frontend; keep tsc/vitest green.
confirmed.evidence: DayView.tsx L41-49/L70-79, WeekView.tsx L32-40/L57-66, TimelineView.tsx L27-35/L41-50 — identical effect + banner copy.

confirmed.title: ErrorState component is the exact same code in DayView, WeekView, and TimelineView
confirmed.detail:
Confirmed verbatim. ErrorState({message,onRetry}) — dashed-border wrapper, muted message

, 'Try again' button with identical Tailwind classes — is defined three times: DayView.tsx L261-274, WeekView.tsx L205-218, TimelineView.tsx L211-224. Identical down to class strings and copy. A token/microcopy tweak must be made in three places.
confirmed.fix: Promote to src/components/ui/ErrorState.tsx, import in all three views, delete the local copies. Straightforward; keep tsc/vitest green.
confirmed.evidence: DayView.tsx L261-274, WeekView.tsx L205-218, TimelineView.tsx L211-224 — byte-identical function bodies.

confirmed.title: DayNav and TimelineNav are identical day-stepper navs
confirmed.detail: Confirmed. DayView's DayNav (L146-193) and TimelineView's TimelineNav (L141-188) are the same component: same Today/long-date label logic (L157-159 vs L152-154), same refresh/prev/next buttons with identical aria-labels, titles, classes, and addDays(date,±1). Only the function name differs. WeekNav (WeekView.tsx L143-189) is the same pattern with ±7 and week labels. Real duplication; kept at info since the reviewer rated it medium and it is presentational with a small per-view divergence (week step/labels).
confirmed.fix: Extract a shared <DayStepperNav> (parameterize the step + label builder so the week case reuses it) into components/, used by DayView, TimelineView, and optionally WeekView. Removes ~45 duplicated lines. Pure-frontend refactor.
confirmed.evidence: DayView.tsx L146-193 vs TimelineView.tsx L141-188 (identical save the name) and WeekView.tsx L143-189 (same pattern, ±7).

confirmed.title: Ledger onMouseLeave calls onHover(isolated) — passes the live value back, so hover-dim sticks when nothing is pinned
confirmed.detail: Confirmed bug. In Ledger (DayView.tsx L195-231), onHover IS setIsolated (passed L137) and there is a single `isolated` state used for BOTH click-pin (Chip toggleIsolate L64/L125, LedgerRow onClick L225) and hover. onMouseEnter (L223) calls onHover(row.slug) → setIsolated(row.slug), which re-renders Ledger; the new render's onMouseLeave closure (L224) now captures isolated === row.slug, so leaving calls onHover(row.slug) — a no-op. Result: with nothing pinned, hovering a row dims the others and leaving does NOT clear it (the dim sticks). The intent (restore prior state on leave) fails because there is no separate pinned-vs-hovered state to restore to.
confirmed.fix: Separate pinned from transient hover. Simplest: track a `pinned` state (set by click/Chip) and a `hovered` state (set by enter, cleared to null on leave); compute the active isolation as `hovered ?? pinned`. Then onMouseLeave={() => setHovered(null)} correctly clears transient hover while preserving a pin. Logic change — verify the Chip active-state (L124 `active={isolated === c.slug}`) and Dial isolatedSlug (L96) read the combined value.
confirmed.evidence: DayView.tsx L31 single `isolated` state; L137 onHover={setIsolated}; L223 onMouseEnter sets row.slug; L224 onMouseLeave={() => onHover(isolated)} reads the just-updated value → no-op; LedgerRow.tsx L33-34 wires these straight through.

confirmed.title: Leftover 'ctx' naming from the Context→Category rename (D46)
confirmed.detail: Confirmed and squarely against a documented intent. SettingsView.tsx uses `ctx` as the map variable (L157), the CategoryRow prop `ctx: Category` (L280/L284), `ctx.id/ctx.slug/ctx.color/ctx.name` (L288-317), and `ctxRules` (L289, L300, L303). D46 explicitly renamed Context→Category EVERYWHERE — 'UI copy, IPC commands/types, Rust internals, frontend identifiers, and component/file names' — to heal the naming split; `ctx` against a `Category` type is exactly the half-renamed leftover the rename was meant to eliminate. Not a deliberate-keep (the SQL `category_id` legacy name is the only intentionally-retained one; this is a local identifier, fair game).
confirmed.fix: Rename `ctx`→`category` (or `cat`) and `ctxRules`→`categoryRules` in the map callback (L157-163) and CategoryRow (L279-317): e.g. `categories.map((category) => <CategoryRow key={category.id} category={category} rules={rules} onEdit={() => setEditing({category})}/>)`. Pure rename; keep tsc green. Not auto-fixable (identifier rename, not a mechanical fmt/dead-import fix).
confirmed.evidence: grep: SettingsView.tsx L157 map(ctx), L160 ctx={ctx}, L280/284 prop ctx:Category, L288-317 ctx.* + L289/300/303 ctxRules. decisions.md D46 mandates the full rename incl. frontend identifiers.

confirmed.title: Doc comment narrates decision history ('like the old dashboard did')
confirmed.detail: Confirmed minor. useDayData.ts L12-16 ends 'it polls every 30s like the old dashboard did.' — 'like the old dashboard did' documents a superseded design, not current behavior. useTimelineData.ts L12-15 and useWeekData.ts L12-16 also re-explain 'Mirrors useDayData', which becomes redundant once they share a base hook (fe-views-s1).
confirmed.fix: Trim to behavior only: 'Polls every 30s while `live` (today); shows the loading state only on range change, not on background refresh.' Drop 'like the old dashboard did'. If the three hooks are consolidated (fe-views-s1) the 'Mirrors useDayData' lines disappear with them.
confirmed.evidence: useDayData.ts L15 'like the old dashboard did'; useTimelineData.ts L13 / useWeekData.ts L13 'Mirrors useDayData'.

confirmed.title: Comment re-narrates a design rationale on topCategory
confirmed.detail: Real but trimmed-scope. DayView.tsx L55-57 comments topCategory: the first clause ('`categories` is sorted longest-first by the rollup, so [0] is the headline') is a fair load-bearing invariant and should stay; only the trailing justification ('Featuring whatever actually led (vs. a fixed Deep work that's often ~0) keeps the stat honest instead of looking broken') is decision-archaeology. Downgraded the reviewer's framing — keep the invariant, drop only the justification. Low impact.
confirmed.fix: Keep one line: '// `categories` is sorted longest-first by the rollup, so [0] is the day's leading category.' Drop the 'vs a fixed Deep work / looking broken' tail.
confirmed.evidence: DayView.tsx L55-58 — invariant clause + justification clause; only the latter is archaeology.

confirmed.title: Retention-options branch duplicates the same RETENTION.map to append a custom value
confirmed.detail: Confirmed but minor. SettingsView.tsx L85-90: both branches of the ternary call `RETENTION.map(([value,label]) => ({value,label}))`, differing only by appending the custom value when currentRetention isn't a preset. The map is written twice. Reviewer marked warning; downgraded to info — it's a small readability nit on a rare 'custom retention from an older build' path, not a correctness or altitude problem.
confirmed.fix: Compute the base once, append conditionally: `const opts = RETENTION.map(([value,label]) => ({value,label})); const retentionOptions = RETENTION.some(([v]) => v === currentRetention) ? opts : [...opts, {value: currentRetention, label: `${currentRetention} days`}];`
confirmed.evidence: SettingsView.tsx L85-90 — RETENTION.map appears on both L86 and L88.

confirmed.title: map→reverse→map to render runs newest-first is roundabout
confirmed.detail: Confirmed as a real but low-value style nit. TimelineView.tsx L67-79: `data.runs.map((run,i) => ({run,i})).reverse().map(({run,i}) => ...)` allocates an index-tagged array, reverses it, then maps — to iterate runs newest-first while keeping the original index for the gap calc (L72 `run.start - data.runs[i-1].end`). It works and is correct; the intermediate {run,i} objects are avoidable. Already info; reviewer confidence low. Keeping as info — optional cleanup, no behavior change and the proposed alternatives aren't clearly more readable.
confirmed.fix: Optional: iterate descending indices once — `Array.from({length: data.runs.length}, (_, k) => data.runs.length-1-k).map((i) => { const run = data.runs[i]; const gap = i > 0 ? run.start - data.runs[i-1].end : 0; ... })`. Or keep chronological order and flip with CSS column-reverse. Low priority.
confirmed.evidence: TimelineView.tsx L67-79 — map/reverse/map chain with {run,i} tuples.

confirmed.title: Module-level cache + inflight + useState mirror for a session catalog
confirmed.detail: Partly confirmed, scope-reduced. useInstalledApps.ts has a module `cache` (L7), an `inflight` promise singleton (L8-23), and useState(cache ?? []) with an `alive` flag plus an early `if (cache) { setApps(cache); return; }` (L28-41). The inflight dedupe is DEFENSIBLE — there are two callers (CategoryEditorModal.tsx L66, ExclusionModal.tsx L22) and both can plausibly mount, so dropping it is not clearly safe; reject that part. The one fair micro-point is that the early `if (cache)` return (L30-33) is largely covered by the `useState(cache ?? [])` initializer. Low confidence, info.
confirmed.fix: Optional only: the early `if (cache)` return (L30-33) is redundant with the `useState(cache ?? [])` seed and could be removed, keeping the module promise-singleton + inflight (the dedupe earns its keep with two callers). Do NOT drop inflight. Verify vitest if touched.
confirmed.evidence: useInstalledApps.ts L7-41; two callers (CategoryEditorModal.tsx L66, ExclusionModal.tsx L22) justify the inflight dedupe.

confirmed.title: AWAY_MIN_SECS comment cites a backend constant + decision id as cross-ref archaeology
confirmed.detail: Confirmed minor. TimelineView.tsx L12-13: 'Matches the backend's run-split threshold (rollup IDLE_GAP_ENDS_RUN_SECS) — D34a dogfood-tunable.' The load-bearing part is the invariant (this must stay in sync with the backend split threshold); the 'D34a dogfood-tunable' tail is decision-archaeology a reader doesn't need inline. Note IDLE_GAP_ENDS_RUN_SECS is itself a documented dogfood-tunable constant (per the brief), so the *constant* is fine — only the comment tail is the nit. Low confidence/info.
confirmed.fix: Trim to the invariant: '// Gaps at least this long get an Away marker; keep in sync with the backend run-split threshold (rollup IDLE_GAP_ENDS_RUN_SECS).' Drop the 'D34a dogfood-tunable' tail.
confirmed.evidence: TimelineView.tsx L12-14 comment + AWAY_MIN_SECS const.

confirmed.title: Hardcoded rgba() bar-muted color bypasses the token system
confirmed.detail: Confirmed at line 5: `const BAR_MUTED = "rgba(255,255,255,0.65)"`, applied at line 49 `style={{ color: BAR_MUTED }}` on the TrackingStatus text. Verified by grep it is the ONLY rgba(...) literal in the entire src tree. tokens.css defines `--bar-bg`/`--bar-fg` per theme (paper/warm/black blocks) but no `--bar-muted`. So this component invents a muted-on-dark color inline, outside the token vocabulary. It reads acceptably today (the bar is dark in all three themes) but will drift if the bar palette ever changes per-theme. Note D42 documents that Tailwind opacity modifiers do NOT work on the hex `var()` tokens (text-bar-fg/60 produced an invalid color and made the indicator invisible) — so the simplest `text-bar-fg opacity-XX` is not a drop-in; the proper fix is a real `--bar-muted` token per theme.
confirmed.fix: Add a `--bar-muted` token to each [data-theme] block in src/styles/tokens.css (a muted-on-dark value per theme) and replace `style={{ color: BAR_MUTED }}` with `style={{ color: 'var(--bar-muted)' }}`, deleting the literal. Do NOT use a Tailwind /opacity modifier on a bar-fg token — D42 shows those don't resolve on the hex var() tokens.
confirmed.evidence: TitleBar.tsx:5,49; grep: sole rgba() in src; tokens.css has --bar-fg/--bar-bg but no --bar-muted; D42 notes opacity modifiers fail on hex var() tokens.

confirmed.title: Expanded header labels segment count as "switches", contradicting countSwitches
confirmed.detail: Confirmed. Line 84: `{run.segments.length} app {run.segments.length === 1 ? "switch" : "switches"}` labels the raw segment count as "switches". Lines 53-54 in the collapsed view show `{countSwitches(run.segments)} switches`, and countSwitches (lines 140-148) counts adjacent app/project transitions (for N segments with all-distinct apps that is N-1). So the same run shows different numbers for the word "switch" in the two surfaces (e.g. 3 segments → collapsed "2 switches", expanded "3 app switches"), and the expanded label is semantically off (N segments are N app visits, not N switches). This is the same issue as fe-components-s0/s1.
confirmed.fix: Relabel the expanded header to what it counts, e.g. `{run.segments.length} app {run.segments.length === 1 ? "visit" : "visits"}` (or "session(s)"), OR show `countSwitches(run.segments)` to match the collapsed line. Pick one definition of "switch" and use it in both places.
confirmed.evidence: TimelineRow.tsx:84 uses run.segments.length labeled "switches"; :53-54 uses countSwitches; :140-148 countSwitches counts transitions, not segments.

confirmed.title: Modal does not trap focus or restore focus on close
confirmed.detail: Confirmed. The open effect (lines 21-30) adds Esc-to-close and calls `dialogRef.current?.focus()` (line 28), but never (a) traps Tab inside the dialog nor (b) captures/restores the previously-focused element on close. The container sets `role="dialog" aria-modal="true"` (lines 43-45), which tells assistive tech focus is contained when it is not — Tab can leave the dialog into the page behind the dimmed overlay, and on close focus falls to <body> instead of the trigger. The Modal is the shared shell for CategoryEditorModal/ExclusionModal/DeleteAllModal, so this affects all settings dialogs. This is a real a11y gap, not a documented deliberate choice. Scope: focus-restore is a small, safe addition; full focus-trap is larger.
confirmed.fix: In the open effect, capture `const prev = document.activeElement as HTMLElement | null` before `dialogRef.current?.focus()`, and in the cleanup call `prev?.focus()`. Add a keydown handler intercepting Tab/Shift+Tab to cycle among the dialog's focusable descendants (focus-trap), or adopt a small headless dialog primitive. At minimum restore focus on close.
confirmed.evidence: Modal.tsx:21-30 (focus on open, no restore), :43-45 (aria-modal=true), no Tab handling anywhere in the file.

confirmed.title: Custom listbox has no keyboard navigation
confirmed.detail: Confirmed. The trigger advertises `aria-haspopup="listbox"`/`aria-expanded` (lines 52-53) and the popover is `role="listbox"` (line 64) with `role="option"` children (line 71). The only keyboard handler is Esc-closes (lines 37-39). There is no ArrowUp/ArrowDown to move an active option, no Enter/Space to commit, no Home/End, and no `aria-activedescendant`; options are operable only by mouse click (line 74 onClick). This diverges from RadioGroup (role=radio buttons, focusable) in the same UI folder. It is a genuine keyboard/SR gap for a control presenting as a combobox. Not a documented deliberate choice.
confirmed.fix: Handle ArrowDown/ArrowUp to move an active index, Enter/Space to commit, Home/End to jump, and reflect the active option via `aria-activedescendant` pointing at the focused option's id. Move keyboard focus into the menu on open or keep it on the trigger and drive selection with aria-activedescendant. Keep Esc-closes.
confirmed.evidence: Select.tsx:52-53,61-64,71,74 (mouse-only options, listbox roles); :37-39 (only Esc handled); RadioGroup.tsx is keyboard-operable by contrast.

confirmed.title: Expand header switch count contradicts the collapsed summary count
confirmed.detail: Confirmed and is the same defect as fe-components-r1 (kept separate per instruction to adjudicate each id). Collapsed (line 53) shows `countSwitches(run.segments)` (transitions, gated >1); expanded header (lines 83-84) shows `run.segments.length` labeled "switch/switches". segments.length is the segment count, not the switch count, so the two figures disagree for the same run. One of them mislabels its quantity.
confirmed.fix: Pick one definition. If the header means app sessions, reword to `{run.segments.length} {run.segments.length === 1 ? "app session" : "app sessions"}`. If it means switches, use countSwitches(run.segments) like the collapsed row. Don't label a segment count as a switch count.
confirmed.evidence: TimelineRow.tsx:83-84 (segments.length as "switches"), :53 (countSwitches), :140-148 (transition count).

confirmed.title: countSwitches and the segment-count header compute overlapping concepts inconsistently
confirmed.detail: Confirmed as the duplication/consistency facet of the same bug (r1/s0). countSwitches (lines 140-148) computes transition counts while the expand header independently uses run.segments.length as a 'switch' count — two ad-hoc notions of 'switch' in one file that will keep drifting. Downgraded to info because it is the same underlying issue as r1/s0 (which carry the warning); fixing those resolves this.
confirmed.fix: Define a single helper/value for the count you actually want to show and use it in both the collapsed and expanded views so the number is consistent.
confirmed.evidence: TimelineRow.tsx:140-148 vs :84 — two definitions of 'switch' in one file.

confirmed.title: projectLine duplicates summarizeRun's project-split logic
confirmed.detail: Confirmed. projectLine (lines 119-132) re-implements the project-split-to-text logic that lib/runs.ts summarizeRun (lines 11-18) already does: filter out NO_PROJECT, map to `name duration`, join. Differences are real and divergent: summarizeRun joins with `" · "` while projectLine uses `"  ·  "` (double-spaced), and projectLine adds a `no project <dur>` branch and single/apps-fallback branches summarizeRun lacks. The Dial uses summarizeRun (Dial.tsx:107); the Timeline rolls its own — two copies of 'how we phrase a project split' that will drift. Note: NO_PROJECT is the string "No project" but projectLine pushes the literal lowercase "no project" (line 127) — a separate small inconsistency. Info severity is right.
confirmed.fix: Extend summarizeRun (or add one shared formatter in lib/runs.ts) to cover the multi-bucket / single-project / apps-fallback / no-project cases, and call it from both Dial and TimelineRow so the separator and phrasing can't diverge.
confirmed.evidence: TimelineRow.tsx:119-132 vs runs.ts:11-18; separators "  ·  " vs " · "; Dial.tsx:107 uses summarizeRun.

confirmed.title: Inline style={{ color: 'var(--fg)' }} instead of the text-fg utility
confirmed.detail: Confirmed at line 47: `style={{ color: "var(--fg)" }}` on the app chip span — verified by grep it is the only inline `color: "var(--fg)"` in src/components. It points at the token (so it is not ad-hoc colour, hard rule 7 is not violated) but it is an inconsistent way to apply foreground colour vs the `text-fg` / `text-fg`-class usage elsewhere. Info severity. Mechanically safe to swap.
confirmed.fix: Replace with className `text-fg` and drop the style prop, matching how other components apply foreground colour.
confirmed.evidence: TimelineRow.tsx:47; grep confirms sole inline var(--fg) color in src/components; text-fg utility used throughout.

confirmed.title: Decision-archaeology comment about absorbed detours (D34a)
confirmed.detail: Confirmed. Lines 87-88: `// An absorbed detour (D34a) carries a different category than the run — mark it with // its own colour dot + label so the expand stays honest about what happened.` The code `const isDetour = seg.category_slug !== run.category_slug` (line 89) is largely self-evident; the D34a rationale belongs in decisions.md. Info; a style/comment-bloat nit, fully subjective. Removing/trimming a comment is mechanically safe but not the kind of trivial autofix the gates verify.
confirmed.fix: Trim to a short note: `// detour: segment categorised differently than its run`. Drop the D34a rationale.
confirmed.evidence: TimelineRow.tsx:87-89.

confirmed.title: generatedBy typed as bare string with a magic 'fm' literal
confirmed.detail: Confirmed. RecapCard props: `generatedBy: string` (line 6) and `const isFm = generatedBy === "fm"` (line 11). Only two states exist ('fm' / 'template'). A union type gives compile-time safety and documents the contract — worthwhile since the AI seam (D49) is dormant and easy to get wrong when wired. Caveat for the fix: the value originates from the Rust read-model via generated bindings (Recap in bindings.ts); RecapCard's own prop type is hand-authored and may currently be typed as `string` to match the generated field. Narrowing the prop to a union is safe as long as the call site passes a value assignable to it; verify the caller and the bindings field type before changing, and do NOT edit bindings.ts (generated, D27).
confirmed.fix: Type the prop as a union: `generatedBy: "fm" | "template"`. Keep the same boolean check. If the generated Recap field is a bare string, cast/narrow at the call site rather than editing bindings.ts.
confirmed.evidence: RecapCard.tsx:6,11; generatedBy flows from Rust Recap via bindings (D27 forbids editing bindings.ts).

confirmed.title: Glyph prefixes ⌁ and ≡ on the recap badge read as noise
confirmed.detail: Confirmed at line 24: `{isFm ? "⌁ Summarized on-device" : "≡ Template"}`. The ⌁ and ≡ glyphs are decorative unicode not in the design token set; they add no meaning over the words and render unpredictably across fonts. The badge already distinguishes the two states via border/text colour (line 21: border-c-deep/text-c-deep vs border-rule/text-muted), so the glyphs are redundant. Low-confidence/info — a copy-voice judgement consistent with the project's 'no AI-tell ornamentation' note, but ultimately a design call.
confirmed.fix: Drop the glyphs: `Summarized on-device` / `Template`. The coloured-border treatment already marks the two states.
confirmed.evidence: RecapCard.tsx:24 glyphs; :21 colour border already distinguishes the states.

confirmed.title: Over-narrated doc comment on AppIcon
confirmed.detail: Confirmed. Lines 14-17 are a 4-line block comment covering the monogram fallback, the shared icon map, and webview decode behaviour ('the same data-URI src is reused across rows, so the webview decodes one copy regardless of count'). The decode-sharing detail is about loadIconMap/appIcons.ts, not this component. Note this detail is also already recorded in decisions.md (D44) and restated in UncategorizedApps — so it is genuinely redundant here. Info, subjective comment nit.
confirmed.fix: Cut to one line, e.g. `/** App icon resolved by name, with a first-letter monogram fallback. */`. The map-sharing note belongs (if anywhere) in appIcons.ts.
confirmed.evidence: AppIcon.tsx:14-17; the decode-sharing fact is in decisions.md D44 and is about loadIconMap.

confirmed.title: Inline comments restating obvious resolve logic
confirmed.detail: Confirmed. Lines 19-20 ('Resolve synchronously when the map is already loaded (no flash when re-entering a view); otherwise load it and update.') and line 20 ('`undefined` means "not loaded yet".') narrate code that is fairly clear from resolveIcon's return handling (the useState initializer at line 21 and the `if (ready !== undefined)` at line 25). Mild belt-and-suspenders. Low/info — subjective. The 'undefined means not loaded' note has a sliver of value since resolveIcon's tri-state (string|null|undefined) is non-obvious, so trimming rather than fully deleting is safer.
confirmed.fix: Trim the synchronous-resolve sentence; optionally keep a short note that resolveIcon returns undefined when the map isn't loaded yet (its tri-state isn't obvious).
confirmed.evidence: AppIcon.tsx:19-21,25 — resolveIcon tri-state string|null|undefined.

confirmed.title: Inline color-mix background instead of a token
confirmed.detail: Confirmed at line 270: `style={{ background: "color-mix(in srgb, var(--c-research) 8%, transparent)" }}` on the conflict-warning panel — a one-off computed tint. It builds on a token (`var(--c-research)`) so it is not ad-hoc hex and does not break hard rule 7; it is just a bespoke inline value used nowhere else. Grep confirms it is the only color-mix in the subsystem. Info, not warning — minor inconsistency, the 2px red border already carries the warning signal. Note D42 documents that Tailwind /opacity modifiers don't work on the hex var() tokens, so the suggested `bg-c-research/[0.08]` utility would NOT resolve — the inline color-mix is actually the working way to tint a token; keep it or drop the tint, but do not replace with a Tailwind opacity utility.
confirmed.fix: Either drop the tint (the 2px red border already signals the warning) or leave the inline color-mix as-is. Do NOT swap to `bg-c-research/[0.08]` — Tailwind opacity modifiers don't resolve on the hex var() tokens (D42).
confirmed.evidence: CategoryEditorModal.tsx:269-270; grep: sole color-mix in subsystem; D42 (opacity modifiers fail on hex var() tokens).

confirmed.title: Defensive 'Missing category id' throw for an impossible case
confirmed.detail: Confirmed at line 188: `if (id == null) throw new Error("Missing category id");`. `id` is `category?.id` (number|undefined); in the edit branch category is non-null (line 183) and in the create branch id is assigned from createCategory (line 186). So in practice id is set on both paths — the throw guards a near-impossible state. However the throw is NOT pure ceremony: it also narrows the type from `number | undefined` to `number` so the subsequent `createRule(id, ...)` calls (line 189) typecheck. Removing it outright would likely break tsc (a merge gate) unless replaced with another narrowing. Downgraded to info; this is a defensible (if slightly defensive) pattern, not a clear smell.
confirmed.fix: Optional: replace the runtime throw with type narrowing tied to the existing branches (e.g. assign id inside each branch so TS sees it as number), or leave as-is. Do not simply delete the throw — `createRule(id,...)` at line 189 needs id narrowed to number or tsc fails.
confirmed.evidence: CategoryEditorModal.tsx:182-189; id is number|undefined and createRule needs number → the throw also serves as a type-narrowing guard.

confirmed.title: Sequential awaits in for-loops serialize independent rule writes
confirmed.detail: Confirmed at lines 189-191: three sequential `for ... await` loops (createRule for added, deleteRule for removedIds, deleteRule for movedIds) followed by reprocessLogs (line 192). These are independent IPC calls serialized one-at-a-time, so save is slower than necessary. However: (1) rule counts here are tiny (a handful per category edit), so the real-world latency win is negligible; (2) parallelizing writes against the Arc<Mutex<Connection>> backend (D31/D42 interim) gives no DB-level concurrency anyway — they serialize at the mutex regardless; (3) there could be a subtle ordering consideration if createRule and deleteRule on the same pattern interact, though here added/removed/moved are disjoint sets. Downgraded to info — a real but low-value micro-optimization, not a correctness issue.
confirmed.fix: Optional: batch with Promise.all over the three lists, then reprocessLogs once. Verify the backend doesn't require ordering; the DB mutex means the latency win is marginal, so this is low priority.
confirmed.evidence: CategoryEditorModal.tsx:189-192; DB is Arc<Mutex<Connection>> (D31/D42) so writes serialize at the mutex regardless.

confirmed.title: InspectorDetail indirection for a single caller
confirmed.detail: Partially confirmed. DetailInspector takes a pre-shaped InspectorDetail (colorVar/title/subtitle/durationLabel/rangeLabel), a null empty state, and a configurable `emptyHint` with a default (lines 11-21). The component is a thin formatting shell. I could not verify the 'single caller' claim within the reviewed file set (the caller wasn't read), so the over-engineering severity is speculative. The concrete, verifiable nit is only the `emptyHint` prop carrying a default that may have one consumer. Downgraded to info; do not rewrite the component on an unverified caller count.
confirmed.fix: If a grep confirms exactly one caller that always uses the default, inline the empty-hint string and drop the `emptyHint` prop. Otherwise leave as-is — a small presentational component with a typed prop is fine.
confirmed.evidence: DetailInspector.tsx:1-21; caller count not verified in the reviewed files.

confirmed.title: Tag and ModePill primitives may be single-use leftovers
confirmed.detail: Confirmed as dead/unused exports. Tag (lines 90-97) and ModePill (lines 100-112) are exported from primitives.tsx with comments tying them to 'the exclusions list'. Grep across src/components for `\bTag\b` / `\bModePill\b` (excluding primitives.tsx) returns NO matches — neither is imported or rendered anywhere; ExclusionModal builds its own pills inline. So these are speculative primitive extraction / dead code. Info severity (dead exports, not a bug). Removal is low-risk but is not a trivially-safe mechanical autofix (it deletes exported API and could in principle be referenced by tests/stories not in this set — verify first).
confirmed.fix: Grep the whole repo (incl. tests/stories) to confirm zero references, then delete Tag and ModePill from primitives.tsx. If a future exclusions list needs them, reintroduce when there's a real caller.
confirmed.evidence: primitives.tsx:90-97,100-112; grep for Tag/ModePill outside primitives.tsx → no matches in src/components.

confirmed.title: Hardcoded rgba muted-on-dark instead of a token
confirmed.detail: Confirmed — this is the same line as fe-components-r0 (line 5, `const BAR_MUTED = "rgba(255,255,255,0.65)"`), viewed through the simplify lane. tokens.css defines `--bar-fg` per theme but no muted variant, so this hardcodes a white that may not match `--bar-fg` in every theme. Info here since r0 already carries the warning. IMPORTANT correction to this finding's fix: the suggested `text-bar-fg opacity-70` does not work as intended — D42 documents that Tailwind opacity modifiers do not resolve on the hex var() tokens (the indicator was once invisible for exactly this reason). The correct fix is a real `--bar-muted` token (see r0).
confirmed.fix: Add a `--bar-muted` token per theme in tokens.css and use `style={{ color: 'var(--bar-muted)' }}` (per r0). Do NOT use `text-bar-fg opacity-XX` — opacity modifiers don't resolve on the hex var() tokens (D42).
confirmed.evidence: TitleBar.tsx:5; tokens.css no --bar-muted; D42 (opacity modifiers fail on hex tokens). Same line as r0.

confirmed.title: SWATCH hardcodes per-theme bg hex (acknowledged but duplicative)
confirmed.detail: Confirmed the literals at lines 6-10: `SWATCH: Record<Theme,string>` = #EEEBE1/#1A1916/#0E0E0E — exact duplicates of the three themes' `--bg` values in tokens.css (verified: paper #EEEBE1, warm #1A1916, black #0E0E0E). The comment (lines 4-5) correctly justifies the literal: each swatch must display theme X's colour regardless of the active theme, so it cannot use `var(--bg)` (which resolves to the active theme). So the literal is genuinely required — this is NOT a hard-rule-7 violation. The only residual is the values live in two places and could silently drift. Info, low priority; do not 'fix' by switching to var(--bg) which would break the feature.
confirmed.fix: Optional: source these three from a single shared theme-metadata constant (alongside THEMES/THEME_LABELS) so tokens.css and this map can't diverge. Do NOT replace with var(--bg) — the swatches must show each theme regardless of the active one.
confirmed.evidence: ThemeSwitcher.tsx:4-10; tokens.css --bg = #EEEBE1/#1A1916/#0E0E0E across the three theme blocks.

confirmed.title: Decision-archaeology references (R77) scattered in Dial comments
confirmed.detail: Confirmed. The Dial doc comment (line 43, '...category-run arcs with a 1px casing (R77)...') and the arc comment (line 155, 'a 1px ink casing (R77) behind the coloured stroke') both cite the risk-register id R77. MiniDial.tsx carries the same R77 tags (its CASING_W comment: '1px of ink either side (R77)'). The visual intent ('ink casing behind the stroke') is the useful part; the R77 tag is decision-archaeology that belongs in the risk register/decisions. Info, subjective comment nit.
confirmed.fix: Drop the (R77) tags in Dial.tsx (and MiniDial.tsx for consistency); keep the plain description of what the casing is.
confirmed.evidence: Dial.tsx:43,155 and MiniDial.tsx:9 all cite R77 in comments.

confirmed.title: Arc-building geometry duplicated between Dial and MiniDial
confirmed.detail: Confirmed. Dial.arcs (lines 60-73) and MiniDial.arcs (MiniDial.tsx lines 26-33) run the same algorithm: minutesSinceMidnight on run.start/end, `trim = endMin - startMin > TRIM*2`, slice by TRIM, `arcPath(...)`, `categoryColorVar(run.category_slug)`. Only the centre/radius/trim constants differ, and the trim constant already silently differs (Dial ARC_GAP_MIN=4, MiniDial TRIM_MIN=3) — exactly the kind of drift the finding predicts. A shared `buildArcs` helper would keep the two dials in lockstep. Info severity (quality/duplication, not a bug). Reasonable cleanup.
confirmed.fix: Extract a `buildArcs(runs, { cx, cy, r, trimMin, dayStartUnix })` helper in lib/geometry.ts and call it from both Dial and MiniDial. Note the trim values currently differ (4 vs 3) intentionally per dial size — pass them as params, don't unify the value.
confirmed.evidence: Dial.tsx:60-73 vs MiniDial.tsx:26-33 — same trim-and-build algorithm; ARC_GAP_MIN=4 (Dial) vs TRIM_MIN=3 (MiniDial).

confirmed.title: Radio dot fill hardcodes c-deep, ignoring selectable accent
confirmed.detail: Confirmed the selected radio dot uses `bg-c-deep` (line 36, inside the span at line 35). This is a token class, not ad-hoc hex, so hard rule 7 is satisfied — the finding itself flags this only as a consistency observation, not a violation. The suggestion to introduce an `--accent` token aliasing `--c-deep` so the active-control colour lives in one place is a reasonable but speculative refactor (the finding does not verify how many primitives repeat the literal). Downgraded to info; current usage is token-based and correct, no action required.
confirmed.fix: Optional/low-priority: introduce an `--accent` token aliasing `--c-deep` and use it across active-control primitives (radio dot, toggle, focus ring) so the accent is changeable in one place. Not required — current usage is correct.
confirmed.evidence: RadioGroup.tsx:35-36 bg-c-deep (a token class, not hex).

confirmed.title: Comment re-narrates D47 suggestion rationale
confirmed.detail: Confirmed. Lines 30-32: '// The app catalog drives the category suggestion (from each app's Apple-assigned // category, D47). Load once, then re-render so suggestions appear.' This restates a decision (the LSApplicationCategoryType suggestion, recorded as D47/D47-era in decisions.md) and narrates the obvious useEffect-loads-then-setState pattern below (lines 33-39). Info, subjective comment nit. (Minor: the citation says D47 but the suggestion mechanism is documented in decisions.md around the same entry — either way the rationale belongs there, not inline.)
confirmed.fix: Trim to `// Load the app catalog so we can suggest a category.` and drop the D47 reference.
confirmed.evidence: UncategorizedApps.tsx:30-39; the suggestion-from-Apple-category rationale lives in decisions.md (D47-era entry).

confirmed.title: Focus-on-open comment over-explains a one-liner
confirmed.detail: Confirmed at line 27: `// Land focus inside the dialog so Esc works and SR users enter the modal.` explaining `dialogRef.current?.focus()` (line 28). Mildly verbose; the SR-entry note is the only non-obvious part. Info, subjective. Worth noting this comment becomes partly inaccurate if fe-components-r2 (focus trap/restore) is addressed, so trimming/updating it pairs naturally with that fix.
confirmed.fix: Shorten to `// Focus the dialog so Esc + screen-reader entry work.` or fold into the r2 focus-management change.
confirmed.evidence: Modal.tsx:27-28.

confirmed.title: Day vs Week use inconsistent day-end semantics; Week can double-count a midnight event
confirmed.detail: Verified end-to-end. dayBounds (dates.ts:7) returns end = local 23:59:59. WeekView.tsx:24-25 passes the 7 dayBounds().start values and weekEnd=dayBounds(days[6]).end into getWeek. Rust get_week (lib.rs:133) sets each interior day's end to `day_starts.get(i+1)` = the NEXT day's local midnight. db::get_activity_logs (db.rs:264) filters `start_time >= ?1 AND start_time <= ?2` — INCLUSIVE on both ends. So an event whose start_time is exactly local midnight T is included in day i's slice ([start_i, T], T<=T) AND day i+1's slice ([T, end], T>=T). build_week_view (rollup.rs:261) sums per-day active_secs, so that event inflates the week total and is double-counted. The single Day view (get_day, end=23:59:59) avoids it only because 23:59:59 is one integer-second short of midnight and timestamps are integer seconds. Real but rare (requires a capture event with start_time at exactly 00:00:00 local). This is NOT the intended dial-vs-ledger divergence of D40 (that is about excursion-absorb), so D40 does not cover it.
confirmed.fix: Adopt one half-open convention across both views. Cleanest: make dayBounds return end = next-local-midnight (exclusive) and change the Rust filter to `start_time >= ?1 AND start_time < ?2` (get_week already passes next-midnight as interior end, so it becomes correct; get_day's caller passes the same exclusive end). Add a test asserting sum over Week day-slices == 7 standalone Day totals for the same dates. Note: this touches src-tauri/src/db.rs:264 SQL (allowed — repository layer, not generated bindings) and must keep get_day's end consistent; existing db.rs tests use distinct non-boundary timestamps so they will not break.
confirmed.evidence: db.rs:264 `WHERE start_time >= ?1 AND start_time <= ?2` (inclusive both ends); lib.rs:133 `let end = day_starts.get(i + 1).copied().unwrap_or(week_end)`; rollup.rs:261 build_week_view sums day active_secs; dates.ts:7 end=23:59:59; WeekView.tsx:24-25 caller. D40 covers excursion-absorb, not boundary inclusivity, so not a documented deliberate choice.

confirmed.title: Prefix icon match can resolve a process to an unrelated app (e.g. slack → slackdownloader)
confirmed.detail: Verified. resolve() (appIcons.ts:71-73) fuzzy fallback iterates `for (const [k, v] of map)` and returns the first entry where `k.length >= 3 && (k.startsWith(key) || key.startsWith(k))`. The comment's QR-Code/Code guard holds (neither is a prefix of the other), but a genuine prefix collision — 'slack' vs an installed 'slackmoji'/'slackdownloader', or 'notes' vs 'notesync' — returns the wrong icon. Map iteration order follows insertion = the order list_installed_apps returned, so the wrong pick is nondeterministic across machines. Worst case is a cosmetically wrong icon, never data corruption — info is right. Same code path is reused by resolveSuggestedSlug (appIcons.ts:93), so a bad prefix collision could also surface a wrong suggested-category slug there (still advisory only — never auto-assigns, per D44).
confirmed.fix: Prefer the shortest matching candidate and break ties deterministically by sorted name, so 'slack'→'slack' beats 'slack'→'slackdownloader'; or only accept a prefix match when the longer name's next char is a boundary. Keep it conservative; do not regress the iTerm2↔iTerm case the test covers.
confirmed.evidence: appIcons.ts:71-73 loop returns first prefix match in Map insertion order; resolve() shared by resolveIcon (l.82) and resolveSuggestedSlug (l.93).

confirmed.title: processOwner skips empty-pattern rules; Rust find_category would treat them as match-all
confirmed.detail: Verified divergence. processOwner (ruleMatch.ts:15) guards `if (r.pattern && name.includes(...))`, so an empty pattern never matches. db::find_category (db.rs:GET via get_rules then `match_target.to_lowercase().contains(&rule.pattern.to_lowercase())`) has no empty guard, and Rust str::contains("") is true — an empty-pattern PROCESS rule would own every app from its id onward. create_rule (db.rs create_rule / lib.rs) does no empty-pattern validation. So if an empty-pattern process rule ever existed, the editor's conflict warning would disagree with what reprocess_logs actually does — a true break of D44's 'mirror the Rust engine, engine is source of truth' contract. Defensive, not a live bug: every UI path that builds rules derives/trims a non-empty pattern, and a user cannot save a blank one through the normal flow. Hence info.
confirmed.fix: Pick one engine-aligned behavior and document it next to the D44 note: either reject empty/whitespace patterns at the Rust create_rule boundary (so the state can't exist on either side — preferred, since match-all is never a useful rule), or drop the `r.pattern &&` short-circuit so the mirror matches find_category's match-all. Do not change find_category's semantics alone, as that would silently make blank rules match-all in the engine.
confirmed.evidence: ruleMatch.ts:15 `if (r.pattern && name.includes(...))` vs db.rs find_category `match_target.to_lowercase().contains(&rule.pattern.to_lowercase())` with no empty guard; create_rule (db.rs) inserts pattern unchanged; D44 states the frontend mirror must match the engine.

confirmed.title: Misleading "exclusive end" doc for getWeek contradicts dayBounds
confirmed.detail: Verified. tauri.ts:81 JSDoc says "`weekEnd` is the exclusive end of the last day." The only caller (WeekView.tsx:25) passes `dayBounds(days[6]).end` = 23:59:59, and the Rust query (db.rs:264) is inclusive (`start_time <= ?2`). So weekEnd is an inclusive 23:59:59 bound, not exclusive — the comment describes a contract the code doesn't implement. A future reader passing a true exclusive midnight would silently shift behavior. The SAME wrong 'exclusive end' wording also appears in the Rust doc at lib.rs:113, so this is a two-site doc lie, not one line. (If fe-lib-r0/s1 are fixed by moving to a real exclusive-midnight half-open convention, this comment becomes true instead — coordinate the two.)
confirmed.fix: Correct both docs to match current reality: "`weekEnd` is the inclusive upper bound (Unix secs) of the last day (23:59:59), matching `dayBounds().end`." If the team instead fixes the semantics (fe-lib-r0), make the code exclusive and leave the doc as-is. Either way the doc must not lie.
confirmed.evidence: tauri.ts:81 doc 'exclusive end'; WeekView.tsx:25 passes dayBounds().end=23:59:59; db.rs:264 inclusive `<= ?2`; lib.rs:113 repeats 'the exclusive end' in Rust doc.

confirmed.title: dayBounds end of 23:59:59 drops the final second of the day
confirmed.detail: Verified the mechanism but this finding substantially overlaps fe-lib-r0/s0 — it is the same 23:59:59-vs-next-midnight seam viewed from the Day side. The 23:59:59 end plus inclusive `start_time <= ?2` (db.rs:264) means an event whose start_time is in the final second (a start exactly at 23:59:59 IS included; only a start at the next 00:00:00 is excluded), and getWeek uses next-midnight as the interior boundary, so the two views place the boundary one second apart. The concrete, actionable harm (Week double-count) is already captured as the confirmed fe-lib-r0; standing alone this is a presentation of the same root cause, so downgrade to info to avoid double-counting the finding. The detail also slightly overstates: a start at exactly 23:59:59 is included (<=), and integer-second timestamps mean there is no '23:59:59.x' rounding case.
confirmed.fix: Resolve via the single fix in fe-lib-r0 (one canonical half-open boundary: end = next-local-midnight, Rust filter `start_time < ?2`). No separate change needed once r0 lands.
confirmed.evidence: dates.ts:7 end=...23,59,59; db.rs:264 inclusive; lib.rs:133 interior end = next midnight. Same root cause as fe-lib-r0 which is confirmed-warning.

confirmed.title: Redundant re-sort of rules already returned id-ordered
confirmed.detail: Verified. ruleMatch.ts:12 does `[...rules].sort((a, b) => a.id - b.id)`. The only source of these rules is getRules() (tauri.ts:115) → commands.getRules → db::get_rules (db.rs) whose SQL is `... FROM rules ORDER BY id ASC`. So input is already id-ordered; the copy+sort duplicates a guaranteed invariant and adds an allocation + sort on every editor keystroke. Minor (info) — a handful of rules, negligible cost — but genuinely redundant.
confirmed.fix: Iterate `rules` directly: `for (const r of rules) { ... }`. If defending against unordered callers is desired, a one-line comment noting getRules() returns id-ordered is enough; the copy+sort is not.
confirmed.evidence: ruleMatch.ts:12 sorts; db.rs:360-ish get_rules SQL `ORDER BY id ASC`; tauri.ts:115 getRules is the only producer.

confirmed.title: Header comment re-narrates design rationale that lives in decisions.md
confirmed.detail: Verified. The header (ruleMatch.ts:3-6) re-argues the D44 rationale and contains a now-stale claim: "this never sorts anything" — yet line 12 literally sorts. The 'why' lives in D44; the code needs only what it mirrors. Note: if fe-lib-s2 is taken (remove the sort), the 'never sorts anything' clause becomes accurate again — but it is still rationale that belongs in decisions.md, so trimming is the right call either way.
confirmed.fix: Trim to one line, e.g.: "// Frontend mirror of Rust db::find_category for PROCESS rules (D44): case-insensitive substring, first-by-id wins. Title rules excluded (they match window titles, not app names)." Drop the 'never sorts anything' sentence.
confirmed.evidence: ruleMatch.ts:3-6 header vs ruleMatch.ts:12 sort; D44 already documents the rationale.

confirmed.title: JSDoc @param/@returns ceremony on a one-line wrapper

But this is a ~12-line readability cleanup of clearly-correct code, not over-engineering or a defect — calling it 'warning/over-engineering' overstates it. Downgrade to info. The two public functions could become thin wrappers over a `lookup(loaded, cache, name)` helper, but the current form is also perfectly clear; treat as optional tidy-up.

confirmed.detail: Verified. categories.ts:20 derives `CANONICAL_SLUGS = CANONICAL_CATEGORIES.map(c => c.slug)` (an array) solely so categoryColorVar (l.23) can do `CANONICAL_SLUGS.includes(slug)` — a linear scan over 5 items on every call. A Set is the idiomatic membership structure. Truly minor (5 elements) — info.

confirmed.title: Defensive Math.max(0, Math.floor()) guards an impossible negative duration
confirmed.detail: Verified the code: format.ts:6 `const total = Math.max(0, Math.floor(secs));`. Durations originate from Rust aggregates (hard rule 6), which are non-negative integer-second counts, so the negative clamp guards a state the data contract makes unreachable. However, removing a harmless defensive clamp from a pure display formatter is a near-zero-value change and the 'ai-smell' framing is a stylistic opinion, not a defect — keeping it costs nothing and protects against a future non-Rust caller. Downgrade to info; treat as optional. Note `Math.floor` itself is still wanted (drops fractional seconds), so only the `Math.max(0, ...)` is in question.
confirmed.fix: Optional: `const total = Math.floor(secs);` if you want to drop the clamp. Given the negligible benefit and the harmlessness, leaving it is also fine. Do not remove Math.floor.
confirmed.evidence: format.ts:6 clamp; hard rule 6 + Rust u/i64 second aggregates make negative input unreachable in the data path; pure display function, no correctness impact either way.

confirmed.title: --serve loop sends unvalidated/blank stdin lines straight to the model
confirmed.detail: Confirmed. Line 97 `while let line = readLine(strippingNewline: true)` and line 98 `await runRecap(prompt: line)` pass each stdin line verbatim into runRecap → session.respond with zero validation (no empty/whitespace/length check). A blank line triggers a full warm model call (~1-2s per the README) with no facts to anchor, so result.content.text would be empty or fabricated prose — the exact failure mode hard rule 6 guards against, except with no number to anchor. The one-in/one-out invariant is preserved (every line still yields one ok/error/unavailable line), so this is robustness not protocol desync. This is the same finding as sidecar-s8 (it cites line 49/98 of the same loop). Correctly scoped as info: this is the D49 spike, and the README explicitly defers real facts-line JSON validation to the wired sidecar — the value here is carrying a blank-input guard into chunk B–D so Rust falls back to the deterministic D48 template rather than getting fabricated prose.
confirmed.fix: In the --serve loop guard blank input before calling the model: `let trimmed = line.trimmingCharacters(in: .whitespacesAndNewlines); guard !trimmed.isEmpty else { emitLine(["status": "error", "detail": "empty input"]); continue }`. Carry this guard into the real sidecar so Rust falls back to the deterministic template on empty/malformed facts.
confirmed.evidence: main.swift:97-98 pass raw line to runRecap with no guard; runRecap (line 49-72) feeds prompt straight to session.respond. README (line 21) documents protocol as 'one JSON-facts line in' and defers real validation to the wired sidecar. D49 confirms the spike is dormant.

confirmed.title: unavailable tag embeds free-text reason despite comment claiming a stable tag
confirmed.detail: Confirmed as a real code-vs-comment mismatch. Line 38 comment promises 'serialized to a stable tag Rust can branch on (never free text)', but line 44 builds `"unavailable:\(String(describing: reason))"`. `String(describing:)` on the availability reason enum is debug-formatted and not a documented-stable string; its spelling can shift across macOS point releases. If a future Rust consumer matches the full tag rather than just the `unavailable` prefix, an OS update could silently break the branch. D49/chunk plan says Rust branches on 'stable status tags (C4)', so pinning the unavailable suffix to a fixed token set is the right shape to carry into the real sidecar. Info, not warning: spike-only today, and the current intent is for Rust to branch on the prefix.
confirmed.fix: Switch on the reason cases to emit fixed tokens (e.g. `unavailable:appleIntelligenceNotEnabled` / `unavailable:modelNotReady` / `unavailable:deviceNotEligible` / `unavailable:other`) instead of `String(describing:)`, and document that Rust branches only on the part before the first `:`.
confirmed.evidence: main.swift:38 comment 'stable tag ... never free text' vs line 44 `String(describing: reason)`. D49 plans Rust branching on 'stable status tags (C4)'.

confirmed.title: emitLine silently drops the response if JSON serialization or UTF-8 decode fails
confirmed.detail: Confirmed as described. Lines 28-31: `if let data = try? JSONSerialization.data(...), let str = String(data: data, encoding: .utf8) { print(str) }` with no else — on failure it emits nothing. The finding itself concedes this cannot realistically fail for the current `[String: String]` payloads (JSONSerialization escapes rather than rejects bad UTF-8), so it is not a live bug — correctly filed as low-confidence info. The consequence if a future payload type makes it fail (emitting zero lines, breaking the strict one-in/one-out contract so Rust blocks on a read that never arrives) is the legitimate robustness point. Same root as sidecar-s3, which proposes the same else-branch fix.
confirmed.fix: Add an else branch so every input still yields one line, e.g. `else { logErr("serialize failed"); print("{\"status\":\"error\",\"detail\":\"serialize failed\"}") }`.
confirmed.evidence: main.swift:28-31 use try?/if let with no else, falling through silently on failure. Payload type is [String:String] (line 27), so failure is not currently reachable.

confirmed.title: File header re-narrates the spike rationale + decision IDs (archaeology)
confirmed.detail: Confirmed the header exists: lines 1-13 are a 13-line block restating why the spike exists and enumerating proof goals tagged C1/C3/C9/C2/C4/C6 — content also in README.md and decisions.md (D49). Real comment-bloat by the project's own standard. But downgrading to info-as-judgment-call: this is a spike whose entire deliverable is the verdict it documents, and the brief flags decision-archaeology as something to be skeptical of rewriting. The header is arguably the spike's charter and harmless. Trimming is reasonable but a style preference, not a defect — leave to author discretion.
confirmed.fix: Optionally cut to 2-3 lines: what the binary does + how to run, e.g. `// Headless Swift CLI: availability gate + one @Generable recap call + a --serve stdio loop.` `// Run: swift run usageos-ai  (or -- --serve). See README.md.` Delete the C1/C3/C9 proof enumeration.
confirmed.evidence: main.swift:1-13 is a 13-line header duplicating README/D49 content. Same duplication pattern in Package.swift:2-8 (sidecar-s7).

confirmed.title: Comment above RecapProse repeats hard-rule-6 narration
confirmed.detail: Confirmed lines 18-19 exist verbatim and restate the prose-only / hard-rule-6 intent already expressed by the @Guide string (line 22) and the instructions block (lines 57-64). Minor redundancy. Downgrading rationale matches s0: low-stakes spike comment, trimming is a style preference not a defect.
confirmed.fix: Reduce to one line, e.g. `// Prose only — no numeric field, so the model can't fabricate a count (hard rule 6).`
confirmed.evidence: main.swift:18-19 restate the prose-only rule already in @Guide (line 22) and instructions (lines 57-64).

confirmed.title: availabilityTag() computed for a startup stderr log, recomputed per request
confirmed.detail: Confirmed the mechanics: line 93 `logErr("[availability] \(availabilityTag().tag)")` queries availability once at startup purely to log, and line 50 inside runRecap recomputes it to actually gate. In --serve mode runRecap runs per line, so availability is queried once for the startup log plus once per request. The extra `SystemLanguageModel.default.availability` query is cheap and runs once at startup, not in a hot loop — so 'computed three times per run' overstates it (it's once at startup + once per request, which is the real gate). Real but trivial; downgrading from the implied smell to info. This is a deliberate stderr affordance ('Report availability to a human on stderr regardless of mode', line 92) useful for a spike a human runs by hand.
confirmed.fix: Optionally drop the line-93 standalone log, or keep it as the deliberate human-facing startup affordance. If kept, no change needed.
confirmed.evidence: main.swift:92-93 startup stderr log calls availabilityTag(); runRecap (line 50) recomputes for the gate. Line 92 comment shows the stderr log is an intentional human affordance.

confirmed.title: emitLine takes [String:String] but forces a numeric field through String(ms)
confirmed.detail: Confirmed. emitLine is typed `[String: String]` (line 27), so the latency must be stringified at the call site: line 74 `"ms": String(ms)`. The homogeneous String-map shape forces numeric coercion at call sites and pairs with the silent-drop behavior flagged in sidecar-r2 (lines 28-31 have no else). The two halves of this finding overlap r2; the actionable part is the same defensive else-branch. The [String:Any] alternative is a real option but the simpler keep-String-map-plus-else-branch is the safer call for a 4-field protocol.
confirmed.fix: Keep [String:String] but add an else branch in emitLine that logErr's on serialization/encoding failure so a dropped line is observable (see sidecar-r2). Or accept [String:Any] so callers pass `ms: ms` directly.
confirmed.evidence: main.swift:27 signature [String:String]; line 74 `String(ms)` coercion; lines 28-31 drop silently on failure.

confirmed.title: Inline C-tag annotations scattered through runRecap restate decisions
confirmed.detail: Confirmed the C-tags exist: line 26 (C4/C6), line 38 (C3), line 48 (C2), line 52 (C4/C5), line 76 (overflow/refusal → fallback). These point into decisions.md/the C-register rather than explaining code. Real comment-bloat by the project standard. Downgrading to info on the same basis as s0/s1: a dormant spike, trimming is author discretion not a defect, and the brief cautions against over-eager decision-archaeology rewrites.
confirmed.fix: Optionally strip the C2/C3/C4/C5/C6 suffixes from inline comments; keep the genuinely-useful note that a non-ok status maps to the Rust template fallback, stated once.
confirmed.evidence: main.swift inline C-tags at lines 26, 38, 48, 52, 76 reference decisions.md/C-register.

confirmed.title: Two catch arms produce the same status with near-identical detail formatting
confirmed.detail: Confirmed. Lines 75-79: `catch let error as LanguageModelSession.GenerationError { emitLine(["status": "error", "detail": String(describing: error)]) }` then generic `catch { emitLine(["status": "error", "detail": "\(error)"]) }`. Both emit status "error" with a stringified error; `String(describing:)` vs `"\(error)"` interpolation produce equivalent output. Since Rust branches only on the status tag (D49 C4), the typed first arm earns nothing today. Valid simplification, though the typed arm is harmless and could be a deliberate seam for future GenerationError-specific handling — hence info, not warning.
confirmed.fix: Collapse to a single `catch { emitLine(["status": "error", "detail": String(describing: error)]) }` and remove the GenerationError-specific arm and its comment.
confirmed.evidence: main.swift:75-79 — two catch arms, both emit status 'error' + stringified error. D49 says Rust branches on status tags, not error subtype.

confirmed.title: Obvious comment restating the temperature choice
confirmed.detail: Confirmed line 69 `// Low temperature: faithful, not creative — minimize embellishment/mangling.` sits above `GenerationOptions(temperature: 0.2)` (line 70), and the README documents the same mitigation ('Low temperature ... less creative, less embellishment'). Minor redundancy. Low-confidence by the reviewer's own rating; downgrading to a discretionary trim — keeping a one-line rationale on a non-obvious magic value (0.2) is defensible.
confirmed.fix: Optionally delete or shorten line 69 to `// low temp = faithful, not creative`.
confirmed.evidence: main.swift:69-70; README 'Mitigations found' item 2 documents temperature 0.2.

confirmed.detail: Confirmed: Package.swift lines 2-8 are a 7-line prose comment ('ISOLATED standalone Swift CLI, NOT wired into Tauri yet ... prove a headless Swift command-line binary can reach Apple's FoundationModels ...') duplicating README.md's Question/Verdict and D49. Note the 'NOT wired into Tauri yet' line is actually accurate per D49 (dormant until chunk D) and is arguably worth keeping.

confirmed.evidence: Package.swift:2-8 duplicates README Question/Verdict + D49 content.

confirmed.title: No stdin validation before passing the line to the model in --serve mode
confirmed.detail: Confirmed — this is the same robustness gap as sidecar-r0, viewed from runRecap's entry (line 49 `func runRecap(prompt: String)`) rather than the loop (line 98). Line 98 feeds each raw stdin line straight into runRecap → session.respond with no length/empty/JSON-shape check; an empty line wastes a model round-trip. The README documents the real protocol as 'one JSON-facts line in' and the brief confirms real facts-line validation is deferred to the wired sidecar (D49 chunk B–D), so this is correctly acceptable-for-a-spike info, with the value being a single guard inherited by the real seam.
confirmed.fix: In the --serve loop skip blank lines (and optionally cap length) before calling runRecap: `guard !line.trimmingCharacters(in: .whitespaces).isEmpty else { continue }`. Note in README that full facts-line JSON validation is deferred to the wired sidecar.
confirmed.evidence: main.swift:97-98 pass raw line into runRecap (line 49) with no validation. README line 21 + D49 defer real validation to the wired sidecar. Duplicate of sidecar-r0.

confirmed.title: Prebuilt sidecar binary is untracked AND not gitignored — would be committed by `git add .`
confirmed.detail: Confirmed. `git status --short` shows `?? src-tauri/binaries/` (untracked); `git check-ignore src-tauri/binaries/usageos-ai-aarch64-apple-darwin` exits 1 (NOT ignored). The root `.gitignore` covers `node_modules`, `dist`, `.build/` (SPM caches) but has no `src-tauri/binaries/` rule; `src-tauri/.gitignore` only ignores `/target/` and `/gen/schemas`. `file` confirms it is a 111KB Mach-O arm64 executable. `grep externalBin src-tauri/tauri.conf.json` returns nothing (exit 1) — Tauri does not consume it, consistent with the dormant chunk-D seam (D49). So an aarch64-only compiled blob is one `git add -A` from entering history of a public, audited, privacy-first repo. Note: this is not an active privacy LEAK (the binary does nothing at runtime today), so the `hr1-privacy` framing is slightly strong; the real risk is committing an opaque build artifact. The fix targets `.gitignore`, not the cited binary file, so I retarget file/line to `.gitignore:1`.
confirmed.fix:
Append to root `.gitignore`:

# Prebuilt AI sidecar (built from spikes/foundation-models; wired via externalBin in chunk D)
src-tauri/binaries/

When the sidecar is wired in chunk D, commit it deliberately (or build in CI) and add the externalBin entry then. Adding an ignore rule is safe and touches no merge gate.
confirmed.evidence: git status `?? src-tauri/binaries/`; git check-ignore exit 1; .gitignore has no binaries rule; src-tauri/.gitignore only /target + /gen/schemas; file = Mach-O arm64 111KB; grep externalBin → none; D49 confirms seam dormant until chunk D.

confirmed.title: Content-Security-Policy is disabled (`csp: null`) in a privacy-first app
confirmed.detail: Confirmed at tauri.conf.json:25 — `app.security.csp` is literally `null`, so Tauri injects no CSP. The frontend is fully local (frontendDist ../dist, local fonts @fontsource/anton + jost, custom SVG dial, no chart CDN), so today there is no outbound call — this is defense-in-depth, not an active leak (severity warning is right). A locked CSP is the runtime enforcement of hard rule 1 at the webview boundary, where an accidental future `fetch()` or a compromised JS dependency could egress with nothing stopping it. This is the higher-value of the two CSP findings; merged with config-s0. CAUTION on the fix: the exact `connect-src` origin Tauri v2 IPC needs varies by version/platform and a too-tight CSP can break the IPC bridge or fonts — must be verified by actually running the app (`tauri dev`), so this is not a blind autofix.
confirmed.fix: Set an explicit restrictive CSP, e.g. `"csp": "default-src 'self'; connect-src 'self' ipc: http://ipc.localhost; img-src 'self' data:; style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'; font-src 'self' data:; script-src 'self'"`. Verify against a running build that IPC and the Bauhaus fonts still load before merging; tune connect-src to the actual Tauri v2 IPC origin.
confirmed.evidence: tauri.conf.json:25 `"csp": null` inside app.security; frontend deps in package.json are all local (fontsource, react, no remote asset libs); hard rule 1 in CLAUDE.md.

confirmed.title: opener:default grants allow-open-url (open arbitrary http/https) — over-broad, unused
confirmed.detail: Confirmed. capabilities/default.json:9 lists `"opener:default"`, and line 10 separately lists `"opener:allow-reveal-item-in-dir"`. I read the plugin's own permissions/default.toml from the cargo cache: `[default]` expands to `allow-open-url`, `allow-reveal-item-in-dir`, `allow-default-urls`. So `opener:default` already includes reveal-item, making line 10 redundant, AND it grants `allow-open-url` (hand arbitrary mailto/tel/http/https URLs to the OS default app). The frontend only ever calls `revealItemInDir` (SettingsView.tsx:1 import, used at lines 108 and 118); grep finds no `openUrl`/`open(` call anywhere in src/. So URL-opening is unused capability surface on a no-network product — least-privilege says drop it. Dropping `opener:default` and keeping only `opener:allow-reveal-item-in-dir` gives exactly the granted-and-used surface.
confirmed.fix: In src-tauri/capabilities/default.json delete line 9 `"opener:default"` and keep line 10 `"opener:allow-reveal-item-in-dir"`. Then run the app once to confirm reveal-in-Finder still works (the only opener call). Not a blind autofix — it's a capability-surface change.
confirmed.evidence: default.json:9 opener:default + :10 allow-reveal-item-in-dir; plugin default.toml expands to allow-open-url + allow-reveal-item-in-dir + allow-default-urls; src grep: only revealItemInDir used (SettingsView.tsx:108,118), no openUrl.

confirmed.title: tokio features 'time' and 'sync' are unused; 'rt' only used in a test
confirmed.detail: Confirmed and tightened. Cargo.toml:35 = `tokio = { version = "1", features = ["rt", "time", "sync"] }`. The ONLY `tokio::` reference in all of src-tauri/src is `tokio::runtime::Builder::new_current_thread()` at ai/mod.rs:72, inside the `#[cfg(test)]` block (a `block_on` helper). grep for `tokio::time`/`tokio::sync` returns nothing. So `time` and `sync` are genuinely dead features. Nuance on the fix: the seam IS production code — `Narrator::narrate` and `build_recap` are real `async fn`s (ai/mod.rs:30,51) per D49 chunk A — but nothing in production *drives* a runtime (no executor, no `.await` outside the trait/seam definitions; build_recap has no non-test callers). So `rt` is only needed by the test `block_on`. Downgrading severity to info: this is dead dependency-feature surface, not a correctness or build problem, and the deferral is consistent with the dormant D49 seam. Trimming `time`+`sync` is safe; moving tokio to dev-dependencies is a more aggressive change that should wait until chunk D confirms the production runtime story.
confirmed.fix: Trim to `tokio = { version = "1", features = ["rt"] }` — drops the unused `time` and `sync`. Verify `cargo test` still builds (the block_on helper needs `rt`). Defer the dev-dependencies move until chunk D wires the real executor.
confirmed.evidence: Cargo.toml:35; grep tokio:: → only ai/mod.rs:72 (inside #[cfg(test)]); no tokio::time/sync; build_recap has no non-test callers; D49 says seam dormant.

confirmed.title: 6-line decision-archaeology comment on the specta pin belongs in decisions.md
