Keeping as info but flagging it is genuinely optional; the project's own docs-in-lockstep ethos tolerates a pointer-style header. No functional impact.
verdicts.fix: Optional trim to a 2-3 line what-it-does header that points to D30 rather than restating it, e.g. 'Project inference (D30): turn cwd/url/title signals into a project_id or an explicit abstain (no-signal/ambiguous). See decisions.md D30 for the abstain/no-fragmentation rationale.' Autofixable as a pure comment edit — but it is subjective wording, so leave to author discretion.
verdicts.evidence: Lines 1-10 vs decisions.md:105 — same phrases. Pure prose, no code impact.

verdicts.title: Candidate struct field comments restate D30 reconciliation rationale
verdicts.detail: Confirmed: the weak-signal reconciliation rationale appears on the Candidate fields (lines 42-45, name_alias/weak), again on resolve (lines 86-87), and in the module header (lines 6-8) — three copies of 'reconcile to an existing project by name_alias before treating canonical_key as new'. Real minor duplication. Downgraded to a discretionary info: the comments are accurate and short, and consolidating is a taste call with zero functional effect. Sibling to enrich-s3/s8 (same over-narration pattern across the file).
verdicts.fix: Optional: keep one terse line on the weak field ('weak signal: reconcile by name_alias before creating — D30') and trim the duplicates on name_alias and in resolve's doc. Autofixable as a pure comment edit; leave to author discretion on wording.
verdicts.evidence: Lines 42-45 (field comments), 86-87 (resolve doc), 6-8 (module header) all carry the same reconciliation prose.

verdicts.title: parse_site duplicates port-stripping that parse_url could own
verdicts.detail: Confirmed. parse_site (mod.rs:13-21) calls project::parse_url, discards scheme and path, then re-strips the port at line 15 (`host.split(':').next().unwrap_or("")`). The localhost classifier at project.rs:176 needs the same port-strip (per enrich-r0/s1). Port-stripping the host is done in two places with the inconsistency that parse_url itself returns the port-bearing host. Low-priority cleanup; correct as-is. Confidence is appropriately low.
verdicts.fix: Either have parse_url return a port-less host (and adjust github/localhost branches that may rely on raw host), or extract a small host_without_port(&str) helper used by both parse_site and the localhost branch. Removes the duplicated split(':').next() idiom. Not autofixable (touches a pub(crate) signature/shared helper extraction).
verdicts.evidence: mod.rs:15 split(':').next() duplicates the same dance needed at project.rs:176; parse_url (project.rs:311) returns raw host.

verdicts.title: no-remote branch clones folder three times
verdicts.detail: Partially confirmed. The double-git observation duplicates enrich-r1 and is explicitly conceded as fine in the finding itself. The substantive part is the no-remote branch (lines 133-139): it does `canonical_key: folder.clone()`, `display_name: folder.clone()`, and `name_alias: Some(folder)` — that is two clones plus a move of the original (not three clones; the third use moves folder). So 'clones folder three times' is slightly overstated: it is two clones + one move. Real but trivial micro-verbosity; downgrading to info. No correctness impact.
verdicts.fix: Minor: e.g. `let key = folder; Candidate { canonical_key: key.clone(), display_name: key.clone(), name_alias: Some(key), .. }` — one fewer clone, or accept as-is. Not worth a behavior risk. Not autofixable (logic-adjacent restructuring of a struct literal).
verdicts.evidence: Lines 133-139: folder.clone() (134), folder.clone() (135), Some(folder) (137) — two clones + a move, not three clones as stated. Double-git is the same as enrich-r1.

verdicts.title: parse_url returns owned Strings where borrows suffice
verdicts.detail: Confirmed factually: parse_url (line 311) returns (String, String, String) and allocates via to_string()/format! (lines 313-321) although callers (classify_url line 144, parse_site mod.rs:14) only read the parts. Borrowing would avoid per-event allocations. But this is a leaf helper called once per focus event (not a hot loop), the alloc cost is negligible at that cadence, and the suggested signature change (returning &str/Cow with the format!("/{path}") owned piece) adds lifetime/Cow complexity for no measurable gain. Over-engineering relative to the call frequency; downgraded to info, do-not-act unless profiling says otherwise. The finding's own confidence is low.
verdicts.fix: Optional micro-opt: return borrowed slices with a Cow for the leading-slash path case. Given once-per-event cadence the current owned-String form is clearer; recommend leaving as-is. Not autofixable (public-ish signature + lifetimes).
verdicts.evidence: parse_url line 311-322 allocates 3 Strings; callers at project.rs:144 and mod.rs:14 only read. Called per focus event, not a hot loop.

verdicts.title: git() comment over-explains the threading rationale
verdicts.detail: Confirmed the comment exists (lines 277-278: 'Shells out (the consumer runs on a dedicated thread, so this never blocks the async executor).'). It does put a caller-invariant on a leaf helper. But the note is accurate and short, and the threading guarantee is load-bearing context for why shelling out here is acceptable — arguably useful at the call boundary. Pure style; downgraded to discretionary info, sibling to enrich-s3/s4 over-narration pattern. No functional impact.
verdicts.fix: Optional: trim to 'Run `git -C <dir> ...`, returning trimmed stdout on success, None on failure/empty.' Autofixable as a comment-only edit; leave to author discretion since the threading note has some documentary value.
verdicts.evidence: Lines 277-278 carry the executor sentence on the git() leaf helper; same over-narration family as the module header and field comments.

verdicts.title: classify_title separator split is over-built for a single observed format
verdicts.detail: Confirmed: lines 191-196 split on ` — ` then flat_map split on ` - `, map trim, filter empty, collect into a Vec, then only use parts[parts.len()-1] (line 201) and parts.len() (line 198). The intermediate Vec + double-delimiter flat_map is heavier than 'take the trailing segment after the rightmost separator'. Real minor over-build. Downgraded to info: it is correct and the allocation is once-per-event (negligible). The suggested rsplit fix is reasonable but a behavior-equivalent rewrite that needs the existing tests (cwd_and_title_do_not_fragment line 391, bare_app_title_is_no_signal line 516) to stay green — worth care, not urgency. Confidence appropriately low.
verdicts.fix: Optional: replace the Vec pipeline with a rightmost-separator split, e.g. find the last ` — `/` - ` and take the trailing trimmed segment; NoSignal if none. Must preserve the parts.len()<2 → NoSignal semantics (a title with no separator yields no project). Verify against existing title tests. Not autofixable (logic rewrite).
verdicts.evidence: Lines 191-196 build a Vec via flat_map over two delimiters; only parts[last] (201) and len (198) are used. Tests at 391 and 516 pin the behavior.

verdicts.title: Abstain reason as &'static str + duplicated literals instead of an enum
verdicts.detail: Confirmed: ProjectAssignment::Abstain(&'static str) (line 33) carries free-form `"ambiguous"`/`"no-signal"` literals minted in infer_project (lines 79-83), the legal values are enumerated in a doc comment (lines 31-32), and the caller stringifies via reason.to_string() (capture/mod.rs:223). An AbstainReason enum would make the two kinds exhaustive and typo-proof. This is a valid design-taste suggestion, not a defect — current code is correct and the two literals are produced in exactly one place (lines 80/82), limiting typo risk. Note a refactor would ripple into capture/mod.rs:223 and must keep persisting the exact strings `no-signal`/`ambiguous` that D31 pins as the stored project_abstain_reason values (decisions.md:110) — so any enum must serialize to those exact strings or it breaks the documented storage contract. Downgraded to info (over-engineering lane, low priority, low confidence by author's own mark).
verdicts.fix: Optional: `enum AbstainReason { Ambiguous, NoSignal }` with `fn as_str(&self) -> &'static str` returning exactly "ambiguous"/"no-signal" (must match D31's stored values), Abstain(AbstainReason); update capture/mod.rs:223 to use as_str(). Removes the doc enumeration and stringly-typed literals. Not autofixable (type change crossing the persistence contract).
verdicts.evidence: Line 33 Abstain(&'static str); literals at 79-83; doc at 31-32; capture/mod.rs:223 reason.to_string(). D31 (decisions.md:110) pins project_abstain_reason ∈ {NULL, no-signal, ambiguous} as stored strings — the constraint a refactor must honor.

verdicts.title: Module doc re-narrates decision IDs (D48/D9/C5/C19) instead of describing behavior
verdicts.detail: Confirmed the lines exist: the 8-line header (lines 1-7) does carry D48/D9/C5/C19 tags. However this is a defensible style choice, not a defect. CLAUDE.md's whole workflow is built on dense decision cross-referencing (D1-D50, ADR-style, 'cross-reference from plans/handoffs'), and D49 documents this seam as deliberately dormant with the tags being the live trace back to rationale. The header still describes behavior (one entry point, formats facts, falls back on failure/empty). Trimming is a matter of taste and the cited tags genuinely point to real decisions a reader can resolve. Downgraded to a take-it-or-leave-it info suggestion; not actionable as a quality bug.
verdicts.fix: Optional: trim D/C tags from prose if the team prefers leaner headers, keeping the behavioral summary. Not required — the tags are consistent with the project's cross-referencing convention.
verdicts.evidence: Read mod.rs lines 1-7 — tags present as described. context/decisions.md D49 confirms the seam is deliberately dormant and the decision IDs are the documented rationale trail; CLAUDE.md mandates decision cross-referencing.

verdicts.title: AiError doc re-explains that build_recap ignores the variant distinction
verdicts.detail: Confirmed: lines 14-15 carry the 'treats every variant identically ... keeps the failure legible for logging' doc. But the premise that this defends an unused split is wrong: D49 explicitly plans chunks B-C to spawn the Swift sidecar with 'status tags (C4-C7)', and Unavailable vs Model map exactly to those planned status tags (Apple Intelligence off / device ineligible vs guardrail refusal / overflow). The comment documents real near-term intent, not dead archaeology. It is slightly verbose but accurate and forward-pointing. Downgraded to an optional trim.
verdicts.fix: Optional: shorten to '/// Why a narration attempt failed.' if leaner is preferred; the per-variant docs (lines 18, 20) carry the detail. Keeping the logging note is also reasonable given the planned chunk-B/C status tags.
verdicts.evidence: Read mod.rs lines 14-22. decisions.md D49: 'productionize the Swift sidecar ... status tags (C4-C7)' — the two variants are the documented future consumers.

verdicts.title: Two-variant AiError earns nothing while the seam is dormant and the only consumer collapses both
verdicts.detail: Dropped: this proposes collapsing AiError to a single variant, which directly contradicts a documented deliberate design. D49 states the seam is built 'in reviewable chunks' and chunks B-C 'productionize the Swift sidecar -> ... status tags (C4-C7)'. Unavailable(String) (Apple Intelligence off / device ineligible / not ready) and Model(String) (guardrail refusal / overflow / malformed) are precisely the status-tag categories the planned sidecar will emit and surface in the recap-card badge. The test at lines 127-137 already exercises both variants distinctly. Collapsing now would force a re-split in the very next chunk — the opposite of simplification. The single-caller-collapses-both observation is true today but is the explicitly-intended dormant state (D49), not premature generality.
verdicts.fix: No change. The two-variant split is the documented chunk-A shape feeding the planned chunk-B/C status tags (C4-C7).
verdicts.evidence: decisions.md D49 documents the chunked sidecar plan with status tags (C4-C7) as the consumer of these variants; mod.rs tests lines 127-137 already distinguish Unavailable vs Model. Verification brief lists the dormant seam (D49) as a deliberate choice not to flag.

verdicts.title: generated_by sentinel strings split across two files (const here, bare literal in rollup.rs)
verdicts.detail: Confirmed the factual claim: GENERATED_BY_MODEL = "foundation-models" is a named const at ai/mod.rs:12, while its counterpart "template" is a bare string literal at rollup.rs:684, and tests assert the raw strings in both files (mod.rs:123 'foundation-models', mod.rs:134/143 'template', rollup.rs:905 'template'). So the two sentinels that classify the same Recap.generated_by field do live in different modules with inconsistent treatment (one const, one inline). This is a mild, real consistency/drift smell. Downgraded from the implied actionable to info: the strings are stable, asserted by tests in both files (drift would be caught by cargo test, a merge gate), and co-locating them is a nicety not a correctness fix. Worth doing opportunistically.
verdicts.fix: Optional: define both sentinels as consts near the Recap struct in rollup.rs (e.g. RECAP_BY_TEMPLATE/RECAP_BY_MODEL) and have render_template_recap (rollup.rs:684) and build_recap (mod.rs:59) reference them instead of literals. Keep test assertions on the literal values so they remain an independent guard.
verdicts.evidence: Read ai/mod.rs:12,59 (const + use) and rollup.rs:684 (bare 'template' literal); grep confirms tests assert raw strings in both files (mod.rs:123/134/143, rollup.rs:905). cargo test is a documented merge gate, limiting drift risk.

verdicts.title: Narrator::narrate doc restates the prompt-guarding decision already covered by build_recap
verdicts.detail: Confirmed: lines 28-29 doc the narrate method with the 'instructed not to alter numbers ... build_recap still defends with the fallback' sentence, which does overlap with build_recap's own doc (lines 48-50) and hard rule 6. Mild redundancy. But it is accurate context for an implementor writing the real narrator (it tells them the contract: don't trust the model to obey, the caller still guards). This is a style preference, not a defect. Downgraded to optional trim.
verdicts.fix: Optional: reduce to '/// Phrase the pre-formatted facts prompt into prose.' The numbers/fallback policy is restated in build_recap (lines 48-62).
verdicts.evidence: Read mod.rs lines 28-30 and 48-62 — the fallback policy is indeed stated in both places. Overlap is real but the trait-doc framing is a legitimate contract note.

verdicts.title: Trait doc explains the dyn/async-crate dispatch rationale (belongs in decisions, not the trait)
verdicts.detail: Confirmed: line 25 carries 'Generic dispatch only (no `dyn`), so the async method needs no extra crate.' next to the #[allow(async_fn_in_trait)] attribute (line 26). The claim that the attribute alone signals the choice is only half-true — the attribute explains why the lint is allowed, but the 'no dyn / no extra crate (avoiding async-trait)' rationale is non-obvious and genuinely useful to a future maintainer tempted to box the trait. This is a defensible explanatory comment, not bloat. Lowest-value of the set; downgraded to optional.
verdicts.fix: Optional: drop the second sentence if leaner is preferred. Retaining it is reasonable — it preempts a maintainer adding async-trait or Box<dyn Narrator>, which would break the generic-dispatch design.
verdicts.evidence: Read mod.rs lines 24-31 — comment and #[allow(async_fn_in_trait)] both present; the rationale explains a real design constraint (generic-only dispatch to avoid async-trait), not merely restating the attribute.

verdicts.title: cache_key collisions can serve one app's icon to a different app
verdicts.detail: Verified real. Dedup in collect_dir (apps.rs:112) keys on stem.to_lowercase(), so "Foo Bar" and "Foo-Bar" are distinct keys and BOTH survive dedup. But the cache filename uses cache_key(stem) (apps.rs:133, 189-193), which maps every non-ASCII-alphanumeric char to '_': cache_key("Foo Bar") == cache_key("Foo-Bar") == "Foo_Bar". Non-ASCII collapses too ("Café"/"Cafe"). When app B is processed after app A wrote the colliding PNG, is_cached_fresh (apps.rs:196) compares B's icns mtime against A's PNG mtime; if A's PNG is newer, sips is skipped and B reads A's PNG (apps.rs:147) — B is shown A's icon. Silent (no error), order-dependent across the cache lifetime. The doc at line 188 says 'stable per app name' but it is only stable per *normalized* name, which is not injective. Real but low-probability (requires two installed apps whose stems differ only in non-alphanumeric/diacritic chars). Note: even the fix's distinct-PNG approach is fine, but the underlying collision can only bite the rare near-homograph pair; impact is one wrong icon, never data loss or a panic.
verdicts.fix: Make the filename injective per stem: append a short stable hash of the original stem, e.g. format!("{}-{:08x}.png", cache_key(stem), hash_of(stem)), or hex-encode the non-alphanumeric bytes instead of collapsing each to '_'. Either stays filesystem-safe while keeping distinct names distinct. Must not panic / add a dep beyond std (a tiny inline FNV-1a fold suffices, matching the project's no-new-dep posture, D43).
verdicts.evidence: apps.rs:112 dedup key = stem.to_lowercase(); apps.rs:133/189-193 cache filename = cache_key(stem) collapsing non-alnum to '_'; apps.rs:196-204 is_cached_fresh compares mtimes; apps.rs:147 reads the cached PNG on a fresh hit. No other call site uses cache_key (grep), so no siblings.

verdicts.title: suggest_slug doc-comment re-narrates the D47 decision rationale
verdicts.detail: Verified. The doc on suggest_slug (apps.rs:49-54) re-narrates the WHY that already lives in D47 ('Deliberately conservative... ambiguous ones span several of our categories... The rules engine remains the only thing that actually assigns'). The match arms (apps.rs:57-63) plus the test suggest_slug_maps_high_confidence_types_and_abstains (apps.rs:253-274) make the conservatism self-evident. Minor style nit only.
verdicts.fix: Trim to one line, e.g. /// Map Apple's LSApplicationCategoryType to a canonical category slug for a *suggested* default (D47); None when absent or ambiguous. The productivity/business/browsers narration and the 'rules engine remains the only thing' sentence belong in decisions.md (where they already are).
verdicts.evidence: apps.rs:49-54 doc text mirrors D47 in context/decisions.md:188 nearly verbatim. The match + test already encode the behavior.

verdicts.title: Module header is a 10-line essay re-narrating privacy/hard-rule rationale
verdicts.detail: Verified. The module doc (apps.rs:1-10) spends ~10 lines justifying choices against hard rules 1/3 ('same subprocess posture as enrich's git', 'no unwrap/expect/panic here (hard rule 3)', 'On non-macOS (CI Linux) the app dirs are absent and sips is missing'). The no-panic / degrade-to-None behavior is visible in the code, and the CI-Linux note recurs at apps.rs:68, 106, 144. This is decision-archaeology that lives in D43. Info-level.
verdicts.fix: Cut to ~3 lines: what the module does (installed-app catalog + cached 64px PNGs via sips) and the one load-bearing invariant (every failure degrades to icon: None). Drop the hard-rule citations and the repeated CI-Linux explanation.
verdicts.evidence: apps.rs:1-10 module doc; D43 (decisions.md:175-176) already records the no-dep/no-panic/degrade-to-None rationale. CI-Linux note repeated at apps.rs:68,106,144.

verdicts.title: unwrap_or(category_type) fallback in suggest_slug is dead — the branch is unreachable
verdicts.detail: Verified high-confidence. apps.rs:56 is `let leaf = category_type.rsplit('.').next().unwrap_or(category_type);`. str::rsplit always yields at least one item (for "" it yields Some("")), so .next() is always Some and the unwrap_or(category_type) fallback is unreachable belt-and-suspenders. Harmless but misleading. Same dead-fallback shape exists at enrich/project.rs:332 (`path.rsplit('/').next().unwrap_or(path)`).
verdicts.fix: Use unwrap_or_default() (still defensive, drops the misleading fallback), or since it cannot fail, .expect("rsplit yields at least one item") with the invariant comment per hard rule 3. unwrap_or_default() is simplest. Note this changes behavior only in the impossible case, so verify the test still passes (it will — "" maps to None either way).
verdicts.evidence: apps.rs:56; std rsplit/next semantics; sibling identical pattern at enrich/project.rs:332 found via grep. Test at apps.rs:273 covers the "" input.

verdicts.title: cache_key is a thin single-caller helper; its doc restates the body
verdicts.detail: Verified but minor. cache_key (apps.rs:189-193) is a 4-line helper called exactly once (apps.rs:133). The extraction itself earns its keep (testability + clarity at the call site), so 'over-engineering' overstates it; the only actionable bit is the doc comment at apps.rs:188 restating the obvious body, which overlaps the comment-bloat findings. Downgraded to info as a pure style nit. (Do not inline if the cache_key collision fix in apps-r0 lands — the helper is the natural home for an injective key.)
verdicts.fix: Keep the function (especially given apps-r0). Optionally drop the one-line doc at 188 since the name + body are self-explanatory. Do not inline.
verdicts.evidence: apps.rs:189-193 body; sole call site apps.rs:133 (grep confirms only one caller).

verdicts.title: best_icns hand-rolls a max-by-key fold instead of Iterator::max_by_key
verdicts.detail: Verified. best_icns (apps.rs:158-185) accumulates Option<(PathBuf, i32, u64)> with an explicit match &best { None => true, Some((_, bscore, bsize)) => score > *bscore || (score == *bscore && size > *bsize) } (apps.rs:177-183). Since (score, size) is a natural Ord tuple key, this can be expressed with max_by_key. Valid simplification, but a style preference, not a defect — the current code is correct (the name-match test at apps.rs:300-310 passes). The codebase already uses max_by_key elsewhere (rollup.rs:268,631,757), so the idiom is established.
verdicts.fix: Build candidates as (score, size, path) and pick with .max_by_key(|(score, size, _)| (*score, *size)).map(|(_, _, p)| p), removing the None => true special case. Verify clippy/fmt stay green and the best_icns_prefers_name_match_over_largest test still passes.
verdicts.evidence: apps.rs:158-185 manual fold; established max_by_key idiom at rollup.rs:268/631/757. Test apps.rs:300-310 pins the tie-break behavior.

verdicts.title: APP_ICON_HINTS includes "icon", which can mis-prefer a document icon
verdicts.detail: Partially verified, downgraded. APP_ICON_HINTS = ["appicon", "app", "icon", "electron"] (apps.rs:32) adds +2 in best_icns (apps.rs:174). 'icon' is indeed a common document-icon stem, so a bundle with a doc icon literally named icon.icns could win +2 over a non-name-matching real app icon. But this is a deliberate dogfood-tuned heuristic (D43: best_icns picks 'a conventional app-icon name, else the largest'); name-match (+3) still beats it, and the report itself marks this low confidence. This is a speculative tuning concern, not a demonstrable bug, so info not warning.
verdicts.fix: Optional: drop "icon" from the hint list (keep appicon/app/electron which are app-specific), or leave as-is — it is a tunable heuristic per D43. If changed, re-run the real_machine_icons ignored test on macOS to confirm icon coverage doesn't regress.
verdicts.evidence: apps.rs:32 hint list, apps.rs:174 +2 scoring; D43 (decisions.md:176) documents best_icns as a deliberate heuristic. No reproducing test case; finding self-rated low confidence.

verdicts.title: Trailing comments restate the obvious control flow
verdicts.detail: Verified. apps.rs:144 `return None; // sips absent (non-macOS) or unreadable icns`, apps.rs:106 `return; // absent / unreadable dir (e.g. on CI Linux) — skip, not fatal.`, and apps.rs:113 `continue; // already have an app by this name` restate what the let-else / seen.insert guards already say. The CI-Linux degradation note appears multiple times across the file (apps.rs:9-10, 68, 106, 144). Info-level.
verdicts.fix: Drop the trailing comments at apps.rs:106, 113, 144 — the `let Ok(..) else { return }` and `seen.insert(...)` guards are self-documenting. State the degrade-to-None invariant once, in the trimmed module header.
verdicts.evidence: apps.rs:106,113,144 trailing comments vs the self-evident guards at apps.rs:105,112; repeated CI-Linux note at apps.rs:9-10,68.

verdicts.title: b64 comment narrates the no-dependency (D43) decision and asserts 'no panics'
verdicts.detail: Verified. apps.rs:206-207 narrates 'hand-rolled to avoid a dependency (the project keeps its supply chain auditable, hard rule 1)... no panics.' — the why-no-crate is a D43 decision and 'no panics' is an assertion, not info. Per the verification brief the hand-rolled base64 is a deliberate D43 choice (do NOT suggest a crate); I confirmed the implementation is correct against RFC 4648 vectors (the b64_matches_known_vectors test at apps.rs:243-250 covers "","f","fo","foo","foob","foobar" — all canonical). Only the comment is bloat.
verdicts.fix: Reduce the doc to /// Standard base64 (RFC 4648). Leave the implementation untouched — it is correct and the no-crate rationale lives in D43. Do NOT replace with a crate.
verdicts.evidence: apps.rs:206-207 comment; D43 (decisions.md:176) records the hand-rolled-base64 / no-new-dep decision; correctness confirmed by RFC-4648 test vectors at apps.rs:243-250.

verdicts.title: extract_icon: the named `ok` bool then immediate branch reads slightly roundabout
verdicts.detail: Verified, minor. apps.rs:135-145 computes `let ok = Command...map(|o| o.status.success()).unwrap_or(false); if !ok { return None; }`. The named ok adds little; a direct guard reads more cleanly. The current form is fully correct and acceptable — this is a low-value readability preference, so info.
verdicts.fix: Optional: collapse to a guard, e.g. `if !Command::new("sips").args([...]).arg(&icns).arg("--out").arg(&png).output().is_ok_and(|o| o.status.success()) { return None; }`. Keep within the `if !is_cached_fresh` block. Ensure clippy (it may suggest is_ok_and) and fmt stay green.
verdicts.evidence: apps.rs:134-146 the let ok = ...; if !ok { return None; } pattern.

verdicts.title: Watcher-status health threshold duplicates capture::ERROR_THRESHOLD as a bare literal
verdicts.detail: Confirmed at lib.rs:248: get_watcher_status returns `healthy: errors < 6` with a bare literal. capture/mod.rs:59 defines `const ERROR_THRESHOLD: u64 = 6` (private), used at the warn site capture/mod.rs:75 `if count == ERROR_THRESHOLD`. The values agree today but are two independent copies of the same tuning boundary; retuning the const would silently leave the UI health flip on the stale `6`. Real single-source-of-truth issue, low severity (the warning is informational, not a correctness path). This is the SAME finding as commands-s0 (the two reviewers flagged identical lines).
verdicts.fix: Make the constant the single source: change capture/mod.rs:59 to `pub const ERROR_THRESHOLD: u64 = 6;` and rewrite lib.rs:248 as `healthy: errors < capture::ERROR_THRESHOLD,`. The const is already module-scoped under `capture`; making it pub is safe and clippy/fmt-clean. Won't break gates.
verdicts.evidence: lib.rs:248 `healthy: errors < 6`; capture/mod.rs:59 `const ERROR_THRESHOLD: u64 = 6` (not pub); capture/mod.rs:75 `if count == ERROR_THRESHOLD`. Not a documented deliberate choice in decisions context.

verdicts.title: get_watcher_status hardcodes `errors < 6`; capture already names ERROR_THRESHOLD = 6
verdicts.detail: Confirmed — this is the identical issue to commands-r0. The literal `6` is at lib.rs:248 (inside get_watcher_status which begins at line 244); ERROR_THRESHOLD = 6 is named at capture/mod.rs:59. Two copies of the same threshold can drift. Reviewer over-stated severity as warning; it is informational — the watcher-status health flag is a UI badge, not a data-path correctness invariant, and the two values are co-located in a small subsystem. Downgrade in spirit but kept confirmed as the same real duplication. Treat as one fix shared with commands-r0.
verdicts.fix: Make capture/mod.rs:59 `pub const ERROR_THRESHOLD: u64 = 6;` and reference it at lib.rs:248: `healthy: errors < capture::ERROR_THRESHOLD`. Delete the literal `6`. Safe; passes fmt/clippy/test.
verdicts.evidence: Same evidence as commands-r0: lib.rs:248 literal `6`; capture/mod.rs:59 named const. Reviewer's 'warning' severity is too high for a non-correctness UI badge; set to info.

verdicts.title: Module-doc block re-narrates `pub` rationale + decision IDs for every mod
verdicts.detail: Confirmed the comments exist: lib.rs:6-29 attach 2-4 line paragraphs to each mod, and the 'why pub (dead_code under -D warnings)' rationale is repeated for db (6-8), capture (10-11), and ai (26-29), with D30/D34/D48 citations. However this is subjective comment-bloat, not a defect, and the project's house style throughout the codebase deliberately cites D-numbers inline (see capture/mod.rs, db.rs etc.) — CLAUDE.md's 'cross-reference from plans' does not forbid inline citations, it is pervasive convention. The `pub`-for-dead_code rationale is genuinely useful context for a reader hitting an otherwise-surprising `pub`. Downgrade to info; not actionable as a bug.
verdicts.fix: Optional: if trimming, state the `pub`-for-dead_code reason once near the file top and keep one-line `///` per module. Keep the D-references — they match repo convention. No change required to pass gates.
verdicts.evidence: lib.rs:6-29 confirmed verbatim. Inline D-number citation is the established pattern across src-tauri/src/. Reviewer severity 'warning' is too high for a stylistic comment preference.

verdicts.title: Identical categories+projects HashMap-building block copied across get_day/get_week/get_timeline
verdicts.detail: Confirmed via grep: the exact two-block read-model setup (`db::get_category_metas(&conn)?...map(|(id,slug,name)| (id, CategoryMeta{slug,name})).collect()` then `db::get_projects(&conn)?...map(|p|(p.id,p.display_name)).collect()`) appears verbatim at get_day (95-102), get_week (123-130), and get_timeline (157-164). No existing helper. Three copies; a shape change touches three handlers. Real low-severity duplication.
verdicts.fix: Extract a helper, e.g. `fn read_models(conn:&Connection) -> Result<(HashMap<i64,rollup::CategoryMeta>,HashMap<i64,String>),AppError>` and call from all three handlers. Pure refactor; SQL stays in db layer (helper only calls existing repo fns), passes hard rule 4, and is clippy/fmt-clean.
verdicts.evidence: grep confirms 3 identical occurrences at lines 95/97, 123/125, 157/159; no helper exists. Not a documented deliberate choice.

verdicts.title: set_retention_days doc over-explains why it isn't update_setting
verdicts.detail: Confirmed the 4-line doc exists at lib.rs:270-273. But this is a subjective preference, not a defect. The doc explains a genuinely non-obvious design point (why retention is a separate command from update_setting — the prune side effect) which is exactly the kind of 'why' a doc-comment should carry, and it states the load-bearing facts (days<=0 = keep forever, returns rows deleted). No code or behavior issue. Dropped as non-actionable stylistic opinion.
verdicts.fix: No change needed. The doc correctly captures the non-obvious rationale plus the behavioral contract.
verdicts.evidence: lib.rs:270-273 confirmed. The 'why separate from update_setting' note is legitimate API documentation, not bloat; the matching set_retention_days body (276-279) does set-then-prune as described.

verdicts.title: Startup cleanup uses println!/eprintln! with `[Startup]` prefixes — ad-hoc logging
verdicts.detail: Confirmed: lib.rs uses println!/eprintln! with hand-prefixed tags at 421 ([Startup]), 423 ([Startup]), 429 ([Startup]), and 448 ([Fatal]). Grep shows this `[Prefix]` println convention is pervasive across the whole codebase (capture/mod.rs:76,241,347,360,363,375,378,383; capture/macos/mod.rs:68,102; polling.rs:34,65; db.rs:170,456; migrations.rs:116). So it is a consistent established convention, not an isolated AI-smell, and adopting a logging facade is a project-wide decision out of scope for this subsystem. Downgrade to info/consistency note; no action this PR.
verdicts.fix: Leave as-is. If a logging facade (`log`/`tracing`) is ever wanted it is a project-wide change, not a command-handler fix. The `[...]` prefix convention is already consistent across files.
verdicts.evidence: grep across src-tauri/src/ shows ~20 println!/eprintln! sites all using the same `[Tag]` prefix convention — confirms it's intentional house style, not localized smell.

verdicts.title: Startup retention-cleanup nesting is 5 levels deep (match → Ok → if let → if let → match)
verdicts.detail: Confirmed nesting at lib.rs:415-430: `match db_conn.lock()` → `Ok(conn)` → `if let Ok(Some(days_str))` → `if let Ok(days)` → `match db::cleanup_old_data`. That is deep. But this is the one-time startup setup closure where early-return is awkward (it returns `Ok(())` for tauri setup), and the logic is correct and readable enough. A flatten (extract `fn run_startup_cleanup(conn:&Connection)` using let-else) is a reasonable nicety but not required. Downgrade to info — readability nit, no defect.
verdicts.fix: Optional: extract `fn run_startup_cleanup(conn:&Connection)` that uses `let Ok(Some(s)) = db::get_setting(...) else { return; }` / `let Ok(days) = s.parse() else { return; }` to cut arrow depth. Keep the outer lock-poison match in setup. Pure refactor; clippy/fmt-clean.
verdicts.evidence: lib.rs:415-430 confirmed 5-deep. Self-contained startup closure; correctness fine. Reviewer's own fix admits the let-else-in-setup is 'awkward'.

verdicts.title: export_bindings doc re-cites hard rule 3 / 'Pattern 8' to justify expect()
verdicts.detail: Confirmed the doc at lib.rs:453-455 explains the expect() is allowed under the test-path exemption. This is defensible: hard rule 3 is a non-negotiable build-failure rule, and an `expect()` sitting in the file is exactly the kind of thing a reviewer would otherwise flag — a one-line note that it's the sanctioned test-only exemption pre-empts that and is good hygiene given the strictness of rule 3. The `#[cfg(test)]` gate proves test-only but does not by itself signal 'this expect is intentionally exempt'. Dropped as a non-actionable style opinion that would slightly reduce clarity around a hard rule.
verdicts.fix: No change needed; the rationale guards against a future reviewer mis-flagging the expect() against hard rule 3.
verdicts.evidence: lib.rs:453-455 + 474 confirmed; the expect() is inside `#[cfg(test)] mod export_bindings`. Hard rule 3 (CLAUDE.md) makes this an area where an explicit exemption note is justified.

verdicts.title: Inline @ts-nocheck explanation duplicates what the header string already says
verdicts.detail: Confirmed: lib.rs:466-468 is a 3-line code comment explaining why `@ts-nocheck` is in the emitted header, and the header literal on line 470 already begins `// @ts-nocheck`. There is some redundancy. However the comment explains the *reason* (the generated file trips strict noUnusedLocals), which the header string itself does not state, so it is not pure duplication. Per the verification context, the @ts-nocheck choice is deliberate (D27) — but documenting *why* at the emit site is reasonable. Minor; downgrade to info, optional trim.
verdicts.fix: Optional: shorten to one line at the .header() call, e.g. `// @ts-nocheck: generated boilerplate trips strict noUnusedLocals (D27)`. Do NOT touch src/bindings.ts (generated). No gate impact.
verdicts.evidence: lib.rs:466-468 comment + 470 header literal confirmed. The comment carries the noUnusedLocals reason absent from the header string, so it's not fully redundant.

verdicts.title: Capture-wiring setup comments restate trait docs + decision rationale
verdicts.detail: Confirmed: lib.rs:434-442 carry comments about main-thread registration (D29), consumer-drains-on-its-own-thread (R57), and consumer-is-sole-writer. The CaptureSource trait doc (capture/mod.rs:48-55) and the module doc (capture/mod.rs:1-8) do already state the main-thread/CFRunLoop and single-writer contracts, so there is duplication. But these are the load-bearing concurrency invariants of the app (why capture runs on the main thread and why the writer is on a dedicated thread, tied to !Send keep-alives per D33 and the deferred WAL writer per R57) — repeating them at the wiring site where the threading actually happens is defensible. Subjective; downgrade to info.
verdicts.fix: Optional: collapse to one line, e.g. `// source registers on the main run loop (D29); consumer is the sole DB writer on its own thread (R57).` Keep at least one citation since the threading model is subtle. No gate impact.
verdicts.evidence: lib.rs:434-442 confirmed; capture/mod.rs:1-8 and 48-55 already document the same contracts. Concurrency-critical site, so some restatement is justified — not a defect.

verdicts.title: useDayData has no out-of-order / stale-response guard — fast day navigation can render the wrong day
verdicts.detail: Confirmed. fetchDay (L22-31) awaits getDay then unconditionally setData (L25); the effect (L33-39) re-runs on [fetchDay, live] (fetchDay is keyed on [startUnix,endUnix]) and a 30s setInterval (L37) also fires it. The effect cleanup only clears the interval (L38), nothing cancels/ignores the in-flight promise. So if the range changes (or a poll for day A resolves) after navigating to day B, A's response lands last and overwrites B. The pattern guard already exists elsewhere in the subsystem: useInstalledApps uses an `alive` flag (useInstalledApps.ts L34-40) and DayView's watcher effect uses `cancelled` (DayView.tsx L42-48) — the three data hooks omit it. Real, low-frequency, visible (header/data desync).
verdicts.fix: Capture a per-run flag in the effect: `let alive = true;` and have fetchDay only setData/setError when alive; set alive=false in cleanup (alongside clearInterval). Best folded into a shared useRangeData (see fe-views-s1) so all three hooks are fixed once. Cosmetic stale data only, no panic/SQL/IPC impact.
verdicts.evidence: useDayData.ts L22-39: setData(await getDay(...)) with cleanup that only clearInterval. Contrast useInstalledApps.ts L34-40 (alive flag) and DayView.tsx L42-48 (cancelled flag) — the guard pattern exists but is absent here.

verdicts.title: useTimelineData shares the same stale-response race as useDayData
verdicts.detail: Confirmed, identical shape. fetchTimeline (L25-34) awaits getTimeline and unconditionally setData (L28); effect (L36-42) re-runs on range change + 30s poll (L40); cleanup only clearInterval (L41). No cancelled/alive guard, so a slow timeline response for a prior day can land after navigation and render under the new header.
verdicts.fix: Mirror the useDayData fix: per-run alive flag gating setData/setError, set false in cleanup. Or extract the shared useRangeData hook (fe-views-s1) to fix all three.
verdicts.evidence: useTimelineData.ts L25-42 — same setData(await ...) + clearInterval-only cleanup as useDayData.

verdicts.title: useWeekData shares the same stale-response race
verdicts.detail: Confirmed. fetchWeek (L23-33) does setData(await getWeek(starts, weekEnd)) (L27) with no guard; effect (L35-41) re-runs on [fetchWeek, live] + 30s poll (L39); cleanup only clearInterval (L40). A slow response for the previous week can land after the user moves week and desync the 7 mini-dials/aggregates from the WeekNav title.
verdicts.fix: Add the same per-run alive guard before setData/setError, set false in cleanup. Or consolidate into useRangeData (fe-views-s1).
verdicts.evidence: useWeekData.ts L23-41 — same pattern; cleanup only clears the interval.

verdicts.title: deepestLabel indexes days[] with backend-provided index without a range check
verdicts.detail: Confirmed as a defensive-info finding. WeekView.tsx L42-45: `data?.deepest_day != null ? days[data.deepest_day].toLocaleDateString(...) : '—'`. bindings.ts L412 types deepest_day as `number | null`, so a non-null but out-of-0..6 value would make days[idx] undefined and throw on .toLocaleDateString. The day slices just below (L96 `const slice = data.days[i]` then `slice?.runs`) already defend against frontend/backend count divergence, so the deepest-day lookup is inconsistent. In practice the backend builds exactly 7, so this is a low-probability guard, not a live bug — info is right.
verdicts.fix: Guard the lookup: `data.deepest_day != null && days[data.deepest_day] ? days[data.deepest_day].toLocaleDateString(undefined,{weekday:'short'}) : '—'`.
verdicts.evidence: WeekView.tsx L42-45 unguarded index vs L96/L127 slice?. guards; bindings.ts L412 `deepest_day: number | null`.

verdicts.title: Capture-health banner only re-checks on day change, not on the 30s poll
verdicts.detail: Confirmed. The getWatcherStatus effect is keyed on [start] (DayView.tsx L49; WeekView.tsx L40 on [dayStarts]; TimelineView.tsx L35 on [start]) — it fires once per navigation but is not part of the 30s data poll (which lives in the useXData hooks). So if capture starts erroring while the user stays on today, captureHealthy stays true and DegradedBanner never appears until they navigate. Real gap, but a degraded-banner latency rather than a correctness/data bug — info.
verdicts.fix: Fold watcher status into the same 30s refresh — either call getWatcherStatus inside the data interval (via a useCaptureHealth hook, see fe-views-s3), or add `healthy` to the day/week/timeline payload so it updates every 30s. Low-risk; touches no SQL/IPC shape if done frontend-side.
verdicts.evidence: DayView.tsx L41-49 keyed [start]; WeekView.tsx L32-40 keyed [dayStarts]; TimelineView.tsx L27-35 keyed [start]; the 30s poll is in the hooks, not these effects.

verdicts.title: Pointless join→split round-trip of dayStarts to make a stable dep key
verdicts.detail: Confirmed. useWeekData.ts L21 `const key = dayStarts.join(',')` then L26 inside fetchWeek `const starts = key.split(',').map(Number)`. The stringify/parse exists only to give useCallback a primitive dep (L33 deps [key, weekEnd]). WeekView already memoizes dayStarts (WeekView.tsx L24 useMemo), so the array identity is stable per week. The number→string→number round-trip is brittle and obscure for no benefit.
verdicts.fix: Keep `key` in the dep list for a stable primitive but call getWeek with the array directly: `setData(await getWeek(dayStarts, weekEnd))` and drop the split. Or, since WeekView memoizes the array, depend on `[dayStarts, weekEnd]` and delete `key` entirely. Verify tsc/vitest still pass (pure refactor).
verdicts.evidence: useWeekData.ts L21 + L26 + L33; WeekView.tsx L24 memoizes dayStarts.

verdicts.title: useDayData / useTimelineData are byte-for-byte identical except the fetch fn and error string
verdicts.detail: Confirmed. useDayData.ts and useTimelineData.ts are structurally identical: same DayDataState/TimelineDataState shape (data/loading/error/refresh), same (start,end,live) signature, same fetchX keyed on [startUnix,endUnix], same effect (setLoading(true)+void fetch+30s interval, cleanup clearInterval), same return object. Only the imported getDay/getTimeline and the fallback string ('Couldn't load this day.' vs 'Couldn't load this timeline.') differ. useWeekData is the same modulo its array key. Three near-identical files means each fix (e.g. the race in r0/r1/r2) must be made three times.
verdicts.fix: Extract a generic `useRangeData<T>(fetcher, deps, live, fallbackMsg)` and make the three hooks one-line wrappers; fold the stale-response guard (r0-r2) into it so all three are fixed at once. Pure-logic refactor — keep vitest coverage green.
verdicts.evidence: Direct read of useDayData.ts (L17-42), useTimelineData.ts (L16-45), useWeekData.ts (L17-44) — same structure, differing only in fetcher + fallback string + week's key.

verdicts.title: No stale-response guard: out-of-order fetches can clobber the current range
verdicts.detail: Confirmed as real but DUPLICATE of fe-views-r0/r1/r2, which already capture this exact race across the three hooks at warning severity from the rules lane. Downgraded to info to avoid double-counting the same defect; track and fix it under r0-r2 (ideally folded into the shared useRangeData of fe-views-s1). The note that DayView's getWatcherStatus effect uses a `cancelled` flag while these hooks don't is accurate (DayView.tsx L42-48).
verdicts.fix: Resolved by the r0/r1/r2 fix: per-run alive flag gating setData/setError, set false in cleanup — best inside the extracted useRangeData (fe-views-s1). No separate change needed.
verdicts.evidence: Same lines as fe-views-r0 (useDayData.ts L22-39); this is the simplify-lane restatement of the rules-lane r0-r2 race.

verdicts.title: Identical getWatcherStatus capture-health effect copy-pasted across all three views
verdicts.detail: Confirmed. The same effect — getWatcherStatus().then(s => !cancelled && setCaptureHealthy(s.healthy)).catch(()=>undefined) with a cancelled flag — plus the same useState(true) and the same <DegradedBanner title="Tracking hit a snag" description="UsageOS ran into repeated errors while recording. Your existing data is safe." actionLabel="Retry" onAction={refresh}/> block appears verbatim in DayView (effect L41-49, banner L70-79), WeekView (effect L32-40, banner L57-66), and TimelineView (effect L27-35, banner L41-50). Three copies of identical logic + user-facing copy.
verdicts.fix: Extract a `useCaptureHealth(rangeKey)` hook returning `healthy` and a `<CaptureDegradedBanner onRetry={refresh}/>` carrying the shared copy; each view drops ~10 lines of effect + ~10 of JSX. Doing this is also the natural place to fix fe-views-r4 (poll health on the 30s interval). Pure-frontend; keep tsc/vitest green.
verdicts.evidence: DayView.tsx L41-49/L70-79, WeekView.tsx L32-40/L57-66, TimelineView.tsx L27-35/L41-50 — identical effect + banner copy.

verdicts.title: ErrorState component is the exact same code in DayView, WeekView, and TimelineView
verdicts.detail:
Confirmed verbatim. ErrorState({message,onRetry}) — dashed-border wrapper, muted message

, 'Try again' button with identical Tailwind classes — is defined three times: DayView.tsx L261-274, WeekView.tsx L205-218, TimelineView.tsx L211-224. Identical down to class strings and copy. A token/microcopy tweak must be made in three places.
verdicts.fix: Promote to src/components/ui/ErrorState.tsx, import in all three views, delete the local copies. Straightforward; keep tsc/vitest green.
verdicts.evidence: DayView.tsx L261-274, WeekView.tsx L205-218, TimelineView.tsx L211-224 — byte-identical function bodies.

verdicts.title: DayNav and TimelineNav are identical day-stepper navs
verdicts.detail: Confirmed. DayView's DayNav (L146-193) and TimelineView's TimelineNav (L141-188) are the same component: same Today/long-date label logic (L157-159 vs L152-154), same refresh/prev/next buttons with identical aria-labels, titles, classes, and addDays(date,±1). Only the function name differs. WeekNav (WeekView.tsx L143-189) is the same pattern with ±7 and week labels. Real duplication; kept at info since the reviewer rated it medium and it is presentational with a small per-view divergence (week step/labels).
verdicts.fix: Extract a shared <DayStepperNav> (parameterize the step + label builder so the week case reuses it) into components/, used by DayView, TimelineView, and optionally WeekView. Removes ~45 duplicated lines. Pure-frontend refactor.
verdicts.evidence: DayView.tsx L146-193 vs TimelineView.tsx L141-188 (identical save the name) and WeekView.tsx L143-189 (same pattern, ±7).

verdicts.title: Ledger onMouseLeave calls onHover(isolated) — passes the live value back, so hover-dim sticks when nothing is pinned
verdicts.detail: Confirmed bug. In Ledger (DayView.tsx L195-231), onHover IS setIsolated (passed L137) and there is a single `isolated` state used for BOTH click-pin (Chip toggleIsolate L64/L125, LedgerRow onClick L225) and hover. onMouseEnter (L223) calls onHover(row.slug) → setIsolated(row.slug), which re-renders Ledger; the new render's onMouseLeave closure (L224) now captures isolated === row.slug, so leaving calls onHover(row.slug) — a no-op. Result: with nothing pinned, hovering a row dims the others and leaving does NOT clear it (the dim sticks). The intent (restore prior state on leave) fails because there is no separate pinned-vs-hovered state to restore to.
verdicts.fix: Separate pinned from transient hover. Simplest: track a `pinned` state (set by click/Chip) and a `hovered` state (set by enter, cleared to null on leave); compute the active isolation as `hovered ?? pinned`. Then onMouseLeave={() => setHovered(null)} correctly clears transient hover while preserving a pin. Logic change — verify the Chip active-state (L124 `active={isolated === c.slug}`) and Dial isolatedSlug (L96) read the combined value.
verdicts.evidence: DayView.tsx L31 single `isolated` state; L137 onHover={setIsolated}; L223 onMouseEnter sets row.slug; L224 onMouseLeave={() => onHover(isolated)} reads the just-updated value → no-op; LedgerRow.tsx L33-34 wires these straight through.

verdicts.title: Leftover 'ctx' naming from the Context→Category rename (D46)
verdicts.detail: Confirmed and squarely against a documented intent. SettingsView.tsx uses `ctx` as the map variable (L157), the CategoryRow prop `ctx: Category` (L280/L284), `ctx.id/ctx.slug/ctx.color/ctx.name` (L288-317), and `ctxRules` (L289, L300, L303). D46 explicitly renamed Context→Category EVERYWHERE — 'UI copy, IPC commands/types, Rust internals, frontend identifiers, and component/file names' — to heal the naming split; `ctx` against a `Category` type is exactly the half-renamed leftover the rename was meant to eliminate. Not a deliberate-keep (the SQL `category_id` legacy name is the only intentionally-retained one; this is a local identifier, fair game).
verdicts.fix: Rename `ctx`→`category` (or `cat`) and `ctxRules`→`categoryRules` in the map callback (L157-163) and CategoryRow (L279-317): e.g. `categories.map((category) => <CategoryRow key={category.id} category={category} rules={rules} onEdit={() => setEditing({category})}/>)`. Pure rename; keep tsc green. Not auto-fixable (identifier rename, not a mechanical fmt/dead-import fix).
verdicts.evidence: grep: SettingsView.tsx L157 map(ctx), L160 ctx={ctx}, L280/284 prop ctx:Category, L288-317 ctx.* + L289/300/303 ctxRules. decisions.md D46 mandates the full rename incl. frontend identifiers.

verdicts.title: Doc comment narrates decision history ('like the old dashboard did')
verdicts.detail: Confirmed minor. useDayData.ts L12-16 ends 'it polls every 30s like the old dashboard did.' — 'like the old dashboard did' documents a superseded design, not current behavior. useTimelineData.ts L12-15 and useWeekData.ts L12-16 also re-explain 'Mirrors useDayData', which becomes redundant once they share a base hook (fe-views-s1).
verdicts.fix: Trim to behavior only: 'Polls every 30s while `live` (today); shows the loading state only on range change, not on background refresh.' Drop 'like the old dashboard did'. If the three hooks are consolidated (fe-views-s1) the 'Mirrors useDayData' lines disappear with them.
verdicts.evidence: useDayData.ts L15 'like the old dashboard did'; useTimelineData.ts L13 / useWeekData.ts L13 'Mirrors useDayData'.

verdicts.title: Comment re-narrates a design rationale on topCategory
verdicts.detail: Real but trimmed-scope. DayView.tsx L55-57 comments topCategory: the first clause ('`categories` is sorted longest-first by the rollup, so [0] is the headline') is a fair load-bearing invariant and should stay; only the trailing justification ('Featuring whatever actually led (vs. a fixed Deep work that's often ~0) keeps the stat honest instead of looking broken') is decision-archaeology. Downgraded the reviewer's framing — keep the invariant, drop only the justification. Low impact.
verdicts.fix: Keep one line: '// `categories` is sorted longest-first by the rollup, so [0] is the day's leading category.' Drop the 'vs a fixed Deep work / looking broken' tail.
verdicts.evidence: DayView.tsx L55-58 — invariant clause + justification clause; only the latter is archaeology.

verdicts.title: Retention-options branch duplicates the same RETENTION.map to append a custom value
verdicts.detail: Confirmed but minor. SettingsView.tsx L85-90: both branches of the ternary call `RETENTION.map(([value,label]) => ({value,label}))`, differing only by appending the custom value when currentRetention isn't a preset. The map is written twice. Reviewer marked warning; downgraded to info — it's a small readability nit on a rare 'custom retention from an older build' path, not a correctness or altitude problem.
verdicts.fix: Compute the base once, append conditionally: `const opts = RETENTION.map(([value,label]) => ({value,label})); const retentionOptions = RETENTION.some(([v]) => v === currentRetention) ? opts : [...opts, {value: currentRetention, label: `${currentRetention} days`}];`
verdicts.evidence: SettingsView.tsx L85-90 — RETENTION.map appears on both L86 and L88.

verdicts.title: map→reverse→map to render runs newest-first is roundabout
verdicts.detail: Confirmed as a real but low-value style nit. TimelineView.tsx L67-79: `data.runs.map((run,i) => ({run,i})).reverse().map(({run,i}) => ...)` allocates an index-tagged array, reverses it, then maps — to iterate runs newest-first while keeping the original index for the gap calc (L72 `run.start - data.runs[i-1].end`). It works and is correct; the intermediate {run,i} objects are avoidable. Already info; reviewer confidence low. Keeping as info — optional cleanup, no behavior change and the proposed alternatives aren't clearly more readable.
verdicts.fix: Optional: iterate descending indices once — `Array.from({length: data.runs.length}, (_, k) => data.runs.length-1-k).map((i) => { const run = data.runs[i]; const gap = i > 0 ? run.start - data.runs[i-1].end : 0; ... })`. Or keep chronological order and flip with CSS column-reverse. Low priority.
verdicts.evidence: TimelineView.tsx L67-79 — map/reverse/map chain with {run,i} tuples.

verdicts.title: Module-level cache + inflight + useState mirror for a session catalog
verdicts.detail: Partly confirmed, scope-reduced. useInstalledApps.ts has a module `cache` (L7), an `inflight` promise singleton (L8-23), and useState(cache ?? []) with an `alive` flag plus an early `if (cache) { setApps(cache); return; }` (L28-41). The inflight dedupe is DEFENSIBLE — there are two callers (CategoryEditorModal.tsx L66, ExclusionModal.tsx L22) and both can plausibly mount, so dropping it is not clearly safe; reject that part. The one fair micro-point is that the early `if (cache)` return (L30-33) is largely covered by the `useState(cache ?? [])` initializer. Low confidence, info.
verdicts.fix: Optional only: the early `if (cache)` return (L30-33) is redundant with the `useState(cache ?? [])` seed and could be removed, keeping the module promise-singleton + inflight (the dedupe earns its keep with two callers). Do NOT drop inflight. Verify vitest if touched.
verdicts.evidence: useInstalledApps.ts L7-41; two callers (CategoryEditorModal.tsx L66, ExclusionModal.tsx L22) justify the inflight dedupe.

verdicts.title: AWAY_MIN_SECS comment cites a backend constant + decision id as cross-ref archaeology
verdicts.detail: Confirmed minor. TimelineView.tsx L12-13: 'Matches the backend's run-split threshold (rollup IDLE_GAP_ENDS_RUN_SECS) — D34a dogfood-tunable.' The load-bearing part is the invariant (this must stay in sync with the backend split threshold); the 'D34a dogfood-tunable' tail is decision-archaeology a reader doesn't need inline. Note IDLE_GAP_ENDS_RUN_SECS is itself a documented dogfood-tunable constant (per the brief), so the *constant* is fine — only the comment tail is the nit. Low confidence/info.
verdicts.fix: Trim to the invariant: '// Gaps at least this long get an Away marker; keep in sync with the backend run-split threshold (rollup IDLE_GAP_ENDS_RUN_SECS).' Drop the 'D34a dogfood-tunable' tail.
verdicts.evidence: TimelineView.tsx L12-14 comment + AWAY_MIN_SECS const.

verdicts.title: Redundant ternary wrapping a pure transform of selectedRun
verdicts.detail: Dropped. DayView.tsx L60-62 is `const inspector: InspectorDetail | null = selectedRun ? buildInspector(selectedRun) : null;` wrapped across three lines. The ternary is necessary (buildInspector requires a non-null run), buildInspector is a named top-level helper used once which the reviewer themselves concede is defensible, and the only actual suggestion is collapsing a line-wrap — which prettier/fmt owns and which would be re-wrapped or left per the formatter anyway. No real simplification or behavior issue; this is noise.
verdicts.fix: None warranted. The multi-line wrap is whatever the formatter produces; leave the ternary as-is.
verdicts.evidence: DayView.tsx L60-62 — a standard null-guarded transform; buildInspector defined L233-243.

verdicts.title: Hardcoded rgba() bar-muted color bypasses the token system
verdicts.detail: Confirmed at line 5: `const BAR_MUTED = "rgba(255,255,255,0.65)"`, applied at line 49 `style={{ color: BAR_MUTED }}` on the TrackingStatus text. Verified by grep it is the ONLY rgba(...) literal in the entire src tree. tokens.css defines `--bar-bg`/`--bar-fg` per theme (paper/warm/black blocks) but no `--bar-muted`. So this component invents a muted-on-dark color inline, outside the token vocabulary. It reads acceptably today (the bar is dark in all three themes) but will drift if the bar palette ever changes per-theme. Note D42 documents that Tailwind opacity modifiers do NOT work on the hex `var()` tokens (text-bar-fg/60 produced an invalid color and made the indicator invisible) — so the simplest `text-bar-fg opacity-XX` is not a drop-in; the proper fix is a real `--bar-muted` token per theme.
verdicts.fix: Add a `--bar-muted` token to each [data-theme] block in src/styles/tokens.css (a muted-on-dark value per theme) and replace `style={{ color: BAR_MUTED }}` with `style={{ color: 'var(--bar-muted)' }}`, deleting the literal. Do NOT use a Tailwind /opacity modifier on a bar-fg token — D42 shows those don't resolve on the hex var() tokens.
verdicts.evidence: TitleBar.tsx:5,49; grep: sole rgba() in src; tokens.css has --bar-fg/--bar-bg but no --bar-muted; D42 notes opacity modifiers fail on hex var() tokens.

verdicts.title: Expanded header labels segment count as "switches", contradicting countSwitches
verdicts.detail: Confirmed. Line 84: `{run.segments.length} app {run.segments.length === 1 ? "switch" : "switches"}` labels the raw segment count as "switches". Lines 53-54 in the collapsed view show `{countSwitches(run.segments)} switches`, and countSwitches (lines 140-148) counts adjacent app/project transitions (for N segments with all-distinct apps that is N-1). So the same run shows different numbers for the word "switch" in the two surfaces (e.g. 3 segments → collapsed "2 switches", expanded "3 app switches"), and the expanded label is semantically off (N segments are N app visits, not N switches). This is the same issue as fe-components-s0/s1.
verdicts.fix: Relabel the expanded header to what it counts, e.g. `{run.segments.length} app {run.segments.length === 1 ? "visit" : "visits"}` (or "session(s)"), OR show `countSwitches(run.segments)` to match the collapsed line. Pick one definition of "switch" and use it in both places.
verdicts.evidence: TimelineRow.tsx:84 uses run.segments.length labeled "switches"; :53-54 uses countSwitches; :140-148 countSwitches counts transitions, not segments.

verdicts.title: Modal does not trap focus or restore focus on close
verdicts.detail: Confirmed. The open effect (lines 21-30) adds Esc-to-close and calls `dialogRef.current?.focus()` (line 28), but never (a) traps Tab inside the dialog nor (b) captures/restores the previously-focused element on close. The container sets `role="dialog" aria-modal="true"` (lines 43-45), which tells assistive tech focus is contained when it is not — Tab can leave the dialog into the page behind the dimmed overlay, and on close focus falls to <body> instead of the trigger. The Modal is the shared shell for CategoryEditorModal/ExclusionModal/DeleteAllModal, so this affects all settings dialogs. This is a real a11y gap, not a documented deliberate choice. Scope: focus-restore is a small, safe addition; full focus-trap is larger.
verdicts.fix: In the open effect, capture `const prev = document.activeElement as HTMLElement | null` before `dialogRef.current?.focus()`, and in the cleanup call `prev?.focus()`. Add a keydown handler intercepting Tab/Shift+Tab to cycle among the dialog's focusable descendants (focus-trap), or adopt a small headless dialog primitive. At minimum restore focus on close.
verdicts.evidence: Modal.tsx:21-30 (focus on open, no restore), :43-45 (aria-modal=true), no Tab handling anywhere in the file.

verdicts.title: Custom listbox has no keyboard navigation
verdicts.detail: Confirmed. The trigger advertises `aria-haspopup="listbox"`/`aria-expanded` (lines 52-53) and the popover is `role="listbox"` (line 64) with `role="option"` children (line 71). The only keyboard handler is Esc-closes (lines 37-39). There is no ArrowUp/ArrowDown to move an active option, no Enter/Space to commit, no Home/End, and no `aria-activedescendant`; options are operable only by mouse click (line 74 onClick). This diverges from RadioGroup (role=radio buttons, focusable) in the same UI folder. It is a genuine keyboard/SR gap for a control presenting as a combobox. Not a documented deliberate choice.
verdicts.fix: Handle ArrowDown/ArrowUp to move an active index, Enter/Space to commit, Home/End to jump, and reflect the active option via `aria-activedescendant` pointing at the focused option's id. Move keyboard focus into the menu on open or keep it on the trigger and drive selection with aria-activedescendant. Keep Esc-closes.
verdicts.evidence: Select.tsx:52-53,61-64,71,74 (mouse-only options, listbox roles); :37-39 (only Esc handled); RadioGroup.tsx is keyboard-operable by contrast.

verdicts.title: Expand header switch count contradicts the collapsed summary count
verdicts.detail: Confirmed and is the same defect as fe-components-r1 (kept separate per instruction to adjudicate each id). Collapsed (line 53) shows `countSwitches(run.segments)` (transitions, gated >1); expanded header (lines 83-84) shows `run.segments.length` labeled "switch/switches". segments.length is the segment count, not the switch count, so the two figures disagree for the same run. One of them mislabels its quantity.
verdicts.fix: Pick one definition. If the header means app sessions, reword to `{run.segments.length} {run.segments.length === 1 ? "app session" : "app sessions"}`. If it means switches, use countSwitches(run.segments) like the collapsed row. Don't label a segment count as a switch count.
verdicts.evidence: TimelineRow.tsx:83-84 (segments.length as "switches"), :53 (countSwitches), :140-148 (transition count).

verdicts.title: countSwitches and the segment-count header compute overlapping concepts inconsistently
verdicts.detail: Confirmed as the duplication/consistency facet of the same bug (r1/s0). countSwitches (lines 140-148) computes transition counts while the expand header independently uses run.segments.length as a 'switch' count — two ad-hoc notions of 'switch' in one file that will keep drifting. Downgraded to info because it is the same underlying issue as r1/s0 (which carry the warning); fixing those resolves this.
verdicts.fix: Define a single helper/value for the count you actually want to show and use it in both the collapsed and expanded views so the number is consistent.
verdicts.evidence: TimelineRow.tsx:140-148 vs :84 — two definitions of 'switch' in one file.

verdicts.title: projectLine duplicates summarizeRun's project-split logic
verdicts.detail: Confirmed. projectLine (lines 119-132) re-implements the project-split-to-text logic that lib/runs.ts summarizeRun (lines 11-18) already does: filter out NO_PROJECT, map to `name duration`, join. Differences are real and divergent: summarizeRun joins with `" · "` while projectLine uses `"  ·  "` (double-spaced), and projectLine adds a `no project <dur>` branch and single/apps-fallback branches summarizeRun lacks. The Dial uses summarizeRun (Dial.tsx:107); the Timeline rolls its own — two copies of 'how we phrase a project split' that will drift. Note: NO_PROJECT is the string "No project" but projectLine pushes the literal lowercase "no project" (line 127) — a separate small inconsistency. Info severity is right.
verdicts.fix: Extend summarizeRun (or add one shared formatter in lib/runs.ts) to cover the multi-bucket / single-project / apps-fallback / no-project cases, and call it from both Dial and TimelineRow so the separator and phrasing can't diverge.
verdicts.evidence: TimelineRow.tsx:119-132 vs runs.ts:11-18; separators "  ·  " vs " · "; Dial.tsx:107 uses summarizeRun.

verdicts.title: Inline style={{ color: 'var(--fg)' }} instead of the text-fg utility
verdicts.detail: Confirmed at line 47: `style={{ color: "var(--fg)" }}` on the app chip span — verified by grep it is the only inline `color: "var(--fg)"` in src/components. It points at the token (so it is not ad-hoc colour, hard rule 7 is not violated) but it is an inconsistent way to apply foreground colour vs the `text-fg` / `text-fg`-class usage elsewhere. Info severity. Mechanically safe to swap.
verdicts.fix: Replace with className `text-fg` and drop the style prop, matching how other components apply foreground colour.
verdicts.evidence: TimelineRow.tsx:47; grep confirms sole inline var(--fg) color in src/components; text-fg utility used throughout.

verdicts.title: Decision-archaeology comment about absorbed detours (D34a)
verdicts.detail: Confirmed. Lines 87-88: `// An absorbed detour (D34a) carries a different category than the run — mark it with // its own colour dot + label so the expand stays honest about what happened.` The code `const isDetour = seg.category_slug !== run.category_slug` (line 89) is largely self-evident; the D34a rationale belongs in decisions.md. Info; a style/comment-bloat nit, fully subjective. Removing/trimming a comment is mechanically safe but not the kind of trivial autofix the gates verify.
verdicts.fix: Trim to a short note: `// detour: segment categorised differently than its run`. Drop the D34a rationale.
verdicts.evidence: TimelineRow.tsx:87-89.

verdicts.title: generatedBy typed as bare string with a magic 'fm' literal
verdicts.detail: Confirmed. RecapCard props: `generatedBy: string` (line 6) and `const isFm = generatedBy === "fm"` (line 11). Only two states exist ('fm' / 'template'). A union type gives compile-time safety and documents the contract — worthwhile since the AI seam (D49) is dormant and easy to get wrong when wired. Caveat for the fix: the value originates from the Rust read-model via generated bindings (Recap in bindings.ts); RecapCard's own prop type is hand-authored and may currently be typed as `string` to match the generated field. Narrowing the prop to a union is safe as long as the call site passes a value assignable to it; verify the caller and the bindings field type before changing, and do NOT edit bindings.ts (generated, D27).
verdicts.fix: Type the prop as a union: `generatedBy: "fm" | "template"`. Keep the same boolean check. If the generated Recap field is a bare string, cast/narrow at the call site rather than editing bindings.ts.
verdicts.evidence: RecapCard.tsx:6,11; generatedBy flows from Rust Recap via bindings (D27 forbids editing bindings.ts).

verdicts.title: Glyph prefixes ⌁ and ≡ on the recap badge read as noise
verdicts.detail: Confirmed at line 24: `{isFm ? "⌁ Summarized on-device" : "≡ Template"}`. The ⌁ and ≡ glyphs are decorative unicode not in the design token set; they add no meaning over the words and render unpredictably across fonts. The badge already distinguishes the two states via border/text colour (line 21: border-c-deep/text-c-deep vs border-rule/text-muted), so the glyphs are redundant. Low-confidence/info — a copy-voice judgement consistent with the project's 'no AI-tell ornamentation' note, but ultimately a design call.
verdicts.fix: Drop the glyphs: `Summarized on-device` / `Template`. The coloured-border treatment already marks the two states.
verdicts.evidence: RecapCard.tsx:24 glyphs; :21 colour border already distinguishes the states.

verdicts.title: Over-narrated doc comment on AppIcon
verdicts.detail: Confirmed. Lines 14-17 are a 4-line block comment covering the monogram fallback, the shared icon map, and webview decode behaviour ('the same data-URI src is reused across rows, so the webview decodes one copy regardless of count'). The decode-sharing detail is about loadIconMap/appIcons.ts, not this component. Note this detail is also already recorded in decisions.md (D44) and restated in UncategorizedApps — so it is genuinely redundant here. Info, subjective comment nit.
verdicts.fix: Cut to one line, e.g. `/** App icon resolved by name, with a first-letter monogram fallback. */`. The map-sharing note belongs (if anywhere) in appIcons.ts.
verdicts.evidence: AppIcon.tsx:14-17; the decode-sharing fact is in decisions.md D44 and is about loadIconMap.

verdicts.title: Inline comments restating obvious resolve logic
verdicts.detail: Confirmed. Lines 19-20 ('Resolve synchronously when the map is already loaded (no flash when re-entering a view); otherwise load it and update.') and line 20 ('`undefined` means "not loaded yet".') narrate code that is fairly clear from resolveIcon's return handling (the useState initializer at line 21 and the `if (ready !== undefined)` at line 25). Mild belt-and-suspenders. Low/info — subjective. The 'undefined means not loaded' note has a sliver of value since resolveIcon's tri-state (string|null|undefined) is non-obvious, so trimming rather than fully deleting is safer.
verdicts.fix: Trim the synchronous-resolve sentence; optionally keep a short note that resolveIcon returns undefined when the map isn't loaded yet (its tri-state isn't obvious).
verdicts.evidence: AppIcon.tsx:19-21,25 — resolveIcon tri-state string|null|undefined.

verdicts.title: PALETTE hardcodes hex that duplicates theme tokens (rule 7)
verdicts.detail: Dropped as a hard-rule-7 violation. Confirmed the literal at line 24: `const PALETTE = ["#1B45BE", "#E0241B", "#EAB308", "#7A4FC2", "#1D9E75", "#161616"]`. But this is the documented deliberate model, not ad-hoc colour: D35 establishes 'NULL slug = a user-created context with its own hex `color`', and design-system.md line 17 fixes colour to the canonical category vocabulary — user categories carry their OWN stored hex, by design, and intentionally do NOT follow theme tokens (a user picks a fixed colour). The canonical categories take their theme token instead (line 83: `category?.slug ? categoryColorVar(...) : color`), exactly as the comment on line 23 says. The finding's own fix text concedes it 'may be intentional'. So the fixed-hex palette is correct by D35/D34. The only residual is that the first four happen to echo the paper-theme token hexes — a cosmetic duplication, not a rule-7 break. Not worth a change.
verdicts.fix: No change required — user-category colours are stored hex by design (D35), independent of theme tokens (design-system.md L17). If desired purely cosmetically, the four overlapping swatches could be sourced from a shared constant, but this is optional and low-value.
verdicts.evidence: CategoryEditorModal.tsx:23-24,83; D35 ('NULL slug = user-created context with its own hex color'); design-system.md:17 (colour = canonical category vocabulary).

verdicts.title: Inline color-mix background instead of a token
verdicts.detail: Confirmed at line 270: `style={{ background: "color-mix(in srgb, var(--c-research) 8%, transparent)" }}` on the conflict-warning panel — a one-off computed tint. It builds on a token (`var(--c-research)`) so it is not ad-hoc hex and does not break hard rule 7; it is just a bespoke inline value used nowhere else. Grep confirms it is the only color-mix in the subsystem. Info, not warning — minor inconsistency, the 2px red border already carries the warning signal. Note D42 documents that Tailwind /opacity modifiers don't work on the hex var() tokens, so the suggested `bg-c-research/[0.08]` utility would NOT resolve — the inline color-mix is actually the working way to tint a token; keep it or drop the tint, but do not replace with a Tailwind opacity utility.
verdicts.fix: Either drop the tint (the 2px red border already signals the warning) or leave the inline color-mix as-is. Do NOT swap to `bg-c-research/[0.08]` — Tailwind opacity modifiers don't resolve on the hex var() tokens (D42).
verdicts.evidence: CategoryEditorModal.tsx:269-270; grep: sole color-mix in subsystem; D42 (opacity modifiers fail on hex var() tokens).

verdicts.title: Defensive 'Missing category id' throw for an impossible case
verdicts.detail: Confirmed at line 188: `if (id == null) throw new Error("Missing category id");`. `id` is `category?.id` (number|undefined); in the edit branch category is non-null (line 183) and in the create branch id is assigned from createCategory (line 186). So in practice id is set on both paths — the throw guards a near-impossible state. However the throw is NOT pure ceremony: it also narrows the type from `number | undefined` to `number` so the subsequent `createRule(id, ...)` calls (line 189) typecheck. Removing it outright would likely break tsc (a merge gate) unless replaced with another narrowing. Downgraded to info; this is a defensible (if slightly defensive) pattern, not a clear smell.
verdicts.fix: Optional: replace the runtime throw with type narrowing tied to the existing branches (e.g. assign id inside each branch so TS sees it as number), or leave as-is. Do not simply delete the throw — `createRule(id,...)` at line 189 needs id narrowed to number or tsc fails.
verdicts.evidence: CategoryEditorModal.tsx:182-189; id is number|undefined and createRule needs number → the throw also serves as a type-narrowing guard.

verdicts.title: Sequential awaits in for-loops serialize independent rule writes
verdicts.detail: Confirmed at lines 189-191: three sequential `for ... await` loops (createRule for added, deleteRule for removedIds, deleteRule for movedIds) followed by reprocessLogs (line 192). These are independent IPC calls serialized one-at-a-time, so save is slower than necessary. However: (1) rule counts here are tiny (a handful per category edit), so the real-world latency win is negligible; (2) parallelizing writes against the Arc<Mutex<Connection>> backend (D31/D42 interim) gives no DB-level concurrency anyway — they serialize at the mutex regardless; (3) there could be a subtle ordering consideration if createRule and deleteRule on the same pattern interact, though here added/removed/moved are disjoint sets. Downgraded to info — a real but low-value micro-optimization, not a correctness issue.
verdicts.fix: Optional: batch with Promise.all over the three lists, then reprocessLogs once. Verify the backend doesn't require ordering; the DB mutex means the latency win is marginal, so this is low priority.
verdicts.evidence: CategoryEditorModal.tsx:189-192; DB is Arc<Mutex<Connection>> (D31/D42) so writes serialize at the mutex regardless.

verdicts.title: InspectorDetail indirection for a single caller
verdicts.detail: Partially confirmed. DetailInspector takes a pre-shaped InspectorDetail (colorVar/title/subtitle/durationLabel/rangeLabel), a null empty state, and a configurable `emptyHint` with a default (lines 11-21). The component is a thin formatting shell. I could not verify the 'single caller' claim within the reviewed file set (the caller wasn't read), so the over-engineering severity is speculative. The concrete, verifiable nit is only the `emptyHint` prop carrying a default that may have one consumer. Downgraded to info; do not rewrite the component on an unverified caller count.
verdicts.fix: If a grep confirms exactly one caller that always uses the default, inline the empty-hint string and drop the `emptyHint` prop. Otherwise leave as-is — a small presentational component with a typed prop is fine.
verdicts.evidence: DetailInspector.tsx:1-21; caller count not verified in the reviewed files.

verdicts.title: Tag and ModePill primitives may be single-use leftovers
verdicts.detail: Confirmed as dead/unused exports. Tag (lines 90-97) and ModePill (lines 100-112) are exported from primitives.tsx with comments tying them to 'the exclusions list'. Grep across src/components for `\bTag\b` / `\bModePill\b` (excluding primitives.tsx) returns NO matches — neither is imported or rendered anywhere; ExclusionModal builds its own pills inline. So these are speculative primitive extraction / dead code. Info severity (dead exports, not a bug). Removal is low-risk but is not a trivially-safe mechanical autofix (it deletes exported API and could in principle be referenced by tests/stories not in this set — verify first).
verdicts.fix: Grep the whole repo (incl. tests/stories) to confirm zero references, then delete Tag and ModePill from primitives.tsx. If a future exclusions list needs them, reintroduce when there's a real caller.
verdicts.evidence: primitives.tsx:90-97,100-112; grep for Tag/ModePill outside primitives.tsx → no matches in src/components.

verdicts.title: Hardcoded rgba muted-on-dark instead of a token
verdicts.detail: Confirmed — this is the same line as fe-components-r0 (line 5, `const BAR_MUTED = "rgba(255,255,255,0.65)"`), viewed through the simplify lane. tokens.css defines `--bar-fg` per theme but no muted variant, so this hardcodes a white that may not match `--bar-fg` in every theme. Info here since r0 already carries the warning. IMPORTANT correction to this finding's fix: the suggested `text-bar-fg opacity-70` does not work as intended — D42 documents that Tailwind opacity modifiers do not resolve on the hex var() tokens (the indicator was once invisible for exactly this reason). The correct fix is a real `--bar-muted` token (see r0).
verdicts.fix: Add a `--bar-muted` token per theme in tokens.css and use `style={{ color: 'var(--bar-muted)' }}` (per r0). Do NOT use `text-bar-fg opacity-XX` — opacity modifiers don't resolve on the hex var() tokens (D42).
verdicts.evidence: TitleBar.tsx:5; tokens.css no --bar-muted; D42 (opacity modifiers fail on hex tokens). Same line as r0.

verdicts.title: SWATCH hardcodes per-theme bg hex (acknowledged but duplicative)
verdicts.detail: Confirmed the literals at lines 6-10: `SWATCH: Record<Theme,string>` = #EEEBE1/#1A1916/#0E0E0E — exact duplicates of the three themes' `--bg` values in tokens.css (verified: paper #EEEBE1, warm #1A1916, black #0E0E0E). The comment (lines 4-5) correctly justifies the literal: each swatch must display theme X's colour regardless of the active theme, so it cannot use `var(--bg)` (which resolves to the active theme). So the literal is genuinely required — this is NOT a hard-rule-7 violation. The only residual is the values live in two places and could silently drift. Info, low priority; do not 'fix' by switching to var(--bg) which would break the feature.
