verdicts.fix: Optional: source these three from a single shared theme-metadata constant (alongside THEMES/THEME_LABELS) so tokens.css and this map can't diverge. Do NOT replace with var(--bg) — the swatches must show each theme regardless of the active one.
verdicts.evidence: ThemeSwitcher.tsx:4-10; tokens.css --bg = #EEEBE1/#1A1916/#0E0E0E across the three theme blocks.

verdicts.title: Decision-archaeology references (R77) scattered in Dial comments
verdicts.detail: Confirmed. The Dial doc comment (line 43, '...category-run arcs with a 1px casing (R77)...') and the arc comment (line 155, 'a 1px ink casing (R77) behind the coloured stroke') both cite the risk-register id R77. MiniDial.tsx carries the same R77 tags (its CASING_W comment: '1px of ink either side (R77)'). The visual intent ('ink casing behind the stroke') is the useful part; the R77 tag is decision-archaeology that belongs in the risk register/decisions. Info, subjective comment nit.
verdicts.fix: Drop the (R77) tags in Dial.tsx (and MiniDial.tsx for consistency); keep the plain description of what the casing is.
verdicts.evidence: Dial.tsx:43,155 and MiniDial.tsx:9 all cite R77 in comments.

verdicts.title: Arc-building geometry duplicated between Dial and MiniDial
verdicts.detail: Confirmed. Dial.arcs (lines 60-73) and MiniDial.arcs (MiniDial.tsx lines 26-33) run the same algorithm: minutesSinceMidnight on run.start/end, `trim = endMin - startMin > TRIM*2`, slice by TRIM, `arcPath(...)`, `categoryColorVar(run.category_slug)`. Only the centre/radius/trim constants differ, and the trim constant already silently differs (Dial ARC_GAP_MIN=4, MiniDial TRIM_MIN=3) — exactly the kind of drift the finding predicts. A shared `buildArcs` helper would keep the two dials in lockstep. Info severity (quality/duplication, not a bug). Reasonable cleanup.
verdicts.fix: Extract a `buildArcs(runs, { cx, cy, r, trimMin, dayStartUnix })` helper in lib/geometry.ts and call it from both Dial and MiniDial. Note the trim values currently differ (4 vs 3) intentionally per dial size — pass them as params, don't unify the value.
verdicts.evidence: Dial.tsx:60-73 vs MiniDial.tsx:26-33 — same trim-and-build algorithm; ARC_GAP_MIN=4 (Dial) vs TRIM_MIN=3 (MiniDial).

verdicts.title: Radio dot fill hardcodes c-deep, ignoring selectable accent
verdicts.detail: Confirmed the selected radio dot uses `bg-c-deep` (line 36, inside the span at line 35). This is a token class, not ad-hoc hex, so hard rule 7 is satisfied — the finding itself flags this only as a consistency observation, not a violation. The suggestion to introduce an `--accent` token aliasing `--c-deep` so the active-control colour lives in one place is a reasonable but speculative refactor (the finding does not verify how many primitives repeat the literal). Downgraded to info; current usage is token-based and correct, no action required.
verdicts.fix: Optional/low-priority: introduce an `--accent` token aliasing `--c-deep` and use it across active-control primitives (radio dot, toggle, focus ring) so the accent is changeable in one place. Not required — current usage is correct.
verdicts.evidence: RadioGroup.tsx:35-36 bg-c-deep (a token class, not hex).

verdicts.title: Comment re-narrates D47 suggestion rationale
verdicts.detail: Confirmed. Lines 30-32: '// The app catalog drives the category suggestion (from each app's Apple-assigned // category, D47). Load once, then re-render so suggestions appear.' This restates a decision (the LSApplicationCategoryType suggestion, recorded as D47/D47-era in decisions.md) and narrates the obvious useEffect-loads-then-setState pattern below (lines 33-39). Info, subjective comment nit. (Minor: the citation says D47 but the suggestion mechanism is documented in decisions.md around the same entry — either way the rationale belongs there, not inline.)
verdicts.fix: Trim to `// Load the app catalog so we can suggest a category.` and drop the D47 reference.
verdicts.evidence: UncategorizedApps.tsx:30-39; the suggestion-from-Apple-category rationale lives in decisions.md (D47-era entry).

verdicts.title: Focus-on-open comment over-explains a one-liner
verdicts.detail: Confirmed at line 27: `// Land focus inside the dialog so Esc works and SR users enter the modal.` explaining `dialogRef.current?.focus()` (line 28). Mildly verbose; the SR-entry note is the only non-obvious part. Info, subjective. Worth noting this comment becomes partly inaccurate if fe-components-r2 (focus trap/restore) is addressed, so trimming/updating it pairs naturally with that fix.
verdicts.fix: Shorten to `// Focus the dialog so Esc + screen-reader entry work.` or fold into the r2 focus-management change.
verdicts.evidence: Modal.tsx:27-28.

verdicts.title: Day vs Week use inconsistent day-end semantics; Week can double-count a midnight event
verdicts.detail: Verified end-to-end. dayBounds (dates.ts:7) returns end = local 23:59:59. WeekView.tsx:24-25 passes the 7 dayBounds().start values and weekEnd=dayBounds(days[6]).end into getWeek. Rust get_week (lib.rs:133) sets each interior day's end to `day_starts.get(i+1)` = the NEXT day's local midnight. db::get_activity_logs (db.rs:264) filters `start_time >= ?1 AND start_time <= ?2` — INCLUSIVE on both ends. So an event whose start_time is exactly local midnight T is included in day i's slice ([start_i, T], T<=T) AND day i+1's slice ([T, end], T>=T). build_week_view (rollup.rs:261) sums per-day active_secs, so that event inflates the week total and is double-counted. The single Day view (get_day, end=23:59:59) avoids it only because 23:59:59 is one integer-second short of midnight and timestamps are integer seconds. Real but rare (requires a capture event with start_time at exactly 00:00:00 local). This is NOT the intended dial-vs-ledger divergence of D40 (that is about excursion-absorb), so D40 does not cover it.
verdicts.fix: Adopt one half-open convention across both views. Cleanest: make dayBounds return end = next-local-midnight (exclusive) and change the Rust filter to `start_time >= ?1 AND start_time < ?2` (get_week already passes next-midnight as interior end, so it becomes correct; get_day's caller passes the same exclusive end). Add a test asserting sum over Week day-slices == 7 standalone Day totals for the same dates. Note: this touches src-tauri/src/db.rs:264 SQL (allowed — repository layer, not generated bindings) and must keep get_day's end consistent; existing db.rs tests use distinct non-boundary timestamps so they will not break.
verdicts.evidence: db.rs:264 `WHERE start_time >= ?1 AND start_time <= ?2` (inclusive both ends); lib.rs:133 `let end = day_starts.get(i + 1).copied().unwrap_or(week_end)`; rollup.rs:261 build_week_view sums day active_secs; dates.ts:7 end=23:59:59; WeekView.tsx:24-25 caller. D40 covers excursion-absorb, not boundary inclusivity, so not a documented deliberate choice.

verdicts.title: Prefix icon match can resolve a process to an unrelated app (e.g. slack → slackdownloader)
verdicts.detail: Verified. resolve() (appIcons.ts:71-73) fuzzy fallback iterates `for (const [k, v] of map)` and returns the first entry where `k.length >= 3 && (k.startsWith(key) || key.startsWith(k))`. The comment's QR-Code/Code guard holds (neither is a prefix of the other), but a genuine prefix collision — 'slack' vs an installed 'slackmoji'/'slackdownloader', or 'notes' vs 'notesync' — returns the wrong icon. Map iteration order follows insertion = the order list_installed_apps returned, so the wrong pick is nondeterministic across machines. Worst case is a cosmetically wrong icon, never data corruption — info is right. Same code path is reused by resolveSuggestedSlug (appIcons.ts:93), so a bad prefix collision could also surface a wrong suggested-category slug there (still advisory only — never auto-assigns, per D44).
verdicts.fix: Prefer the shortest matching candidate and break ties deterministically by sorted name, so 'slack'→'slack' beats 'slack'→'slackdownloader'; or only accept a prefix match when the longer name's next char is a boundary. Keep it conservative; do not regress the iTerm2↔iTerm case the test covers.
verdicts.evidence: appIcons.ts:71-73 loop returns first prefix match in Map insertion order; resolve() shared by resolveIcon (l.82) and resolveSuggestedSlug (l.93).

verdicts.title: processOwner skips empty-pattern rules; Rust find_category would treat them as match-all
verdicts.detail: Verified divergence. processOwner (ruleMatch.ts:15) guards `if (r.pattern && name.includes(...))`, so an empty pattern never matches. db::find_category (db.rs:GET via get_rules then `match_target.to_lowercase().contains(&rule.pattern.to_lowercase())`) has no empty guard, and Rust str::contains("") is true — an empty-pattern PROCESS rule would own every app from its id onward. create_rule (db.rs create_rule / lib.rs) does no empty-pattern validation. So if an empty-pattern process rule ever existed, the editor's conflict warning would disagree with what reprocess_logs actually does — a true break of D44's 'mirror the Rust engine, engine is source of truth' contract. Defensive, not a live bug: every UI path that builds rules derives/trims a non-empty pattern, and a user cannot save a blank one through the normal flow. Hence info.
verdicts.fix: Pick one engine-aligned behavior and document it next to the D44 note: either reject empty/whitespace patterns at the Rust create_rule boundary (so the state can't exist on either side — preferred, since match-all is never a useful rule), or drop the `r.pattern &&` short-circuit so the mirror matches find_category's match-all. Do not change find_category's semantics alone, as that would silently make blank rules match-all in the engine.
verdicts.evidence: ruleMatch.ts:15 `if (r.pattern && name.includes(...))` vs db.rs find_category `match_target.to_lowercase().contains(&rule.pattern.to_lowercase())` with no empty guard; create_rule (db.rs) inserts pattern unchanged; D44 states the frontend mirror must match the engine.

verdicts.title: Misleading "exclusive end" doc for getWeek contradicts dayBounds
verdicts.detail: Verified. tauri.ts:81 JSDoc says "`weekEnd` is the exclusive end of the last day." The only caller (WeekView.tsx:25) passes `dayBounds(days[6]).end` = 23:59:59, and the Rust query (db.rs:264) is inclusive (`start_time <= ?2`). So weekEnd is an inclusive 23:59:59 bound, not exclusive — the comment describes a contract the code doesn't implement. A future reader passing a true exclusive midnight would silently shift behavior. The SAME wrong 'exclusive end' wording also appears in the Rust doc at lib.rs:113, so this is a two-site doc lie, not one line. (If fe-lib-r0/s1 are fixed by moving to a real exclusive-midnight half-open convention, this comment becomes true instead — coordinate the two.)
verdicts.fix: Correct both docs to match current reality: "`weekEnd` is the inclusive upper bound (Unix secs) of the last day (23:59:59), matching `dayBounds().end`." If the team instead fixes the semantics (fe-lib-r0), make the code exclusive and leave the doc as-is. Either way the doc must not lie.
verdicts.evidence: tauri.ts:81 doc 'exclusive end'; WeekView.tsx:25 passes dayBounds().end=23:59:59; db.rs:264 inclusive `<= ?2`; lib.rs:113 repeats 'the exclusive end' in Rust doc.

verdicts.title: dayBounds end of 23:59:59 drops the final second of the day
verdicts.detail: Verified the mechanism but this finding substantially overlaps fe-lib-r0/s0 — it is the same 23:59:59-vs-next-midnight seam viewed from the Day side. The 23:59:59 end plus inclusive `start_time <= ?2` (db.rs:264) means an event whose start_time is in the final second (a start exactly at 23:59:59 IS included; only a start at the next 00:00:00 is excluded), and getWeek uses next-midnight as the interior boundary, so the two views place the boundary one second apart. The concrete, actionable harm (Week double-count) is already captured as the confirmed fe-lib-r0; standing alone this is a presentation of the same root cause, so downgrade to info to avoid double-counting the finding. The detail also slightly overstates: a start at exactly 23:59:59 is included (<=), and integer-second timestamps mean there is no '23:59:59.x' rounding case.
verdicts.fix: Resolve via the single fix in fe-lib-r0 (one canonical half-open boundary: end = next-local-midnight, Rust filter `start_time < ?2`). No separate change needed once r0 lands.
verdicts.evidence: dates.ts:7 end=...23,59,59; db.rs:264 inclusive; lib.rs:133 interior end = next midnight. Same root cause as fe-lib-r0 which is confirmed-warning.

verdicts.title: Redundant re-sort of rules already returned id-ordered
verdicts.detail: Verified. ruleMatch.ts:12 does `[...rules].sort((a, b) => a.id - b.id)`. The only source of these rules is getRules() (tauri.ts:115) → commands.getRules → db::get_rules (db.rs) whose SQL is `... FROM rules ORDER BY id ASC`. So input is already id-ordered; the copy+sort duplicates a guaranteed invariant and adds an allocation + sort on every editor keystroke. Minor (info) — a handful of rules, negligible cost — but genuinely redundant.
verdicts.fix: Iterate `rules` directly: `for (const r of rules) { ... }`. If defending against unordered callers is desired, a one-line comment noting getRules() returns id-ordered is enough; the copy+sort is not.
verdicts.evidence: ruleMatch.ts:12 sorts; db.rs:360-ish get_rules SQL `ORDER BY id ASC`; tauri.ts:115 getRules is the only producer.

verdicts.title: Header comment re-narrates design rationale that lives in decisions.md
verdicts.detail: Verified. The header (ruleMatch.ts:3-6) re-argues the D44 rationale and contains a now-stale claim: "this never sorts anything" — yet line 12 literally sorts. The 'why' lives in D44; the code needs only what it mirrors. Note: if fe-lib-s2 is taken (remove the sort), the 'never sorts anything' clause becomes accurate again — but it is still rationale that belongs in decisions.md, so trimming is the right call either way.
verdicts.fix: Trim to one line, e.g.: "// Frontend mirror of Rust db::find_category for PROCESS rules (D44): case-insensitive substring, first-by-id wins. Title rules excluded (they match window titles, not app names)." Drop the 'never sorts anything' sentence.
verdicts.evidence: ruleMatch.ts:3-6 header vs ruleMatch.ts:12 sort; D44 already documents the rationale.

verdicts.title: JSDoc @param/@returns ceremony on a one-line wrapper
verdicts.detail: Verified. getActivityStats (tauri.ts:56-62) carries a full @param startTime / @param endTime / @returns block restating the typed signature, while every sibling wrapper in the same file (getDay l.70, getWeek l.78, getTimeline l.87, etc.) uses a single terse line. Inconsistent and redundant with the types. Cosmetic — info.
verdicts.fix: Replace with a one-liner matching neighbors, e.g. `/** Raw activity logs for a [start, end] Unix-second range. */`, and drop the @param/@returns lines.
verdicts.evidence: tauri.ts:56-62 verbose JSDoc vs single-line docs on getDay/getWeek/getTimeline in the same file.

verdicts.title: 9-line file-header narrates the whole matching design
verdicts.detail: Verified. appIcons.ts:1-9 walks through exact→alias→prefix, the QR-Code/Code anecdote, monogram fallback, and the decode-sharing optimization. The prefix detail is re-explained inline at resolve() (l.69-70) and the alias at ALIAS (l.17-21). The 'shared data-URIs mean the webview decodes one copy per unique icon' sentence is design-archaeology that doesn't help a reader of this file. Cosmetic — info.
verdicts.fix: Cut to ~2 lines: "// Map a capture process_name to an installed app's icon (data-URI from list_installed_apps, loaded once). Match: exact → alias → conservative prefix; unresolved names fall back to a monogram." Let the inline comment on resolve() carry the prefix detail.
verdicts.evidence: appIcons.ts:1-9 header; resolve() inline comment l.69-70 duplicates prefix rationale; ALIAS l.17-21.

verdicts.title: Two near-identical resolver caches/maps that could share one loop
verdicts.detail: Verified the duplication exists: loadedMap/resolveCache/resolveIcon (l.24-25, 79-85) and loadedSuggestMap/suggestCache/resolveSuggestedSlug (l.28-29, 90-96) share the same lazy-load gate and the same `if cache.has → return; if !loaded → undefined; else resolve+cache` shape, differing only in which map they read. resolve(map, name) is already factored. But this is a ~12-line readability cleanup of clearly-correct code, not over-engineering or a defect — calling it 'warning/over-engineering' overstates it. Downgrade to info. The two public functions could become thin wrappers over a `lookup(loaded, cache, name)` helper, but the current form is also perfectly clear; treat as optional tidy-up.
verdicts.fix: Optional: extract `function lookup(loaded: Map<string,string> | null, cache: Map<string, string|null>, name: string): string|null|undefined` and have resolveIcon/resolveSuggestedSlug delegate. Keep loadIconMap populating both maps as now. Verify tsc + vitest stay green; behavior must be byte-identical.
verdicts.evidence: appIcons.ts:79-85 vs 90-96 are structurally identical around the shared resolve(); no correctness issue, pure readability.

verdicts.title: "Ported from design/day.html" provenance note is archaeology
verdicts.detail: Verified. geometry.ts:3 "Ported from design/day.html so the React dial matches the frozen mockup exactly." is provenance, not a live invariant. Line 1-2 (24h clock, midnight top, clockwise, D14) is genuinely useful and should stay. Cosmetic — info.
verdicts.fix: Keep the D14 invariant line; delete the 'Ported from design/day.html...' sentence (provenance belongs in the commit/decisions).
verdicts.evidence: geometry.ts:1-3 header; line 1 carries the useful D14 invariant, line 3 is provenance.

verdicts.title: Over-explained docstring on a 5-line formatter
verdicts.detail: Verified. runs.ts:7-11 explains the D34 bar-vs-text rationale and the off-project omission for summarizeRun, which is a trivial filter+map+join (l.13-17). The behavior is obvious from the code; the 'never a bar — D34' rationale belongs in decisions.md. Cosmetic — info.
verdicts.fix: Trim to one line: "/** Project split as a text line (off-project omitted) + the run's unique app list. */". Drop the D34 narration.
verdicts.evidence: runs.ts:7-11 docstring vs l.13-17 body (filter NO_PROJECT, map, join).

verdicts.title: Two stacked header blocks re-arguing D34/D46/D47 above 13 lines of data
verdicts.detail: Verified. categories.ts:1-10 is a prose essay (colour-encodes-category-not-project, slugs-are-identity D46, names-are-defaults D47, the full deep→Work etc. mapping) sitting directly above CANONICAL_CATEGORIES (l.12-18) which IS that mapping. The table is self-documenting; the essay restates it plus three decision IDs. Cosmetic — info.
verdicts.fix: Collapse to one line above the table: "// Five canonical categories (D34/D46): slug = stable identity, name = default label. Colour encodes category only; others → --muted." Delete the duplicated deep→Work prose.
verdicts.evidence: categories.ts:1-10 prose vs the CANONICAL_CATEGORIES array l.12-18 that encodes the same slug→name mapping.

verdicts.title: Array .includes for a fixed 5-slug membership check
verdicts.detail: Verified. categories.ts:20 derives `CANONICAL_SLUGS = CANONICAL_CATEGORIES.map(c => c.slug)` (an array) solely so categoryColorVar (l.23) can do `CANONICAL_SLUGS.includes(slug)` — a linear scan over 5 items on every call. A Set is the idiomatic membership structure. Truly minor (5 elements) — info.
verdicts.fix: `const CANONICAL_SLUGS = new Set(CANONICAL_CATEGORIES.map((c) => c.slug));` then `CANONICAL_SLUGS.has(slug)`. Confirm no other module imports CANONICAL_SLUGS expecting an array (grep first); it is not exported here, so the change is local.
verdicts.evidence: categories.ts:20 builds the array; l.23 `CANONICAL_SLUGS.includes(slug)`. CANONICAL_SLUGS is module-private (not exported).

verdicts.title: Defensive Math.max(0, Math.floor()) guards an impossible negative duration
verdicts.detail: Verified the code: format.ts:6 `const total = Math.max(0, Math.floor(secs));`. Durations originate from Rust aggregates (hard rule 6), which are non-negative integer-second counts, so the negative clamp guards a state the data contract makes unreachable. However, removing a harmless defensive clamp from a pure display formatter is a near-zero-value change and the 'ai-smell' framing is a stylistic opinion, not a defect — keeping it costs nothing and protects against a future non-Rust caller. Downgrade to info; treat as optional. Note `Math.floor` itself is still wanted (drops fractional seconds), so only the `Math.max(0, ...)` is in question.
verdicts.fix: Optional: `const total = Math.floor(secs);` if you want to drop the clamp. Given the negligible benefit and the harmlessness, leaving it is also fine. Do not remove Math.floor.
verdicts.evidence: format.ts:6 clamp; hard rule 6 + Rust u/i64 second aggregates make negative input unreachable in the data path; pure display function, no correctness impact either way.

verdicts.title: --serve loop sends unvalidated/blank stdin lines straight to the model
verdicts.detail: Confirmed. Line 97 `while let line = readLine(strippingNewline: true)` and line 98 `await runRecap(prompt: line)` pass each stdin line verbatim into runRecap → session.respond with zero validation (no empty/whitespace/length check). A blank line triggers a full warm model call (~1-2s per the README) with no facts to anchor, so result.content.text would be empty or fabricated prose — the exact failure mode hard rule 6 guards against, except with no number to anchor. The one-in/one-out invariant is preserved (every line still yields one ok/error/unavailable line), so this is robustness not protocol desync. This is the same finding as sidecar-s8 (it cites line 49/98 of the same loop). Correctly scoped as info: this is the D49 spike, and the README explicitly defers real facts-line JSON validation to the wired sidecar — the value here is carrying a blank-input guard into chunk B–D so Rust falls back to the deterministic D48 template rather than getting fabricated prose.
verdicts.fix: In the --serve loop guard blank input before calling the model: `let trimmed = line.trimmingCharacters(in: .whitespacesAndNewlines); guard !trimmed.isEmpty else { emitLine(["status": "error", "detail": "empty input"]); continue }`. Carry this guard into the real sidecar so Rust falls back to the deterministic template on empty/malformed facts.
verdicts.evidence: main.swift:97-98 pass raw line to runRecap with no guard; runRecap (line 49-72) feeds prompt straight to session.respond. README (line 21) documents protocol as 'one JSON-facts line in' and defers real validation to the wired sidecar. D49 confirms the spike is dormant.

verdicts.title: unavailable tag embeds free-text reason despite comment claiming a stable tag
verdicts.detail: Confirmed as a real code-vs-comment mismatch. Line 38 comment promises 'serialized to a stable tag Rust can branch on (never free text)', but line 44 builds `"unavailable:\(String(describing: reason))"`. `String(describing:)` on the availability reason enum is debug-formatted and not a documented-stable string; its spelling can shift across macOS point releases. If a future Rust consumer matches the full tag rather than just the `unavailable` prefix, an OS update could silently break the branch. D49/chunk plan says Rust branches on 'stable status tags (C4)', so pinning the unavailable suffix to a fixed token set is the right shape to carry into the real sidecar. Info, not warning: spike-only today, and the current intent is for Rust to branch on the prefix.
verdicts.fix: Switch on the reason cases to emit fixed tokens (e.g. `unavailable:appleIntelligenceNotEnabled` / `unavailable:modelNotReady` / `unavailable:deviceNotEligible` / `unavailable:other`) instead of `String(describing:)`, and document that Rust branches only on the part before the first `:`.
verdicts.evidence: main.swift:38 comment 'stable tag ... never free text' vs line 44 `String(describing: reason)`. D49 plans Rust branching on 'stable status tags (C4)'.

verdicts.title: emitLine silently drops the response if JSON serialization or UTF-8 decode fails
verdicts.detail: Confirmed as described. Lines 28-31: `if let data = try? JSONSerialization.data(...), let str = String(data: data, encoding: .utf8) { print(str) }` with no else — on failure it emits nothing. The finding itself concedes this cannot realistically fail for the current `[String: String]` payloads (JSONSerialization escapes rather than rejects bad UTF-8), so it is not a live bug — correctly filed as low-confidence info. The consequence if a future payload type makes it fail (emitting zero lines, breaking the strict one-in/one-out contract so Rust blocks on a read that never arrives) is the legitimate robustness point. Same root as sidecar-s3, which proposes the same else-branch fix.
verdicts.fix: Add an else branch so every input still yields one line, e.g. `else { logErr("serialize failed"); print("{\"status\":\"error\",\"detail\":\"serialize failed\"}") }`.
verdicts.evidence: main.swift:28-31 use try?/if let with no else, falling through silently on failure. Payload type is [String:String] (line 27), so failure is not currently reachable.

verdicts.title: File header re-narrates the spike rationale + decision IDs (archaeology)
verdicts.detail: Confirmed the header exists: lines 1-13 are a 13-line block restating why the spike exists and enumerating proof goals tagged C1/C3/C9/C2/C4/C6 — content also in README.md and decisions.md (D49). Real comment-bloat by the project's own standard. But downgrading to info-as-judgment-call: this is a spike whose entire deliverable is the verdict it documents, and the brief flags decision-archaeology as something to be skeptical of rewriting. The header is arguably the spike's charter and harmless. Trimming is reasonable but a style preference, not a defect — leave to author discretion.
verdicts.fix: Optionally cut to 2-3 lines: what the binary does + how to run, e.g. `// Headless Swift CLI: availability gate + one @Generable recap call + a --serve stdio loop.` `// Run: swift run usageos-ai  (or -- --serve). See README.md.` Delete the C1/C3/C9 proof enumeration.
verdicts.evidence: main.swift:1-13 is a 13-line header duplicating README/D49 content. Same duplication pattern in Package.swift:2-8 (sidecar-s7).

verdicts.title: Comment above RecapProse repeats hard-rule-6 narration
verdicts.detail: Confirmed lines 18-19 exist verbatim and restate the prose-only / hard-rule-6 intent already expressed by the @Guide string (line 22) and the instructions block (lines 57-64). Minor redundancy. Downgrading rationale matches s0: low-stakes spike comment, trimming is a style preference not a defect.
verdicts.fix: Reduce to one line, e.g. `// Prose only — no numeric field, so the model can't fabricate a count (hard rule 6).`
verdicts.evidence: main.swift:18-19 restate the prose-only rule already in @Guide (line 22) and instructions (lines 57-64).

verdicts.title: availabilityTag() computed for a startup stderr log, recomputed per request
verdicts.detail: Confirmed the mechanics: line 93 `logErr("[availability] \(availabilityTag().tag)")` queries availability once at startup purely to log, and line 50 inside runRecap recomputes it to actually gate. In --serve mode runRecap runs per line, so availability is queried once for the startup log plus once per request. The extra `SystemLanguageModel.default.availability` query is cheap and runs once at startup, not in a hot loop — so 'computed three times per run' overstates it (it's once at startup + once per request, which is the real gate). Real but trivial; downgrading from the implied smell to info. This is a deliberate stderr affordance ('Report availability to a human on stderr regardless of mode', line 92) useful for a spike a human runs by hand.
verdicts.fix: Optionally drop the line-93 standalone log, or keep it as the deliberate human-facing startup affordance. If kept, no change needed.
verdicts.evidence: main.swift:92-93 startup stderr log calls availabilityTag(); runRecap (line 50) recomputes for the gate. Line 92 comment shows the stderr log is an intentional human affordance.

verdicts.title: emitLine takes [String:String] but forces a numeric field through String(ms)
verdicts.detail: Confirmed. emitLine is typed `[String: String]` (line 27), so the latency must be stringified at the call site: line 74 `"ms": String(ms)`. The homogeneous String-map shape forces numeric coercion at call sites and pairs with the silent-drop behavior flagged in sidecar-r2 (lines 28-31 have no else). The two halves of this finding overlap r2; the actionable part is the same defensive else-branch. The [String:Any] alternative is a real option but the simpler keep-String-map-plus-else-branch is the safer call for a 4-field protocol.
verdicts.fix: Keep [String:String] but add an else branch in emitLine that logErr's on serialization/encoding failure so a dropped line is observable (see sidecar-r2). Or accept [String:Any] so callers pass `ms: ms` directly.
verdicts.evidence: main.swift:27 signature [String:String]; line 74 `String(ms)` coercion; lines 28-31 drop silently on failure.

verdicts.title: Inline C-tag annotations scattered through runRecap restate decisions
verdicts.detail: Confirmed the C-tags exist: line 26 (C4/C6), line 38 (C3), line 48 (C2), line 52 (C4/C5), line 76 (overflow/refusal → fallback). These point into decisions.md/the C-register rather than explaining code. Real comment-bloat by the project standard. Downgrading to info on the same basis as s0/s1: a dormant spike, trimming is author discretion not a defect, and the brief cautions against over-eager decision-archaeology rewrites.
verdicts.fix: Optionally strip the C2/C3/C4/C5/C6 suffixes from inline comments; keep the genuinely-useful note that a non-ok status maps to the Rust template fallback, stated once.
verdicts.evidence: main.swift inline C-tags at lines 26, 38, 48, 52, 76 reference decisions.md/C-register.

verdicts.title: Two catch arms produce the same status with near-identical detail formatting
verdicts.detail: Confirmed. Lines 75-79: `catch let error as LanguageModelSession.GenerationError { emitLine(["status": "error", "detail": String(describing: error)]) }` then generic `catch { emitLine(["status": "error", "detail": "\(error)"]) }`. Both emit status "error" with a stringified error; `String(describing:)` vs `"\(error)"` interpolation produce equivalent output. Since Rust branches only on the status tag (D49 C4), the typed first arm earns nothing today. Valid simplification, though the typed arm is harmless and could be a deliberate seam for future GenerationError-specific handling — hence info, not warning.
verdicts.fix: Collapse to a single `catch { emitLine(["status": "error", "detail": String(describing: error)]) }` and remove the GenerationError-specific arm and its comment.
verdicts.evidence: main.swift:75-79 — two catch arms, both emit status 'error' + stringified error. D49 says Rust branches on status tags, not error subtype.

verdicts.title: Obvious comment restating the temperature choice
verdicts.detail: Confirmed line 69 `// Low temperature: faithful, not creative — minimize embellishment/mangling.` sits above `GenerationOptions(temperature: 0.2)` (line 70), and the README documents the same mitigation ('Low temperature ... less creative, less embellishment'). Minor redundancy. Low-confidence by the reviewer's own rating; downgrading to a discretionary trim — keeping a one-line rationale on a non-obvious magic value (0.2) is defensible.
verdicts.fix: Optionally delete or shorten line 69 to `// low temp = faithful, not creative`.
verdicts.evidence: main.swift:69-70; README 'Mitigations found' item 2 documents temperature 0.2.

verdicts.title: Package.swift header duplicates the README's purpose blurb
verdicts.detail: Confirmed: Package.swift lines 2-8 are a 7-line prose comment ('ISOLATED standalone Swift CLI, NOT wired into Tauri yet ... prove a headless Swift command-line binary can reach Apple's FoundationModels ...') duplicating README.md's Question/Verdict and D49. Real duplication-drift risk by the project standard. Downgrading to info on the same basis as the other comment findings: dormant spike, discretionary trim, not a defect. Note the 'NOT wired into Tauri yet' line is actually accurate per D49 (dormant until chunk D) and is arguably worth keeping.
verdicts.fix: Optionally trim to 1-2 lines, e.g. `// usageos-ai — standalone Swift CLI spike for the FoundationModels recap sidecar. See README.md.`
verdicts.evidence: Package.swift:2-8 duplicates README Question/Verdict + D49 content.

verdicts.title: No stdin validation before passing the line to the model in --serve mode
verdicts.detail: Confirmed — this is the same robustness gap as sidecar-r0, viewed from runRecap's entry (line 49 `func runRecap(prompt: String)`) rather than the loop (line 98). Line 98 feeds each raw stdin line straight into runRecap → session.respond with no length/empty/JSON-shape check; an empty line wastes a model round-trip. The README documents the real protocol as 'one JSON-facts line in' and the brief confirms real facts-line validation is deferred to the wired sidecar (D49 chunk B–D), so this is correctly acceptable-for-a-spike info, with the value being a single guard inherited by the real seam.
verdicts.fix: In the --serve loop skip blank lines (and optionally cap length) before calling runRecap: `guard !line.trimmingCharacters(in: .whitespaces).isEmpty else { continue }`. Note in README that full facts-line JSON validation is deferred to the wired sidecar.
verdicts.evidence: main.swift:97-98 pass raw line into runRecap (line 49) with no validation. README line 21 + D49 defer real validation to the wired sidecar. Duplicate of sidecar-r0.

verdicts.title: Prebuilt sidecar binary is untracked AND not gitignored — would be committed by `git add .`
verdicts.detail: Confirmed. `git status --short` shows `?? src-tauri/binaries/` (untracked); `git check-ignore src-tauri/binaries/usageos-ai-aarch64-apple-darwin` exits 1 (NOT ignored). The root `.gitignore` covers `node_modules`, `dist`, `.build/` (SPM caches) but has no `src-tauri/binaries/` rule; `src-tauri/.gitignore` only ignores `/target/` and `/gen/schemas`. `file` confirms it is a 111KB Mach-O arm64 executable. `grep externalBin src-tauri/tauri.conf.json` returns nothing (exit 1) — Tauri does not consume it, consistent with the dormant chunk-D seam (D49). So an aarch64-only compiled blob is one `git add -A` from entering history of a public, audited, privacy-first repo. Note: this is not an active privacy LEAK (the binary does nothing at runtime today), so the `hr1-privacy` framing is slightly strong; the real risk is committing an opaque build artifact. The fix targets `.gitignore`, not the cited binary file, so I retarget file/line to `.gitignore:1`.
verdicts.fix:
Append to root `.gitignore`:

# Prebuilt AI sidecar (built from spikes/foundation-models; wired via externalBin in chunk D)
src-tauri/binaries/

When the sidecar is wired in chunk D, commit it deliberately (or build in CI) and add the externalBin entry then. Adding an ignore rule is safe and touches no merge gate.
verdicts.evidence: git status `?? src-tauri/binaries/`; git check-ignore exit 1; .gitignore has no binaries rule; src-tauri/.gitignore only /target + /gen/schemas; file = Mach-O arm64 111KB; grep externalBin → none; D49 confirms seam dormant until chunk D.

verdicts.title: Content-Security-Policy is disabled (`csp: null`) in a privacy-first app
verdicts.detail: Confirmed at tauri.conf.json:25 — `app.security.csp` is literally `null`, so Tauri injects no CSP. The frontend is fully local (frontendDist ../dist, local fonts @fontsource/anton + jost, custom SVG dial, no chart CDN), so today there is no outbound call — this is defense-in-depth, not an active leak (severity warning is right). A locked CSP is the runtime enforcement of hard rule 1 at the webview boundary, where an accidental future `fetch()` or a compromised JS dependency could egress with nothing stopping it. This is the higher-value of the two CSP findings; merged with config-s0. CAUTION on the fix: the exact `connect-src` origin Tauri v2 IPC needs varies by version/platform and a too-tight CSP can break the IPC bridge or fonts — must be verified by actually running the app (`tauri dev`), so this is not a blind autofix.
verdicts.fix: Set an explicit restrictive CSP, e.g. `"csp": "default-src 'self'; connect-src 'self' ipc: http://ipc.localhost; img-src 'self' data:; style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'; font-src 'self' data:; script-src 'self'"`. Verify against a running build that IPC and the Bauhaus fonts still load before merging; tune connect-src to the actual Tauri v2 IPC origin.
verdicts.evidence: tauri.conf.json:25 `"csp": null` inside app.security; frontend deps in package.json are all local (fontsource, react, no remote asset libs); hard rule 1 in CLAUDE.md.

verdicts.title: CSP is null — no Content-Security-Policy injected for a privacy-first app
verdicts.detail: Dropped as a duplicate of config-r1 — same file, same line (tauri.conf.json:25), same `"csp": null` issue and same fix. Merged into config-r1 to avoid double-reporting one line.
verdicts.fix: See config-r1.
verdicts.evidence: Identical finding to config-r1 (tauri.conf.json:25, csp null).

verdicts.title: opener:default grants allow-open-url (open arbitrary http/https) — over-broad, unused
verdicts.detail: Confirmed. capabilities/default.json:9 lists `"opener:default"`, and line 10 separately lists `"opener:allow-reveal-item-in-dir"`. I read the plugin's own permissions/default.toml from the cargo cache: `[default]` expands to `allow-open-url`, `allow-reveal-item-in-dir`, `allow-default-urls`. So `opener:default` already includes reveal-item, making line 10 redundant, AND it grants `allow-open-url` (hand arbitrary mailto/tel/http/https URLs to the OS default app). The frontend only ever calls `revealItemInDir` (SettingsView.tsx:1 import, used at lines 108 and 118); grep finds no `openUrl`/`open(` call anywhere in src/. So URL-opening is unused capability surface on a no-network product — least-privilege says drop it. Dropping `opener:default` and keeping only `opener:allow-reveal-item-in-dir` gives exactly the granted-and-used surface.
verdicts.fix: In src-tauri/capabilities/default.json delete line 9 `"opener:default"` and keep line 10 `"opener:allow-reveal-item-in-dir"`. Then run the app once to confirm reveal-in-Finder still works (the only opener call). Not a blind autofix — it's a capability-surface change.
verdicts.evidence: default.json:9 opener:default + :10 allow-reveal-item-in-dir; plugin default.toml expands to allow-open-url + allow-reveal-item-in-dir + allow-default-urls; src grep: only revealItemInDir used (SettingsView.tsx:108,118), no openUrl.

verdicts.title: Prebuilt 111KB sidecar binary is untracked and unwired (no externalBin, not gitignored)
verdicts.detail: Dropped as a duplicate of config-r0 — same untracked/un-gitignored/un-externalBin binary, same core remedy (gitignore it now, wire via bundle.externalBin in chunk D). The one extra suggestion here — 'delete the stray binary' — is a judgment call I do not endorse blindly: the binary is the spike's compiled output and the author may want it on disk while iterating on chunk D; the safe, sufficient action is the ignore rule from config-r0. Merged.
verdicts.fix: See config-r0 (add `src-tauri/binaries/` to .gitignore). Wire via `bundle.externalBin` only when chunk D lands; deleting the on-disk binary is optional, not required.
verdicts.evidence: Same evidence as config-r0: git status ??, check-ignore exit 1, no externalBin in tauri.conf.json, D49 dormant seam.

verdicts.title: tokio features 'time' and 'sync' are unused; 'rt' only used in a test
verdicts.detail: Confirmed and tightened. Cargo.toml:35 = `tokio = { version = "1", features = ["rt", "time", "sync"] }`. The ONLY `tokio::` reference in all of src-tauri/src is `tokio::runtime::Builder::new_current_thread()` at ai/mod.rs:72, inside the `#[cfg(test)]` block (a `block_on` helper). grep for `tokio::time`/`tokio::sync` returns nothing. So `time` and `sync` are genuinely dead features. Nuance on the fix: the seam IS production code — `Narrator::narrate` and `build_recap` are real `async fn`s (ai/mod.rs:30,51) per D49 chunk A — but nothing in production *drives* a runtime (no executor, no `.await` outside the trait/seam definitions; build_recap has no non-test callers). So `rt` is only needed by the test `block_on`. Downgrading severity to info: this is dead dependency-feature surface, not a correctness or build problem, and the deferral is consistent with the dormant D49 seam. Trimming `time`+`sync` is safe; moving tokio to dev-dependencies is a more aggressive change that should wait until chunk D confirms the production runtime story.
verdicts.fix: Trim to `tokio = { version = "1", features = ["rt"] }` — drops the unused `time` and `sync`. Verify `cargo test` still builds (the block_on helper needs `rt`). Defer the dev-dependencies move until chunk D wires the real executor.
verdicts.evidence: Cargo.toml:35; grep tokio:: → only ai/mod.rs:72 (inside #[cfg(test)]); no tokio::time/sync; build_recap has no non-test callers; D49 says seam dormant.

verdicts.title: 6-line decision-archaeology comment on the specta pin belongs in decisions.md
verdicts.detail: Confirmed the comment exists: Cargo.toml:21-26 narrate the full D27 rationale (rc.24 removed `#[specta(rename)]` on containers, tauri 2.9.3 still uses it, rc.20 is the highest compiling combo, move all four together). D27 in decisions.md already records this debate. This is a style/taste call, not a defect — and the comment has genuine value at the point of pain: it stops a future contributor from 'helpfully' bumping the RC and breaking the build, which a one-line pointer to a D-number does less forcefully. Keeping severity info (it already was), but I'd lean toward leaving it; flagging only as a low-value optional trim. Not autofixable (editing rationale prose is a judgment call, and the pins themselves must stay exactly `=2.0.0-rc.20` per D27).
verdicts.fix: Optional: collapse to `# specta v2 trio pinned as a set — newer RCs break with tauri 2.9.3. See decisions.md D27.` Do NOT change the `=` pins. Low priority; the verbose form arguably earns its place as a load-bearing warning.
verdicts.evidence: Cargo.toml:21-26 matches D27 (decisions.md:89). Pins are deliberate per task brief / D27.

verdicts.title: 5-line macOS-deps comment re-narrates D29/D32/D33 design decisions
verdicts.detail: Confirmed: Cargo.toml:37-41 explain the capture-trait boundary, the cfg gate, that spikes proved the pins, the AXObserver-in-objc2-application-services / no-accessibility-sys choice (D29), and libc backing proc_pidinfo. D29/D32/D33 in decisions.md cover all of this. Same class as config-s4: a taste-level comment-volume call, not a defect. The per-crate feature lists below it (NSWorkspace, AXUIElement, etc.) are the load-bearing part and stay regardless. Downgraded to info / optional; leaving it is defensible since it documents why a maintainer must not casually bump the objc2 train (which is unified with Tauri's). Not autofixable.
verdicts.fix: Optional: reduce to `# Native macOS capture, gated to macos, isolated behind the capture trait (hard rule 5). Pins proven by the spikes; see decisions.md D29/D32/D33.` Keep the feature lists. Low priority.
verdicts.evidence: Cargo.toml:37-41 matches D29/D32/D33 (decisions.md:97-120).

verdicts.title: Placeholder metadata: authors = ["you"] and a stale generic description
verdicts.detail: Confirmed. Cargo.toml:5 = `authors = ["you"]` (unedited create-tauri-app scaffold placeholder) and Cargo.toml:4 = `description = "A personal desktop utility app that tracks computer usage activity"` (generic stub). CLAUDE.md gives the real product one-liner ('a calm rear-view mirror, not a productivity coach' / 'records where your time goes') and the git author is 'Favour Agozie'. This metadata ships into the crate manifest and the bundle. Cosmetic (info), but real scaffold cruft for a project that's MIT/public from day one.
verdicts.fix: Set `authors = ["Favour Agozie"]` and a product-accurate description, e.g. `description = "UsageOS — a private, on-device record of where your time goes"`. Safe edit; rerun `cargo fmt --check` (manifest isn't fmt-gated, but harmless).
verdicts.evidence: Cargo.toml:4-5 placeholder values vs CLAUDE.md product description + git user 'Favour Agozie'.

verdicts.title: package.json name is still the scaffold default 'tauri-app'
verdicts.detail: Confirmed: package.json:2 = `"name": "tauri-app"`, the create-tauri-app default. Everything else is branded — tauri.conf.json productName 'UsageOS' (:3), identifier 'com.favour.usage-os' (:5), crate name 'usage-os' (Cargo.toml:2). The npm package name was never updated. Cosmetic (info) and `private: true` so it never publishes, but it's leftover scaffold inconsistency.
verdicts.fix: Rename to `"name": "usage-os"` (or `"usageos"`) to match the crate and product. Safe; no script or import references the package name.
verdicts.evidence: package.json:2 'tauri-app' vs tauri.conf.json productName 'UsageOS'/identifier com.favour.usage-os and Cargo.toml name 'usage-os'.

verdicts.title: tauri-build features = [] is redundant
verdicts.detail: Confirmed. Cargo.toml:13 = `tauri-build = { version = "2", features = [] }` and :16 = `tauri = { version = "2", features = [] }`. An empty `features` array is the Cargo default and adds nothing but noise (and reads as if features were once intended). Trivially cosmetic (info, the finding self-rated low). Collapsing to `tauri-build = "2"` / `tauri = "2"` is behavior-identical. Note this only applies to deps with a genuinely empty feature list — the tokio/objc2 entries have real features and must keep their table form.
verdicts.fix: Simplify Cargo.toml:13 to `tauri-build = "2"` and :16 to `tauri = "2"`. Behavior-identical; leave the feature-bearing deps untouched. Run `cargo build` once to confirm resolution is unchanged.
verdicts.evidence: Cargo.toml:13 and :16 both have `features = []` (Cargo default); tokio/objc2 entries have non-empty features and are excluded.

themes.title: Decision-archaeology comments are the dominant noise across every subsystem
themes.detail: By far the largest cluster of findings (40+ across Rust, TS, Swift, SQL, and Cargo/package manifests) is the same pattern: source comments that re-narrate decisions (D8/D9/D26/D29/D30/D31/D34/D34a/D35/D41/D43/D46/D47/D48, R18/R22/R57/R77, C5/C6/C19) and spike provenance instead of describing what the code does. Module headers run 6-10 lines re-arguing settled debates (apps.rs:1, browser.rs:1, terminal.rs:1, migrations.rs:1, project.rs:1, ruleMatch.ts:3, appIcons.ts:1, categories.ts:1, main.swift:1); migration .sql headers re-litigate the recategorization debate (0004, 0005, 0006); constants carry dogfooding history (GATE_SECS/PATIENT_GATE_SECS, rollup.rs:20). The project already has the right home for this: CLAUDE.md mandates decisions.md as the append-only ADR log and explicitly says cross-reference from code, not duplicate. The comments have drifted stale in several places (0001 says rename 'deferred D31' but D46 reversed it; README chain lists 2 of 6 migrations), which is the predictable failure mode of narrating decisions inline.
themes.recommendation: Do one sweeping pass: delete the decision-narration from code, replacing each with at most a one-line 'why' plus a bare cross-ref (e.g. `// patient gate — see D39`). Keep comments that explain non-obvious mechanism (UAF/Drop ordering in macos/mod.rs is legitimate). Reconcile the two confirmed-stale spots (migrations/README.md chain table, 0001 rename comment). This is low-risk, mechanical, and removes the single biggest source of AI-smell and staleness in the tree.

themes.title: Three React data hooks are byte-identical and all share the same stale-response race
themes.detail: useDayData, useTimelineData, and useWeekData are the same hook differing only in the fetch fn and error string (useDayData.ts:1). All three (useDayData.ts:22, useTimelineData.ts:25, useWeekData.ts:23) have no out-of-order guard: a fast day/week navigation fires overlapping async fetches and whichever resolves last wins, so rapid stepping can render the wrong range's data — a real correctness bug, not just cleanup. useWeekData additionally does a pointless join->split round-trip to build a stable dep key (useWeekData.ts:21). The capture-health getWatcherStatus effect and the ErrorState component are independently copy-pasted across all three views (DayView.tsx:40, DayView.tsx:261), and DayNav/TimelineNav are identical steppers (DayView.tsx:146).
themes.recommendation: Collapse the three hooks into one generic `useViewData<T>(fetchFn, deps, live)` that owns the stale-response guard once (track a request id or AbortController; ignore resolutions that aren't the latest). That single change fixes three correctness bugs and deletes ~2x of the hook code. Extract ErrorState and the capture-health effect into shared components/a `useCaptureHealth` hook, and unify DayNav/TimelineNav into one `<DayStepper>`.

themes.title: Day/week time-boundary math is duplicated across Rust and TS and disagrees with itself
themes.detail: Date/range semantics live in both the frontend (dates.ts dayBounds, tauri.ts getWeek docs) and the backend (lib.rs get_week 'exclusive end', rollup), and they don't agree. dayBounds uses an inclusive end of 23:59:59 (dates.ts:7) which drops the final second of the day and is inconsistent with the half-open `[day_start, next_day_start)` contract the Week path uses — Week can double-count a midnight event while Day silently loses one second. tauri.ts:80 documents getWeek as 'exclusive end' contradicting the inclusive dayBounds it's built from. This split-brain boundary logic is the kind of thing that produces off-by-one totals that are nearly impossible to spot in the dial.
themes.recommendation: Pick one canonical convention — half-open `[start, next_start)` everywhere — and implement day/week bounds in exactly one place. Have the frontend compute only the local midnights (it must, for DST) and pass them through; let Rust own all range arithmetic. Fix dayBounds to return the next-midnight exclusive end and update the getWeek doc to match. Add one test asserting a midnight-boundary event lands in exactly one bucket.

themes.title: Rust/TS categorization and host-matching logic is reimplemented and the copies diverge
themes.detail: The same matching rules exist in both languages with subtly different behavior. reprocess_logs uses SQL LIKE while find_category uses Rust substring — they categorize the same rows differently (db.rs:429). Empty patterns are match-all in Rust find_category but skipped by the frontend ruleMatch (ruleMatch.ts:15) and also match-everything via contains("") in db.rs:801. Host matching in enrich is asymmetric and loose: github.com via ends_with lets lookalike subdomains through (project.rs:159), and 127.0.0.1:port isn't treated as local while localhost:port is (project.rs:176). The frontend appIcons prefix-match resolves slack->slackdownloader (appIcons.ts:72), the same class of substring-too-loose bug. These are all the same root cause: ad-hoc substring/ends_with/LIKE matching reimplemented per call site.
themes.recommendation: Define one matching primitive in Rust (exact host with subdomain rules; explicit empty-pattern semantics; one substring helper) and have reprocess_logs and find_category call it so they can never diverge. For the TS mirror in ruleMatch/appIcons, treat Rust as the source of truth — ideally the frontend shouldn't re-derive categories at all (it already gets pre-computed aggregates per hard rule 6); audit whether ruleMatch.ts is even needed at read time. At minimum, align empty-pattern semantics and replace ends_with host checks with a real host equality/suffix-with-dot check.

themes.title: Capture span state machine has idle-inflation gaps and noisy redundant writes
themes.detail: The single-writer consume() is the heart of the app's honesty promise, but the same-window event path extends/reopens the span with no idle check (mod.rs:282) — exactly the chatty-while-away inflation the docstring claims to prevent — and even rewrites set_span_end when the timestamp didn't advance (mod.rs:282 info). The on_tick resume gate keys off last_focus app (mod.rs:316), widening the patient-gate window for phantom reopens. Title-only dedupe drops same-title URL/cwd navigation (macos/mod.rs:187). Separately, emit_with_title spawns osascript/pgrep synchronously on the main run loop (macos/mod.rs:214), so a wedged scripted app freezes the whole UI. FocusEvent.bundle_id and .pid are dead carrier fields — set by the macOS source, never read (mod.rs:35-36).
themes.recommendation: Route the same-window re-fire through the same idle gate the tick path uses, and make set_span_end a no-op when the end doesn't advance. Move the osascript/pgrep title-resolution off the main run loop (dispatch to a worker, deliver via the existing channel) so capture can never block the UI. Drop bundle_id/pid from FocusEvent until something reads them. These touch the correctness core, so pair each with a focused test.

themes.title: The dormant AI sidecar seam is over-built relative to its zero live callers
themes.detail: The ai module and Swift sidecar are not yet wired (lib.rs comments call them 'not-yet-wired'), yet they already carry trait-dispatch rationale docs (ai/mod.rs:24), AiError variant-distinction narration that build_recap then ignores (ai/mod.rs:14), and a sentinel 'fm'/generated_by string split across two files as both a const and a bare literal (ai/mod.rs:12, mirrored as a magic 'fm' in RecapCard.tsx:4). The Swift side, still a spike, has real robustness gaps for when it does go live: --serve sends unvalidated/blank stdin straight to the model (main.swift:49/97), emitLine silently drops responses on serialization failure (main.swift:28), and the availabilityTag is recomputed per request (main.swift:93).
themes.recommendation: Keep the seam minimal until it has a caller: collapse AiError to the variants build_recap actually distinguishes, define the generated_by sentinel exactly once and export it to TS via specta (so RecapCard stops using a magic literal), and delete the dispatch-rationale prose. Defer the Swift hardening to when the sidecar is wired, but capture the three correctness gaps (blank-stdin guard, emitLine failure surfacing) as the acceptance criteria for that chunk rather than leaving them latent.

themes.title: Recurring micro-duplication: hand-rolled folds, repeated query-one-row, copied struct shapes
themes.detail: A pattern of not-quite-extracted helpers recurs in the Rust core. get_day/get_week/get_timeline each rebuild the identical categories+projects HashMap block (lib.rs:94). get_setting/find_project_by_alias/get_project repeat the same query-one-row dance (db.rs:546). RawRunBuilder and SegRun are effectively the same struct with a pure-copy conversion (rollup.rs:280), and build_runs hand-copies seven fields that are a strict subset of TimelineRun (rollup.rs:529). best_icns hand-rolls a max-by-key fold instead of Iterator::max_by_key (apps.rs:158). The watcher-status health threshold is duplicated as a bare `errors < 6` instead of reusing capture::ERROR_THRESHOLD = 6 (lib.rs:244/248). parse_site/parse_url each re-strip ports (enrich/mod.rs:13).
themes.recommendation: Extract a `load_lookup_maps(&conn) -> (categories, projects)` helper for the three command handlers; add a generic `query_one_opt` repository helper for the single-row reads; merge RawRunBuilder into SegRun (or make build_runs construct TimelineRun via `..` struct-update / a From impl) so the seven-field copy disappears; replace the manual fold with max_by_key; reference ERROR_THRESHOLD instead of the literal 6. Each is small and individually low-risk.

themes.title: Custom UI primitives ship without baseline accessibility and bypass the design tokens
themes.detail: Hard rule 7 says build against the design system, yet several components hardcode colors instead of tokens: TitleBar rgba() bar-muted (TitleBar.tsx:5), TimelineRow inline `var(--fg)` instead of text-fg (TimelineRow.tsx:47), CategoryEditorModal inline color-mix (CategoryEditorModal.tsx:270), RadioGroup dot hardcodes c-deep ignoring the selectable accent (RadioGroup.tsx:35), ThemeSwitcher hardcodes per-theme hex. Separately the hand-rolled primitives skip table-stakes a11y: Modal doesn't trap or restore focus (Modal.tsx:21), the custom Select listbox has no keyboard navigation (Select.tsx:61). There's also dead/single-use abstraction (DetailInspector indirection for one caller, Tag/ModePill possibly unused) sitting alongside the gaps.
themes.recommendation: Sweep the hardcoded colors to tokens in one pass (they're enumerated). For Modal/Select, either adopt a headless primitive (Radix/React Aria) or add the minimum: focus trap + restore on Modal, arrow/Home/End/type-ahead + roving tabindex on Select. Delete the confirmed single-use indirections (DetailInspector, unused primitives) rather than keeping them 'just in case'.

themes.title: Defensive guards and ad-hoc logging that don't match the codebase's own error contract
themes.detail: Scattered defensive code guards impossible states or diverges from the project's typed-error discipline. open_span swallows category-lookup errors with a print while its neighbors propagate Result (mod.rs:245). Startup retention cleanup uses println!/eprintln! with [Startup] prefixes — ad-hoc logging in a tree that otherwise has a typed AppError contract (lib.rs:421) — nested 5 levels deep (lib.rs:413). duration() clamps negative spans with .max(0) with nothing documenting why end<start can occur (rollup.rs:156); format.ts mirrors this with Math.max(0, Math.floor()) guarding an impossible negative (format.ts:6); CategoryEditorModal throws 'Missing category id' for an impossible case (CategoryEditorModal.tsx:188).
themes.recommendation: Make open_span propagate its error like its siblings. Either flatten the startup cleanup into a small `fn run_startup_cleanup() -> Result<...>` returning AppError and log once at the call site, or adopt a real logger (tracing) consistently — the [Startup] println pattern should not coexist with AppError. For the negative-duration clamps: either prove end>=start at the write boundary (capture) and remove the clamps, or keep one clamp with a one-line invariant comment and delete the redundant second one in TS. Drop the impossible-case throw.

top_simplifications: Run one decision-archaeology sweep: strip D-id/R-id/C-id narration and spike provenance from all code/SQL/manifest comments, leaving a one-line why + bare cross-ref; fix the 2 confirmed-stale comments (migrations README chain, 0001 rename).
Collapse useDayData/useTimelineData/useWeekData into one generic useViewData<T> with a stale-response guard — kills 3x duplication and fixes 3 wrong-range correctness bugs at once.

Define day/week bounds in exactly one place with a single half-open convention; frontend passes only midnights, Rust owns the arithmetic — removes the Day-vs-Week midnight double-count / dropped-second split.
Extract a load_lookup_maps(conn) helper used by get_day/get_week/get_timeline, replacing the three copied categories+projects HashMap blocks.
Move emit_with_title's osascript/pgrep off the main run loop so a wedged scripted app can't freeze the UI.
Route same-window re-fires through the idle gate (and no-op set_span_end when the end didn't advance) to stop chatty-while-away inflation.
Merge RawRunBuilder into SegRun and build TimelineRun via struct-update/From, deleting the pure-copy conversion and the 7-field hand-copy in build_runs.
Extract shared ErrorState, useCaptureHealth, and a single DayStepper; reference capture::ERROR_THRESHOLD instead of the literal 6 in get_watcher_status.
Define one host/pattern matching primitive in Rust so reprocess_logs (LIKE) and find_category (substring) can't diverge; fix github.com ends_with and 127.0.0.1:port asymmetry.
Sweep hardcoded colors (TitleBar, TimelineRow, CategoryEditorModal, RadioGroup, ThemeSwitcher) to design tokens per hard rule 7.
Delete dead carrier fields FocusEvent.bundle_id/pid and confirmed single-use indirection (DetailInspector, unused settings primitives).
best_practice_gaps: React data fetching with no cancellation/stale-guard: hooks set state from the last-resolving promise (useDayData/Timeline/Week), the canonical out-of-order bug; idiomatic fix is an AbortController or request-id latch, or TanStack Query.

Custom interactive components (Modal, Select) ship without WAI-ARIA keyboard support or focus management — modern React+TS practice is a headless lib (Radix/React Aria) or at least roving-tabindex + focus trap.

Magic string literals crossing the Rust/TS boundary by hand (generated_by 'fm', WatcherStatus threshold 6) instead of being generated/exported via tauri-specta — undercuts the project's own 'IPC contract is generated' hard rule.

Rust error handling inconsistency: open_span uses print-and-swallow while siblings return Result; startup path uses println!/eprintln! ad-hoc logging instead of the typed AppError contract or a tracing logger.
Hand-rolled iterator folds where std combinators read better (best_icns vs Iterator::max_by_key; rollup match-on-Option-to-break vs let-else/while-let).
Owned Strings where borrows suffice (parse_url returning owned, no-remote branch cloning folder 3x) — unnecessary allocation in hot capture/enrich paths.

Loose string matching used as identity checks (ends_with for hosts, contains("") match-all, prefix icon match) instead of explicit equality/suffix-with-dot/empty-pattern handling — a recurring Rust+TS smell.
Subprocess spawned synchronously on the main thread (emit_with_title osascript/pgrep) and git shelled out per terminal focus with no caching — blocking I/O on latency-sensitive paths.
&'static str sentinels with duplicated literals for a closed set of abstain reasons (enrich project.rs) where a Rust enum is the idiomatic, exhaustively-matchable choice.
Sequential awaits in a for-loop for independent writes (CategoryEditorModal rule writes) instead of Promise.all — needless serialization.

tauri-specta types not fully leveraged: generatedBy typed as bare string and a backend index used to dereference days[] without bounds checks (WeekView) — losing the type-safety the generated contract is supposed to provide.

Placeholder/scaffold metadata left in manifests (Cargo authors=['you'], package.json name='tauri-app') and unused tokio features ('time','sync') — config hygiene gaps for a public-from-day-one MIT repo.
